Original_Search_Title: Contractors Of Niger Delta Varsity Urged To Speed Up Work
Headline: Nigeria: Contractors Of Niger Delta Varsity Urged To Speed Up Work
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — Contractors handling project work at the Niger Delta
University site have been directed to put in extra effort to
translate the state government vision for the institution into
reality.
The Commissioner for Education Dr. Mark Atei Okorobia gave the
directive while on an unscheduled visit to the university site at
Wilberforce Island to assess the progressive made so far.
Dr Okorobia said the directive became necessary as the university
must commence full academic work by the year 2001.
The education commissioner said government was committed to providing
an ultra modern university for the state and urged the contractors to
co-operate with the construction manager to achieve the desired
result.
He advised the contractor not to hesitate to report any obstacle in
the execution of their job to his office for necessary action.
The commissioner was accompanied by the Vice Chancellor of the
university, Prof. John Buseri and the registrar, Mr. Perelade
Apotala. Conducting the commissioner and his team round the site, the
Niger Delta University project engineer, Amos Waritimi assured the
commissioner that the project would be completed on scheduled.
Meanwhile, residents of Ovom in Yenagoa have been warned against the
vandalisation of the fence of Bishop Dimeari Grammar School.
The state Commissioner for Education who gave this warning expressed
disappointment that inspite of the agreement reached with the
representative of the community some resident from the area have
resorted to vandalising the newly constructed fence.
Dr. Okorobia warned that he would hold the representative of the
community responsible for any further damage due to the school fence.
He disclosed that the state runs the risk of losing its allocation
from the Education Tax Fund (ETF) if the fence is not completed.
The commissioner also directed the contractor handling the
construction of Gwegwe road to come to his office for consultation in
respect of the school fence pulled down in the process of
constructing the road.
He also advised the Winners Chapel and yam sellers occupying part of
the school premises to vacate the place as directed by the state
governor recently.
He recalled that they were given enough time to find alternative site
for their various activities nothing that illegal occupation of the
premises has contributed to the insecurity of lives and property in
the school.

Original_Search_Title: Guide For Xmas Shopping
Headline: Nigeria: Guide For Xmas Shopping
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mile 12 market at the Ketu end of Ikorodu Road Lagos needs no
introduction. Neither does it need any description. Virtually every
one living in Lagos State and regular visitors to the Lagos
Metropolis know this market primarily because food stuffs are
generally cheaper there than in any other market in the state.
Traders from various parts of the country come to off-load trailer
loads of fresh farm produce in the market which is located almost at
the outskirts of the Lagos metropolis. A survey of leading markets in
the state revealed that yam, vegetable, tomato, pepper, onions and
vegetable salad ingredients are cheaper here and the vegetables come
fresh. A basket with 40 pieces of average sized onions sells for N150
as against N350 in other markets at this time of the scarcity of
onions. Early in the year, when onions were in season, the same
quantity sold for N40. A tuber of "Ada Onitsha' yam which can feed a
family of seven with healthy appetite sells for N60 whereas the same
size of yam sells for N100 in other markets. A small basket of tomato
and pepper sells for N40. Alhaji Sule Abubakar, a market union
member, said that because formers usually converge on the market to
dispose of their wares it was only natural for prices to be cheaper.
The only hitch at this market is the mammoth crowd and traffic hold
up. But for car owners, it boasts of two modern garages. The best
time to shop here is early in the morning when the farmers are just
getting to the market; that way, you buy straight from them at a good
price. For fruits of any type, Ketu market otherwise called Jakande
Market is the place to go. The market is situated in the heart of
Ketu. Here fruits are sold in heaps. They are cheaper and fresher
than the fruits one can get from any other part of Lagos. Just like
in Mile 12, farmers from various parts of the country come here to
sell their commodities to distributors from where distributors and
other middlemen make bulk purchases. Now that citrus fruit is in
season, a basket of 50 big oranges sells for N50 and can even be
gotten cheaper. In other markets and bus stops the same quantity of
oranges will go for nothing less than N250. The only snag here is
that apart from paying for the oranges, touts charge N20 'ground
money' for any product bought. This ground money, the Iya Oloja
(market leader), Madam Adebayo, said is paid to the community leader
as rent for making use of the land. Idi Oro market in Mushin local
government area of Lagos offers plantain, gari, palm oil and snail at
very reasonable prices. Most of the products are brought into the
market directly by farmers from the Eastern part of the country.
Fruits like pineapple and oranges are also sold in this market but
not as cheaply as in Ketu market. A bag of 100 kg yellow gari sells
for N2,000 while white gari of the same quantity is sold for N1,700.
At the Oshodi market, for instance, the same yellow gari sell for
N2,300 while the white one costs N2,000. A 100 kg bag of drum is
N5,200 while white beans or black eyed beans is N4,000. Palm oil
which is not in season now is also cheaper here. 25 litres sell for
N1,800 while else where it is between and N2,500. A bunch of ripe
plantains with about 20 fingers is N70 but unripe ones of the same
size is between N50 and N60. For rice, vegetable oil and sugar,
Daleko market on Oshodi Mile 2 Express way is where to go. Located
within Isolo local government area, the market is ever busy. 50kg bag
of imported rice sells for between N2,500 and N2,700 in most Lagos
markets, but at the Daleko market, it goes for between N2,100 and
N2,300. Consumers wishing to buy dry fish, stock fish, crayfish,
ogbono, egusi and other soup condiments are advised to go to Oto/Iddo
market where these are cheaper. The market is located immediately
before Carter bridge in Lagos Mainland. At the market, a carton of
mangala-dry fish costs N3,300 while a cup of ogbono is between N45
and N50. Anyone wishing to buy hot drinks, beverages, wine and
kitchen utensils should head for Oke Arin market in Lagos Island
while frozen foods are cheapest at Ijora market in Apapa LGA. For
fashion accessories, consumers are advised to go to Balogun market
Gaba square for georges, various wrappers, laces and shoes. For men's
wear, behind the Mandilas building are located shops said to be the
best for affordable wears. High quality guinea brocade are sold at
reasonable prices at Moshalasi area around the Lagos Central Mosque
in Lagos Island. Gbajumo Street in the Balogun Market occupies the
prime place for ladies shoes while Breadfruit street in the same
area, offers children's wears at good prices.

Original_Search_Title: Music Is The Food Of Matters Of The Heart
Headline: Zimbabwe: Music Is The Food Of Matters Of The Heart
Body_Text:
Harare — "It started with a kiss in the back row of a classroom"- so
go the lyrics of one of Hot Chocolate's most popular tunes, a song
that several years ago marked the beginning of some steamy and
youthful relationships.
In Zimbabwe and South Africa in the 1970s, the Hurricanes had
everyone crooning: "Whisper to me, tell me how much do you love me?
Tell me, tell me that you love me true, let me know about your inner
feelings. I will believe whatever you say even if you lie baby."
But if you are today's kid on the block, then you would probably say:
"I don't care who you are, where you're from, where you've been as
long as you love me," and that would be courtesy of the Backstreet
Boys.
Ringo Madlingozi, in Into Yam, says: "For a long time I have wanted
to speak to you but I don't even know your name. I'm not one of those
guys who mess around. I just want you alone."
Madlingozi will go on to claim that his love is so strong even his
brains can no longer function. And he adds that what is his is yours
and yours is his.
These words - brothers and sisters - have become part of everyday
repertoire of men and women in love or those trying to win over
someone.
Music plays a very important role in articulating our feelings about
various issues, especially the affairs of the heart.
When things work out, you find yourself imagining all sorts of
passionate things about your new lover. This is when Don Williams
comes in and tells you that last night he dreamt he held you in his
arms. You made love and it lasted so long.
Dolly Paton and Porter Wagoner will make their contribution in
Together You and I.
"My love is deeper than the depth of an ocean, as faithful as the
stars that grace the night and as constant as the sun's journey
through the heaven, that's why we will always be together you and I,
" goes the song.
So far folks our love life is sparkling but then at some point things
will slow down or you get attracted to another person. But this is
not to say you want to give up what you have. Remember there is such
a thing as having your cake and eating it too.
RoyC will tell you that he's got to get enough of your sweet love,
even though he knows that you are married. He sings that he can't see
anything wrong with making love to another man's wife as long as you
do it right. Wow! It is indeed getting steamy and UB40 will admit
that "I'm guilty of loving you, even though I know you are engaged to
someone else. Love is a thing that's a lot like quicksand, the more
you wriggle the more you sink".
And when you do succumb to the temptation of an extra-marital affair,
then you have to be very careful because you do not want your man to
spot you walking with his best friend laughing and talking.
RoyC comes back with She Kept on Walking, a song about a man who
finds out that his best friend has taken over his woman. With tears
in his eyes, he wonders why she should leave him. Is it because the
friend makes love better than he does? But if you do find out that
your friend's woman is cheating on him, keep it to yourself because,
as Percy Sledge says, when a man loves a woman he can't see anything
wrong with her.
When a man loves a woman, he would even sleep in the rain if the
woman said it was the right thing to do.
"You say you're gonna leave me? I'm begging you to stay but baby I
can't get through to you. When I wake up in the morning, I find you
gone. Baby please don't make me cry because I know there will be
nothing left for me," this is UB40, finally admitting that the end
has come.
Now my brother or sister, you have to deal with the pain of losing
someone but you still want them back and Dolly Paton gives you the
words in Come Back To Me.
"Come back to me and kiss me as only you can," she says. "Touch me
like nobody else can. Come back to me and hold this torch that's
burning me. Come back to me and never leave me, come back and mend
this broken heart in me."
But for those who cannot bow out gracefully, Dolly Paton has the
perfect song for you in I hope You're Never Happy.
It goes: "I wish you all the luck, I wish you only the best:
satisfaction, love and true happiness. I wish you blue skies and
rainbows. I wish you joy but only if you are with me. I hope you're
never happy with anybody but me. I wish you nothing but trouble if
you decide to leave."
This song holds true for many people, which is why you hear stories
of people who torch themselves or their spouses if they are ditched.
When we fall in love, at times friends or relatives tell us that we
have made a bad choice, to which Randy Crawford says: "They told me I
was such a fool to love you. They said you were the kind that will
never be true. I gave you so much love and you pretended you loved
me. I will never leave you - that's how heartaches are made."
Relationships are very complex so half the time when you have a good
thing it is hard to realise it. Don Williams reflects on this in his
song In The Shelter of Your Eyes.
"It took me so long to realise I just can't make it on my own. Words
are only what they say but this feeling is not wrong. I'm so glad I
found my way, it's good to be where I belong and I'm gonna stay right
here because I'm in rhythm with your mind."
While trying for a second time might work for others, megastar
Madonna believes there is power in saying goodbye, and Luther
Vandross simply says: "Give me a reason to love you back. Why should
I love you again? Tell me how to forgive and forget."
And when you are really over him or her, then you can go the Dolly
Paton way and whenever his or her picture falls out of your wallet,
you tell people he's just someone you used to know.
Paton's Just Someone I Used To Know is a very powerful song and I
know so many people who will tell you that this is just a flame that
lost its glow. They will never tell you about the nights they cry
without the ex.
While we can vow that we will never love again, you should be grown
up enough to admit as Sybil does that "we loved each other we just
couldn't get along, take a good look at me the love that I lost. I'm
so sad and lonely, I'm sorry to see you go your way and I will go
mine."
Like Al Green, you will one day get tired of being alone and you
start all over again. If you have never been lucky in love, Gerald
Levert might help you find your Mr. Too Damn Good.
Levert wants to be your sunny day, your favourite tune, your
breakfast and oh, baby, he wants to be your food, basically your Mr.
Too Damn Good.
So there you are folks! Go out and buy your loved one or yourself
music this Christmas.
I have had to rephrase some of the music lyrics that I have used due
to space restrictions. In the future, I will take you on another
musical journey but this time with our local musicians.
More love!

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Governing Style
Headline: Nigeria: How He Governs
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ebitimi Banigo, former minister of science and technology had
a rough experience with President Olusegun Obasanjo. After getting
over the shock of not being given a portfolio that matched his
qualification as a banker, he settled down to make the best of the
science and technology ministry allotted to him.
He was fully conscious of his limitations in this area, and so
prepared to make it work. His strategy was to scout for people with
ideas that could help him do his job better.
Obsessed with total technological transformation for the country, the
minister, on a trip to the United States of America, USA, in March
this year met with Thomas Emeagwali, the Nigerian-born computer whiz
kid who is making waves in the world of information technology,
discussing with him, over several nights how Nigeria could benefit
from his wealth of experience and connections in that field.
By the time he had left Maryland, the base of Emeagwali, he had
persuaded Emeagwali to visit Nigeria. On Banigo's return, he spoke
with the president about this agreement with Emeagwali and together,
they agreed that Banigo should present a memo and a proposal for
approval as soon as possible. Banigo prepared the two documents and
promptly dropped them at Aso Rock Presidential Villa and then awaited
the president's directives.
When it didn't come early enough, he went back a week later to check
with the president if his papers have been approved. To his
consternation, he found out that the president had changed his mind.
Obasanjo told him that he had discussed the contents of Banigo's
papers with another minister who suggested that the matter be kept in
view for the moment.
Banigo could hardly believe his ears. He tried to convince the
president that there was no time to waste as Nigeria's future in
information technology depended on the papers. But rather than be
convinced, the president asked whether or not he had a personal
interest in the matter. A flustered Banigo was shocked at the
question. Speechless, he left the presidential villa, for his office
where he dated his copy of the resignation letter each minister was
made to sign on assumption of duty and sent it back to the president,
becoming the first minister to resign voluntarily.
But Phil Agbasi was not given that option. He was appointed liaison
officer to the senate after former senate president Chuba Okadigbo
complained that he and his colleagues were finding it difficult to
work with Kashim Ibrahim Imam his predecessor. Given that background,
Agbasi tried his best to secure the confidence of the president. He
often complained to his colleagues that he felt the president did not
like him. At different times, he wanted to quit the post but was
assured that once he won the confidence of the president, he would
enjoy working with him.
He held on. His moment of proof came when the National Assembly was
going to throw away the president's inputs into the Niger-Delta
Development Commission, NDDC bill. Having lost in the Lower House,
the adviser proposed a three-point plan that would force the house to
be divided so that the president can now lobby and have his way.
Sensing the urgency of getting the president's assent to such a
proposal, he drove to the presidential villa with a short memo. He
was lucky to see the president in the office. He quickly discussed
his mission and dropped the proposal on his table to wait in the
adjoining conference room for his final approval.
After what he thought was a reasonable waiting time, he went back to
the president's office. Obasanjo lifted his eyes from the paper he
was poring through, and seeing Agbasi he shouted at him saying -
"what do you want?" The officer told him he had come to get feedback
on the proposal so that he can put to work the approval before the
senate met that afternoon.
Obasanjo was so furious he flung his file at him. He was overheard
shouting at Agbasi: "You just brought this file and you want me to
stop all that I am doing and attend to you. Oya, see your file, take
your file. Take your file and go." Efforts by Agbasi to convince the
president that he meant well fell on deaf ears. He left the villa
hurt and frustrated not knowing whether to return to his post as
adviser to Dubem Onyia, the minister of state for foreign affairs or
to go back to the senate as liaison officer. He did not see the
president again until Okadigbo was removed and Imam was re-appointed
to his former post.
As some Aso Rock insiders believe, the two examples are pointers that
the president cannot be rushed to do anything. He has to have
personal conviction on anything that bears his signature. Newswatch
gathered that those who wish to work with the president must have a
dose of patience and lady luck on their side. Depending on his mood,
President Obasanjo could be a difficult or an amiable boss to his
aides. Those who deal with him daily say they have to weigh his moods
from the time he walks to his office on the first floor of the Aso
Rock Presidential Villa up to the time he leaves. If by seeing him,
he is perceived to be in good mood, then you can push a proposal to
him without much ado and perhaps, if his mood does not change before
he finished reading it, as it often does, you could get an approval.
But if he is in a bad mood, it is futile, even dangerous to confront
him. At council meetings, President Obasanjo is known to hurl angry
words at his ministers. Sani Zangon Daura, former minister of
agriculture, had been known to stand up to the president on a number
of occasions telling him he did not beg him for a job. Daura is noted
to be one of the few ministers who could talk to the president
eyeball to eyeball.
Iyorchia Ayu, former minister of industry is another. On one
occasion, Ayu changed the mood at a federal executive council, FEC
meeting when he spent over thirty minutes arguing with the president
on the unconstitutionality of selling the Benue Cement Company, BCC
to Dangote Industries Limited. Ayu had argued that DIL had no
capacity to salvage the company and that the privatisation council
had not recommended the company. But Obasanjo, who apparently had
been convinced by Vice-President Atiku Abubakar, the chairman of that
council, had made up his mind. For thirty minutes, Ayu was on his
feet exchanging words with the president. The president had his way,
and came out to defend his decision.
Indeed, a few weeks after, Ayu took ill and was on admission at the
National Hospital Abuja when his resignation was announced over the
radio. For Ayu, it did not come as total shock except for the timing.
Stella, the president's wife had just visited Ayu's bedside trying to
cheer him up. It appeared that Stella had no inkling what her husband
was about to do when she went to the hospital.
According to most of his ministers, there is a thin line between the
president's penchant for anger and his capacity to crack jokes. The
mood of the president may be determined by what happened during
family devotion. This starts at 6.30 a.m. everyday usually with Jerry
Gana, his minister for information and national orientation in
attendance. Others who attend this ritual include his wife, members
of his family when they are around, sometimes a minister from his
local Baptist Church and his personal aides.
After morning prayers, the president takes a light breakfast before
heading for the office. For the president, official duties begin at
7.30 a.m. either in front of his desk at the office or with breakfast
meetings in his official residence. Once in his office, he treats
files on his table and attends to visitors who are on scheduled
appointments. Others who are not on schedule have to be cleared by
Abdullahi Mohammed, the chief of staff to the president. But because
the president hates keeping out people who request for audience with
him, they often also abuse his generosity by clustering round his two
waiting rooms.
Recently, Mohammed was forced to do something about that. On August
31, he issued a four-paragraph memo to Ufot Ekaette, the secretary to
the government of the federation, SGF to warn government officials to
desist from violating "holding procedures" on seeing the president.
He noted that rather than wait in the president's waiting room
downstairs, visitors often loiter around his two waiting rooms
chattering noisily. He wrote: "This is unhealthy and a disrespect for
the exalted office of the president and therefore unacceptable." He
announced that the conference room would remain locked and that
visitors would be compelled to use the VIP waiting room on the ground
floor where they are to remain seated until called up on the orders
of the president. On September 19, S. A. Ogunniyi, special assistant
to the COS wrote to all concerned to abide by the new regulation.
Apart from receiving local visitors, the president's day also
involves attending to official engagements outside the villa. These
may involve the opening or closing of seminars and conferences
usually at the conference centre, the women centre or any of the
hotels in Abuja. The president sometimes receives his visitors at the
presidential wing of the Nnamdi Azikiwe International Airport.
Depending on the duration of such visits, usually by foreign heads of
states and United Nations top shots, the president may choose to
drive in with his visitors or stay on and hold bilateral talks using
the airport's facilities.
After the day's work is over, which is mostly far into the evening,
the president retires to a sumptuous dinner. His best food being
pounded yam and egusi soup with assorted meat. He eats his meal like
a soldier, not having time for niceties and more often in company of
his closest aides and invitees. Depending on the way he feels, the
president may retire to bed as early as 8.00 o'clock and wake up at
midnight to play squash, the game he has learnt to play as a soldier.
President Obasanjo's most regular squash partners are said to be his
aide-de- camp who is a keen squash player or one Gbenga who heads the
gym at the villa. But occasionally, the president plays with Solomon
Ewuga, the minister of state for the federal capital territory and
Aminu Wali, his special adviser on national assembly matters and a
man with whom it is believed he consults regularly on matters
affecting the northern part of the country.
In most of his actions, the president still allows the soldier in him
to override his actions. Close aides say that it would take time for
the president to get over this, having spent over 30 years in a
command structure. He is used to being obeyed without questions. But
aides claim he is willing to learn and that weighed against his
training and background, his other credentials as a democrat are
highly commendable. Doyin Okupe, his special assistant on media and
publicity vows that the president consults with a wide range of
people both within and outside his cabinet before arriving at
decisions.
During such consultations, the president hardly talks preferring to
be a good listener except to those who may use the opportunity to
make what Okupe termed "outrageous suggestions." Apart from seeking
expert advice on technical things, the president has members of a
kitchen cabinet. They include his chief of staff, the national
security adviser Mohammed Gusau, whom he interacts with daily. On
this list is Gana, whose calling as an Anglican lay preacher endears
him to the president. He also consults with Vice-President Atiku
Abubakar on a number of issues.
Close observers have identified Tony Anenih, the minister of works
and housing as the man who makes the best impression on the
president. Anenih executes most of the president's plans without
asking questions and for this reason he has been nicknamed 'Mr.
Fix-it' by people outside government circles. He is one of the few
ministers who could see the president without a formal appointment
and at any time of the day or night. The other person being Jubril
Martins-Kuye, the minister of state for finance and J. O. Sanusi, the
governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria, CBN. The irony is that
Anenih is also said to do the dirty deeds of the administration. Both
he and the vice-president are said to be linked to the crisis between
the legislature and the executive.
Although the president consults, it is difficult to know if he puts
to use any advice given to him. Close Aso Rock watchers identified
his mind as his best judge when taking decisions and that once his
mind is made up, it is pointless to argue with him. Ayu alluded to
this in his recent interview with Newswatch. Said he: "Because of his
very strong personality, people have come to say that he doesn't want
anybody to argue with him." Even when he is wrong, the soldier in him
does not make him apologise or retrace his steps. He carries on
without as much as a comment.
To help him make important decisions, the president has around him, a
host of experts on various issues. These range from experienced
politicians to experts in diplomacy, politics and other fields. They
help him mould his policy statements and analyse his actions.
These officers include 13 senior special assistants, 15 advisers and
about 18 special assistants. But until recently when the Bullet wing
of the federal secretariat was taken over by this array of advisers,
most of them simply hung around the villa with no offices of their
own. It has also been argued that the president might not be using
the expertise of these people when they are needed most.
In his interview with Newswatch, President Obasanjo said his advisers
are there to be consulted when he needs their advice. When the
advisers requested for time to meet him, it took more than four
months for him to grant their request. Even at that, on their first
meeting, the president was conspicuously absent. Surprisingly, the
president had said on a television network: "I am not sure some of
them give me any advice." This gives the impression that most of the
posts are mere "job for the boys."
Most of the criticisms against his government have come out of that
belief. Such critics say that the president's inability to gauge the
mood of Nigerians through his advisers account for certain decisions
regarded as unpopular.
For example, when the president consented to an increase in the pump
price of petroleum, he did not seek the view of his vice-president or
of Rilwanu Lukman, his special adviser on Petroleum. It would also
appear he neglected the views of the other experts he has appointed
in that field. Newswatch gathered that the president took the
decision simply based on details and arguments made available to him
by Jackson Gaius-Obaseki, head of the Nigerian National Petroleum
Corporation, NNPC.
The same lack of consultation presaged his pronouncement of a new
minimum wage for workers in the federal and state civil service. The
president attended a workers rally in Lagos, where, to please labour
leaders, he announced the new minimum wage. Prior to that, he had not
presented a bill to the national assembly; neither had he consulted
with its leadership. This brought friction and exchange of harsh
words between him and the leadership of the assembly.
There was also no mention of the issue to members of the National
Council of State, consisting of all state governors to know if they
could afford to pay. The result was felt almost immediately at the
federal and state levels. In the federal civil service, there was a
three month delay in the payment of salaries, while workers in the
state level went on long and tortuous strikes, which practically
paralysed the activities in some states. Many states have not
recovered from the effects and have had to lay off workers or are
contemplating sacking some. President Obasanjo did not offer any
apologies; instead, he got the revenue mobilisation and fiscal
commission to make a statement that the delay in payment of salaries
was a result of a nation-wide staff audit. Here again, the president
exhibited a carry-over of the dictatorial tendency associated with
the military.
Sometimes, it is hard not to believe that the president goes out of
his way to look for the trouble of the national assembly. Apart from
insulting them on their turf the president's actions have shown that
he has no regard for legislative niceties. For example, he has
unilaterally declared a public holiday on May 29 this year to mark
one year in office without legislative debate or approval and claims
that he was right. The former attorney-general Kanu Agabi tried to
explain this away by quoting an arcane law inconsistent with the
constitution.
It did not end there. Recently, the president granted approval for
the construction of a stadium in Abuja outside the budget. He laid
the foundation of a Millennium Park to mark the 40th anniversary of
the country's independence, another multi-million project without
discussion or approval by the legislature.
For all this, the president has been called a dictator and advised to
change tactics. But the president told Newswatch recently that he
does not listen to criticisms of his administration. He said he would
be left with no chance to work if he had to listen to what every
critic had to say about his administration. Some say this is
arrogance.
But, his aides say he does things with the best intentions and that
his patriotism is not in doubt. His aides say he lives, breathes and
sleeps Nigeria and that he is so obsessed with transforming the
country that he sometimes forgets that things could not be done with
the same military fiat he is used to.
In no other area has there been as much friction as between the
executive and the legislature. When the president sneezes, the
national assembly catches the flu. On their part, his advisers on
legislative matters have tried their best to reverse the trend of the
cat and mouse game with the legislature.
Proposals have often been sent to the president on how best to handle
this but in most cases, they gather dust before approvals are
reluctantly given. One of such proposals was designed to help the
president familiarise himself with lawmakers at the individual level.
He was provided with the telephone numbers of lawmakers so that at
intervals he could call them and exchange pleasantries. Said a
source: "No matter how hard you are against him, if the president of
the federal republic of Nigeria calls you to say hi, you are likely
to feel elated. Whatever grouse you have against him would vanish."
But Obasanjo does not believe in such things.
Seeing the futility of trying to make him do that, a dinner was
proposed for the president to fete at least 100 lawmakers from both
houses at regular intervals. Most lawmakers jumped at the idea when
it was mooted and looked forward to a chance to dine with the
president. President Obasanjo hosted the first two dinners before
word went round that he made his guests very nervous at table. He
usually comes into the hall as he would in a formal engagement.
Lawmakers who attended complained that he would not exchange any
pleasantries and that even when he pumps the hands of his guests, he
does not look at their faces. With such preliminaries, the guests
barely touch their meals while the president enjoys his and when they
leave, they go with the impression of unwanted guests. The dinner was
aborted when, according to sources, Obasanjo thought it was too
expensive and a waste of resources.
As a result of his reputation as the first African soldier to hand
over power to an elected government, President Obasanjo's contacts
with foreign governments predate his current mandate. His aides say
this is what is responsible for his frequent trips abroad and his
belief that Nigeria must be re-introduced to the world.
The visits abroad have been criticised by many among whom is Lagos
lawyer and human rights campaigner, Gani Fawehinmi. At a recent press
conference, Fawehinmi estimates that on the average, the president
spends three days out of a week abroad. The president has replied
that the trips are necessary to sell a nation coming in from the cold
of isolation." I will not stop," he says defiantly.
Nigerians agree that the country needs to come clean from the cold,
but they equally argue that there is enough to engage the president's
attention at home. The president told Newswatch: "This is a country
that needs to come into the mainstream of the international community
and for that reason, you need to go round and say, well, look, we
have a new Nigeria and I'm the epitome of the new Nigeria." But his
critics argue that he robs his ministers of the ability to do their
constitutional duties. Sule Lamido, the minister for foreign affairs
recently lashed back at the president's critics when he said that he
did not have the clout to do for Nigeria, what the president could do
for it.
Yet some say that the president's frequent international trips have
robbed them of the ability to get his approval as and when needed on
urgent state matters. Said an official: "It is easier to get an
approval with him in London than in Abuja. Those who must see this
president must learn to fly." Of recent though, it seems the
president's tough talk is just a facade. In the past couple of
months, he has stayed more at home than abroad. He has also paid
official visits to some states to commission projects.
The president is believed to be very strict when it comes to anything
involving money. An apostle of anti-corruption, the president would
look with finicky fastidiousness at any monetary proposal to ensure
that it would not benefit the writer before appending his signature.
The meticulousness with money has stalled many a proposal, which
should have benefited his government. Said one minister. "If the
president has a remote reason to believe that a financial proposal
would benefit you even remotely, you are better off keeping it away
from him." His favourite cliche is often - "where am I going to get
that kind of money to execute that project. Abeg forget it ojare." To
show his seriousness, Newswatch learnt the president usually cuts
down on the allowances accruable to him on foreign trips. He told his
protocol officers to slash his allowances and those of his aides who
travel with him wherever the host government or where his government
is paying or providing accommodation for them.
Convinced that corruption is the greatest challenge of Nigeria, the
president has vowed that he would do something about it. Some of his
critics think he has become neurotic about the issue and question his
ability to achieve much with the campaign. It is this battle against
corruption that drives the president to want to supervise every
project. He is usually constantly briefed about the progress of all
capital projects, although he has a project-monitoring group, which
does its own evaluation.
In his cabinet, only one man can boast that his file has never been
returned without an approval. That man is said to be Uffot J. Ekaette
the secretary to the government of the federation. Aso Rock sources
confirmed that Ekaette always gets an approval because he has never
presented a proposal that involves the use of money.
On the other hand, Anenih is one man who could not complain of being
starved of funds in his own ministry. From the results of meetings of
the federal executive council, FEC, he is a favoured minister in a
favoured ministry. Hardly a week passes without the announcement of
some road projects to either be constructed or rehabilitated. This,
observers say, shows the degree of confidence the president has in
him. He has also co-ordinated government programmes such as the
poverty alleviation programme, PAP and its sister, youth employment
scheme, YES which would be handled by him also.
This has made him the envy of other ministers who could hardly boast
of having the required money for their capital projects. Newswatch
gathered that with only a few weeks to the end of the year, many of
them are still battling to have their second and third quarter
allocations for capital projects released. Some have even tried to
get the president's approval using Anenih and a few close aides
including Jubril Martins-Kuye, the minister of state for finance.
Ironically, there are complaints that such major roads as the
Shagamu-Benin expressway, are still in terrible shape.
Apart from favoured ministers and advisers, the president is believed
to take the advice of foreign governments, especially those of the
United States of America, USA very seriously. The president is on
first name terms with most world leaders and is a good friend of
President Bill Clinton. His leadership position in the West African
sub-region has also brought him close to French president, Jacques
Chirac while Nigeria's historical link with the United Kingdom gives
him a favoured place with Tony Blair. It is said that the president
consults with Clinton on many issues. The two are Baptists. He also
respects the views of leaders of the IMF and the World Bank in taking
decisions.
Although he does not act on rumours, when it comes to talks about
financial dealings, the president is believed to be all ears. He
fired one of his ministers on account of that. The minister had been
accused by his senior colleague of lodging government money in his
private account. The president confronted him. He did not deny but
asked for pardon. The president dismissed him. To ensure that he was
pardoned, he wrote a personal letter to the president asking for
forgiveness. The president accepted the letter and gladly showed it
to just about everyone.
A few days after the incident, the minister came to inform the
president that he would be travelling abroad for medical check-up.
The president nodded his approval promising that he would pray for
the minister. When he left the villa, his name was announced as
having resigned.
To show his determination to fight corruption, the first bill to be
presented to the national assembly on his assumption of office was
the anti-corruption bill. Before he swore in his cabinet, the
president brought in Transparency International, an anti-corruption
agency to conduct a seminar for them. At the end of that exercise,
the president formulated a resignation letter, which he made each of
his ministers to sign leaving the date column blank.
He told them that any day he has reason to believe that they were
engaged in corrupt practices, he would simply date the letter and
announce their resignation. He has shown his seriousness in the case
of the sacking of at least one of his former ministers. Newswatch
gathered that he has amended that order to include prosecution before
the anti-corruption commission. The president said he is doing this
in order to 'widen the island of integrity' before the expiration of
his first term.
To ensure that he is in touch with what is going on in the various
ministries, the president instituted a weekly meeting of the federal
executive council, which he mostly chairs. The first item on the
agenda of each meeting is a detailed analysis of what happens in each
ministry. To complement this, he has also instituted the Saturday
forum where experts, ministers and advisers rub minds with him over
issues of national importance.
He has told the national assembly in the wake of the corruption
scandal that rocked the legislature that he would henceforth not
release any capital votes to them. He said he would give it to the
federal capital development authority, FCDA or any appropriate
ministry charged with the responsibility of executing such contracts.
While some Nigerians say that his constant harping on corruption
would rob Nigerians of the prestige he has won for them with his
mandate, the president does not believe that. He said that the fact
of Nigeria being a corrupt country is well known. Hear him: "What are
you saying. They know. They know."
The president's leadership style has earned him a lot of enemies. The
northern part of the country, where he got the largest support at the
polls is up in arms against him. But the president has said he would
never change. It does seem though that behind that hard and
uncompromising stance, which is the hallmark of a military officer,
there is the soft part which shows that he cares. At least, a couple
of weeks ago, he sent Abubakar, his vice to Kaduna not only to be
honoured but also to hear the grouse of the north.
Whether or not he would do something about their legion of complaints
is something alse. One thing the northerners are sure of is that
without their votes his bid for a second term would be futile. For
the president, only God gives power and only him can take it away.

Original_Search_Title: Israel Assures Nigeria Of Economic Aid
Headline: Nigeria: Israel Assures Nigeria Of Economic Aid
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Israeli Ambassador to Nigeria, Mr. Arie Avidor, has
assured that his country was ready to give Nigeria all the necessary
assistance to enable it overcome some of its agricultural,
communication and other socio-political problems.
Avidor, who was speaking on Wednesday at a reception held for him by
the Nigeria-Israel Chamber of Commerce, said because the state of
Israel holds Nigeria and the entire African continent in high esteem,
it was willing to render as much help as possible to it.
"By contributing to the economy of Nigeria," he said, "we will be
contributing to the stability of Nigeria politically." This, he
reasoned, would lead to greater security in the sub-region. "We
believe also that we must play a role in Africa as we did in the 60's
and 70's. We have an experience to share... We don't have oil but we
have... ideas. Nigeria has both.
Noting that the human resources in Nigeria were more than the natural
resources, he said what was needed was that the human resources must
be employed rightly.
Avidor admitted, however, that much as Africa was the centre-piece of
Israel's international trade, the volume of trade between Israel and
Africa was not enough. "There's room to improve," he said, "because
we believe we are closer to Africa more than any other continent. But
there is greater expectation."
He said the Israeli embassy, in conjunction with the Nigerian embassy
in Israel, would endeavour to get more Israeli investors to come to
Nigeria while he expects Israeli companies in Nigeria to serve as
reliable partners in the development of Nigeria while playing active
roles in the Nigeria-Israel Chamber by "building bridges."
The Israeli envoy said the current tension in the Middle East caused
by the rift between the Palestinians and the Israelis would not have
any significant negative impact on the economy of Israel since its
six million population with a per capita income of $17,000 places the
country as a middle level economy.
The President of the Nigeria-Israel Chamber of Commerce, Chief Ben
Obi, cautioned members of the need never to be caught "in any conduct
that is on the grey side of dignity and honour. Anyone who has a
dubious background, or a shady character should please steer of the
portals of our chambers."
He spoke of the need to diversify interest and investment policies,
whether in commerce or industry as it is no longer strange for a
people to go into the most unlikely areas of investment.
Describing Nigeria as a land flowing with milk and honey, former
Chairman of UAC, Mr. Bassey Ndiokho, urged the Israelis to use their
skills to "get milk flowing" in Nigeria as they were able to turn a
desert to a fertile land. He also remarked that Israel had developed
a species of yam that could be cultivated three to four times a year,
instead of the one that is presently cultivated once yearly in
Nigeria.
Dr. Pat Utomi of the Lagos Business School described the formation of
the Nigeria-Israel Chamber of Commerce as "an opportunity to sow new
seed".
Saying it would be of mutual benefit to both countries, he stated
that it was erroneous to say business scams known as 419 usually
perpetrated by some Nigerians involved millions of Nigerians as only
a few thousands were the culprits.

Original_Search_Title: Plant &Machinery Valuation Re-Echoes As Surveyors Demand Role
Headline: Nigeria: Plant &Machinery Valuation Re-Echoes As Surveyors Demand Role
Body_Text:
Lagos — Which professional is best qualified to handle plant and
machinery valuation? This knotty issue dominated discourse at a
one-day National Workshop on "Current issues on Property and Assets
Valuation", held last week in Lagos by the Nigeria Institution of
Estate Surveyors and valuers (NIESV).
The debate which pitched the surveyors against the two engineers at
the forum, however ended with estate surveyors restating their
argument that all valuations fall under the specialist arena of their
profession.
But the session did not end without the surveyors being told the
bitter truth by some of their colleagues.
Chief Simon Ojo, an engineer as a well as fellow of the Nigeria
Institution of Estate Surveyors and Valuers (NIESV) who presented
what could be regarded as the lead paper at the workshop, observed
that "using an engineer or technician to give you an "input" to
complete a valuation for which you know you don't have specialist
training is a disservice to your client and profession".
Chief Ojo, a partner with the estate-surveying firm of Knight Frank
regretted that NIESV, which became charted 25years ago, has until
date not produced a trained Plant and machinery, agricultural mineral
valuer.
"NIESV should embark on the massive training of specialists in the
valuation profession and use these qualified specialists within
themselves", Chief Ojo advised, regretting that until date, Plant and
machinery valuation has not been included in the syllabus of the
institute.
The Knight Frank partner who is the first Nigerian to specialise in
Plant and Machinery valuation, wondered why estate surveyors are
jittery about encroachment into the practice of their profession.
"Myself as a qualified and licensed valuer was even being looked upon
at that time with suspicion because I happen to be a qualified
engineer. Why are engineers not jittery about professionals
encroaching into their profession? Why are doctors not jittery about
pharmacists encroaching into their profession?
"The reason is because we have not taken the bull by the horn. If we
had trained enough Plant and Machinery Valuers, all these problems
won't arise. If you go to the engineers to help you do a plant and
Machinery Valuation job, he is not giving you input, he is doing the
job for you", Chief Ojo declared.
Canvassing specialisation of estate surveyors in Plant and machinery
Valuation, he berated those whom he accused of "cheap money making"
who disregard Professionalism in their practice.
Chief Ojo called on the institute to embark upon the training of
auxiliaries or surveying technicians with special training in Plant
and Machinery Valuation, just as there are technologists, technicians
and craftsmen in the engineering Profession and accounting
technicians in the Accountancy profession.
"Foreigners are coming back and taking over companies. They won't use
you if you don't have the experience and training", he said.
Although Chief Ojo maintained "other professionals outside the
valuation profession have no role to play, talkless of limitation",
he warned estate surveyors "if you put your yams down and you don't
take care of them, goats will come and help themselves with the
yams".
Mr. Sam Udo-Akagha, NIESV 7th President and current Chairman of
Estate Surveyors and Valuers Registration Board (ESVRBON) informed
the surveyors that arrangement to make Plant and Machinery Valuation
part of the Institutes Syllabus is already in the pipeline.
Although most members did not buy the argument, Mr. Udo-Akagha
maintained that "Rome was not built in a day". According to him,
inviting other professionals into areas where estate surveyors don't
have the experience is not limited to the profession.
"No single profession can handle oil spills valuation," he said,
adding that the Board is however working closely with the
universities to introduce Plant and Machinery Valuation in their
curriculum.
But another frontline Realtor Mr. Charles Adebiyi challenged the
surveyors' institute to put its house in order. He regretted that
there are "no standards, manuals, models or benchmarks", to guide the
practice of estate surveying, noting that the last manual was
published by the institute in 1985.
Mr. Austin Otegbolu, a former NIESV secretary lent his weight behind
Mr. Ojo, stressing that "the fact that you have been doing something
for the past 20-30 years does not mean you are doing it right". He
stressed the urgent need for specialisation.
But Mr. Williams Oruka Odudu, a former NIESV President who presented
the first paper and incumbent president, Mr. Nweke Umezurike
cautioned against wholesale or immediate specialisation. Said Mr.
Umezurike: "The stage of development we are in now, does not favour
immediate specialisation. Someday we will get there. Let's start the
preparation for tomorrow today".
Speaking on "Engineering input in Plant and Machinery Valuation-Scope
and issues", Engr Adetokunbo Sangowawa maintained that "appropriate
engineering inputs is indispensable if we intend to have the "true"
worth of an asset".

Original_Search_Title: Returnees Depend On Relief From Council of Churches
Headline: Sierra Leone: Returnees Depend On Relief From Council of Churches
Body_Text:
Matagelema — Matagelema in the south of Sierra Leone was a prosperous
town. People were employed by the mining companies, working on
bauxite and rutile. The town was a commercial centre with a big
market place.
But the nine-year civil war has destroyed everything. Now people are
getting a new lease of life with the support of the Council of
Churches in Sierra Leone CCSL, a member of the ACT alliance.
Edward was working in the mines when the rebels came in January 95.
"We had to run away to save our lives, we could take nothing with
us", the 52-year-old man says. "About 100 people were killed", he
adds.
With his wife, his seven children and some other families he stayed
in the bush for six months. "We lived like animals," Edward recalls
the days when they had to sleep on the bare floor and eat raw food -
they lived on wild yam and cassava.
But they couldn't continue like this, so they made their way to the
town of Bo, which was held by the Civil Defence Force CDF. "Some of
us slept on the streets, market places or in schools," Edward says.
Later they could move to the newly constructed camps. "But we were
not welcome, there was a lot of suspicion because we came from the
rebel area," explains Edward as he describes the tense atmosphere in
the town at that time.
When his hometown was liberated two years ago, Edward decided to go
back. "When we returned we found nothing left - everything was razed
to the ground," he says, pointing at some ruins. "We had to start
from zero and learn how to farm. We are all farmers now".
The mining companies are still closed, because the civil war has not
come to an end and most of the 3,000 people, who lived in the town,
remain in the camps with only 200 returning so far.
ACT members, CCSL's Relief and Rehabilitation department in Bo, helps
the farmers begin a new life. CCSL distributes vegetable seeds, tools
and twines for hunting nets. Although the department works towards
self reliance by telling the people to collect seeds and provide them
for the following year, conditions in Matagelema are still difficult.
"We don't have enough to eat," Edward says. He grows rice and cassava
for subsistence. But birds are eating the cereals, rats destroy the
rice and some of the fruit plants are attacked with diseases. Now a
group has started to produce palm oil, which seems to be more
productive.
What also concerns people is that there is no hospital within reach
and no medicine available. "When you are ill, sometimes you die,"
Edward says, pointing out that Lassa fever is spreading. Even for the
CCSL staff it is difficult to reach the remote villages as it takes a
half day drive from Bo on the bumpy, muddy and unpaved roads.
CCSL is not only distributing seeds and tools. It also tries to
improve the infrastructure. In the town of Gbangbatoke CCSL has built
a primary school, a court barrie - the central meeting place of the
town and drying floors for the corn.
If there were more funding the department could start with its well
digging programme. During the civil war electricity, toilets and the
water system were destroyed. People use the stream to get drinking
water and as a toilet.
Subsequently water born diseases such as blood diarrhoea are
spreading. That has already caused 200 deaths in the area of
Gbangbatoke, where about 4,000 people live, Chief Tommy Jannah says.
People in Gbangbatoke fled their homes in 1995 when the rebels came.
Most of them were farmers like Tommie Fobbie, who had a palm tree
plantation. For more than a year the 60-year-old man lived with his
wife and five children and about 50 other families in the bush, where
one of Tommies children died from fever.
Then they sought refuge in other villages, where they got some food
from the villagers. For seven months they moved around and some of
them returned to their hometown which was still occupied by the
rebels, who killed ten and harassed the other people, Tommie says.
Tommie started farming again, but the war has made him dependant on
support.
Due to the bad road conditions nearly all the cars and trucks of the
R &R department have broken down or are in very bad condition and
there is no money for new vehicles. Lutheran World Federation LWF,
operating in the nearby Kenema district wants to support CCSL with
transportation now - six new trucks for LWF have just arrived.
The LWF office is located in Kenema - a commercial centre that has
the atmosphere of a border town, because it is close to the rebel
area. Many diamond dealers are in the town and members of the rebels
are said to come regularly into the town. With an estimated 50,000
IDPs the town is overcrowded.
LWF distributes seeds and food for agriculture in villages near
Kenema and is going to reconstruct the school in Konia soon. There
the people have had their first harvest since 1993, when the rebels
attacked and people had to flee to the bush.
"It has been peaceful for about one year now," says Baindu Mohmoh,
the 35- year-old mother of six children who lost her baby when she
fled: "The baby fell and died while I was running".
With 60 others her father was killed in the attack. Seeds from LWF
enable her to grow rice, bulgur, beans and groundnuts and she can
even sell some to buy clothes for her family. What she fears most is
the war coming back. "We suffered a lot, that's enough," she says.
Reported by Rainer Lang following a tour of Sierra Leone during
November 2000

Original_Search_Title: Energy Crisis Pushes Up Consumer Price Index
Headline: Nigeria: Energy Crisis Pushes Up Consumer Price Index
Body_Text:
Lagos — Hike in the prices of petroleum products in recent month has
brought about increase in the composite consumer price index that
measures changes in the average level of retail prices paid by house
holds in both urban and rural areas.
The Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) in its November Report said
that the average level of retail prices paid by consumers in both
urban and rural areas increased because of the increase in the prices
of kerosine, charcoal and firewood.
The report said that the composite consumer price index in both area
stood at 3792.4 in September 2000, this shows an increase of 1.4
percent above 3740.4 recorded in August 2000.
According to FOS, most price collection outlets recorded improved
supply of farm produce from the current harvests. This brought about
an appreciable drop in price of food items, while urban and rural
item indices went up by 0.3 and 1.6 percent respectively over the
period.
On a similar note, FOS reported that in the twelve months to
September 2000, the average consumer price level rose by 15.5
percent. This, according to the FOS, was higher than 13.1 percent
observes in August 2000. It added that the urban and rural indices
showed increase of 15.4 and 15.5 percent respectively during the same
period.
However, the report said that the average annual rate of rise of the
consumer retail prices was 3.3 percent which was higher than the 2.2
percent observe in the preceding month.
The record further stated that the composite food index fell by 1.2
percent in September, 2000, as the prices of yam tuber, plantain,
beans and fruits dropped in many center. However, in the twelve
months to September 2000, the composite food index advanced by 5.3
percent while the average annual rate of rise of index was 0.6
percent.
On all items less farm produce, FOS said that the index which
excluded the agricultural produce rose by 4.9 percent between August
and September 2000. While in the twelve months to September 2000, the
index went up by 29.2 percent, indicating an average annual increase
of about 7.3 percent.
Further analysis of the composite consumer price index table shows
that, in the month of September 2000, 3834.2, 3647.8, 3200.2 and
4884.4 way paid for household goods were, medical care and health,
food and transportation respectively. While, 3113.2 and 3205.1 went
into clothing and drinks.

Original_Search_Title: National Award: Matters Arising
Headline: Nigeria: National Award: Matters Arising
Body_Text:
Lagos — In this commentary, Ekwe Nche, questions the criteria used in
conferring the esteemed National award to some Nigerians. He reasoned
that some recipients have not achieved anything to have been so
honoured, especially when the president had before now alleged that
most of those so honoured were part and parcel of the nation's woes.
It is really wonderful to hear that some Nigerians will be conferred
with Nigeria's highest honors by the Government of Olusegun Obasanjo.
Yes, after working for Nigeria and producing excellent results for
the country these individuals should be rewarded with at least
National Honours. In many parts of the world, people are rewarded for
their enormous contributions to the development of their Nation.
Civil Rights Leaders like Martin Luther King Jr., eminent scientists
like Einstein, Edison, statesmen who distinguished themselves in
particular ways in their service to their country for example.
Charles de Gaulle, Winston Churchill, F. D. Roosevelt, Nelson Mandela
were given such honours. One thing that distinguishes the people who
are given these honours is evidence of an enormously transparent
contribution they have made to the progress and development of their
nation or to humanity in general.
Nigeria's Special Duties Minister, Yomi Edu announced a number of
criteria used for selecting those to whom the awards werel given.
Some of these include:
* outstanding contribution to the nation;
* service to community and international organizations, selfless
service and philanthropy,
* act of bravery,
* distinguished career,
* patriotism,
* morality,
Yomi Edu gave us the names of those who received the highest awards.
This means that these are probably those who made the greatest
contributions in these areas. The names include General Yakubu Gowon,
former President Alhaji Shehu Shagari, Vice President Atiku Abubakar,
Justice Mohammed Lawal Uwais, former Vice President, Dr. Alex
Ekwueme, Maj. General Abdullahi Mohammed, former Inspector General of
Police, Alhaji M. D. Yusufu.
These are the top recipients, the men who have recently made Nigeria
one of the most enviable "nations" on the face of the earth. They
have made Nigeria the one country that everybody wants to live in. In
fact when you travel overseas, people hold Nigeria and Nigerians in
such high esteem, almost in awe and that is the result of the
wonderful service of these men of distinction. But Mr. President, is
there really a nation called Nigeria or are you talking about a
contraption, a patchwork quilt of independent nations that are
currently held together by sheer force of arms just like Yugoslavia,
Czechoslovakia and the Soviet Union a few years ago? Is that the
nation? Never mind.
These men have rendered tremendous selfless service to our
communities. Oh yes, in fact this is one of the greatest
accomplishments of these individuals to our communities. Yes we must
honor their selfless service. Our only disappointment is that your
list of recipients is not complete. How about Ibrahim Babangida,
Theophilous Danjuma, John Shagaya, Arthur Eze, Umaru Dikko, the
strongman of Ibadan, Adedibu, Ebenezar Babatope and your good old
self Mr. President. You missed all these selfless partriots of
Nigeria? Oh, how so sad. You know they sacrificed so much, most of
them have almost became homeless themselves. Think of Baba who
publicly stated that he gave you more than one hundred million Naira
from his pocket change to enable you run for the presidency of this
country. You never denied it or challenged him. Well we all know that
Baba had been toiling as a businessman since the age of five years
when he would carry bags of groundnut on his head and trek several
miles to the next market to sell it at a small gain.
Over forty years he built a giant business conglomerate worth
billions of dollars. So he can afford to give you Mr. President one
hundred million Naira from his pocket change. Nobody should dare
suggest that he was a common thief who emptied the Nigerian treasury
into his pocket the same way Mobutu Sese Seko emptied the treasury of
Congo/Zaira into his private account. Nobody should ask how an army
officer whose salary over fifty years could never have amounted to
one million Naira, could afford to give Aremu one hundred million
naira gift. But he, think of it.
Think of paranoid Theo who upon retiring from the Army established
his own bank, bought ocean going ships, dozens of houses and a chain
of businesses all over North and Lagos. Think of John who bought a
fleet of cars and a chain of houses and businesses. Umaru's rice is
still feeding Nigerians. The people of this country will never be
hungry talk less eating from garbage cans. The people of Anambra,
Enugu, Abia, Ebonyi, Imo, Delta Rivers enjoy the best and cleanest
pipe borne water supply in the whole of Africa. Thanks to Mr. 1,000.
Can you imagine this philanthropy? Well enough for the people you
missed. How about the blessed ones your eagle eyes spotted and picked
for their contributions to the development of the nation. Wow, what a
great job they have done.
Just to show the wonder they have wrought, let's look at the
realities of the years 1965 and 2000. In 1965, power hardly ever went
out in Enugu, Onitsha, Port Harcourt, Lagos, Ibadan, Kaduna, Kano and
Jos. Clean pipe borne water was readily available in these cities.
You could make telephone calls to these cities without crying blood
from your eyes. You could make post a letter inside which you
enclosed one pound currency note. You could post that letter in Port
Harcourt, and it will arrive at the doorstep of the addressee in Kano
one week later and the visible currrency note inside the envelope
will still be in the letter. You could fly Nigeria Airways from Lagos
to Enugu, Kano, Port Harcourt or London and catch the appointment you
are chasing on time, with your luggage still intact. You could buy a
load of yams and tomatoes (perishables) and transport them from
Jos/Lafia to Port harcourt and Lagos by train without losing all of
it because of delays. The trains ran on time. Airplane pilots,
Locomotive Engine drivers, mechanics and post office workers were
Nigerians. In those primary and secondary schools, as well as
universities were places where our children went to be educated. The
students knew when schools will open and when they will vacate for
the holidays. Our university graduates were rated first class in the
world. Serious teaching and learning were actually taking place in
these schools, colleges and universities. Scholarship Boards actually
awarded scholarships to the brightest students. Roads were actually
built and maintained to last more than 10 years. Even dirty roads
were maintained and kept in good condition by men of the PWD who
actually worked to keep the roads in good shape. Senior, civil
servants, general managers of public companies knew what they were
doing. And so public companies declared profits at the end of the
year. You drove into a petrol filling station and in 10 minutes you
had your tank filled and you were on your way. When people including
civil servants said they were going to work, they actually went to
work and worked in their offices.
See the wonder that Aremu, Shehu, Jack, Alex, Lawal, Muhammadu, IG,
and the other worthy recipients have wrought. Nigeria has developed
so much that South Africa is managing Nigerian Airways flights to New
York. Ghana missed getting that job by hairs breath. Pakistan is now
running Nigerian Railway. China will rnaintain the lines and the
coaches. The Nigerian Central Bank may be supervised by the British
Chancellor of the exchequer. Getting petrol and cooking gas in
Nigeria is now as difficult as collecting rocks from the moon. Food
is so scarce and expensive in Nigeria that there is no garbage for
the poor to pick from. There are Nigerian families that have not
eaten meat in over five years. Pipe borne water is so unavailable in
cities like Lagos, Onitsha, Enugu, Port Harcourt, Jos and lbadan that
private wells and commercial water tankers have become permanent
features of every house and every neighborhood in Nigeria. Supply of
electricity is so erratic and so poor that NEPA has been renamed
Never Expect Power At all.
Take a look at some the engineering wonders these patriotic and
selfless Nigerians have built. Look at the Lagos-Benin, Enugu-Port
Harcourt, and Enugu- Onitsha dual-carriage ways. Check out the
Onitsha Owerri Port Harcourt road. If your car slips into one of the
potholes in the middle of one of these roads, that car will be
swallowed by the lake in the middle of the road and can only be seen
by divers. The Benin-Asaba dual-carriage way has been under
construction for almost ten years. Soon all the red earth from Agbor,
Ogwashi-ukwu, through Umunede to Asaba will be finished and we may
have to import red earth from Liberia to finish the job. Have you
been to our universities lately? Not only is there almost no teaching
and learning going on there, but a new culture has evolved in the
places called universities. The culture of cults, which teaches
students how to commit murder, rape, armed robbery, looting, and
arson. Intimidation, forgery, cheating, stealing, prostitution, is
now the core curriculum of our universities.
Road travel has been made a lot more comfortable in our country.
Thanks to the enterprise of private transport owners. But travelling
by bus from Aba to Lagos or Lagos to Kaduna has become as dangerous
as being on the frontline at Abagana during the Biafra - Nigeria war.
Our Post Office have become so efficient that if you post in,a steel
box containing sawdust, before it arrives Benin, steel box will be
opened and the sawdust stolen. Do you sometimes feel sorry for those
paper envelopes? Only God knows the torture they must go through
before arriving at their destinations if they ever make it there
.Have you been to a government secretariat recently? Do the
hardworking, courteous, dedicated and honest workers you meet not
pleasanty surprise you? Come to think of it isn't every clerk in the
Customs and Immigration Service, for example a multi millionaire? Who
says that is not progress. To demonstrate our competence Mike
Wallace, a 70 year old White Man [from the United States of America]
of the CBS 60 Minutes fame became a Black Nigerian farmer from Otta
after parting with just one hundred dollars at the Murtala Muhammed
airport. He went back to the Unites States of America with his
Nigerian- passport and a full Nigerian citizenship.
Today Nigeria is a corruption-free country. Thanks to War Against
Indiscipline (WAI), [MAMSER], National Orientation Program [NOP],
NIPSS National Institute for Democracy and other sham money-guzzling
programs. Do you remember the days of MAMSER when conscripted
citizens were promised fifty naira pay for showing up at a MAMSER
rally? Local government chairmen were threatened with dismissal if
they failed to conscript and deliver stipulated bus-loads of people
at a scheduled rally? Most of the conscripted folks never got a kobo
of the promised money. Do you remember all those empty, goofy
professors who were the directors of these programs? Today, they are
Ministers and Director Generals. They are the ones running the
country. Very soon they will stand in line for their National Awards
and we will cheer. Who says that licking the ass of military generals
does not pay? Does anyone still remember the diminutive but
incorruptible Professor Eme Awa who refused to rubber stamp
Babangida's selected winners before an election was even conducted
and so got promptly dismissed as Chairman of Electoral Commission?
Don't you sometimes wish we had a few more professors like that who
had spine?
Never mind the stupid international agency that classifies Nigeria as
the most corrupt country in the whole world. They don't know what
they are talking about. How can there be corruption in Nigeria when
Babangida, who could never legitimately earn one million Naira from
his job as an army officer, publicly told the country that he gave
Obasanjo the sum of one hundred and forty million Naira to run for
the presidency of Nigeria. Well the 140 million came from his lunch
or pocket money. How can there be corruption in Nigeria when
President Obasanjo was publicly accused of trying to bribe MASSOB
leader with one hundred million dollars to kill the movement. Don't
talk about bribery when President Obasanjo was recently accused by
some members of the House of Representatives of trying to undermine
the integrity of the House by bribing some members of the House with
four million Naira, to remove the Speaker. How can there be
corruption in Nigeria when his accusers reported that they had the
bribery attempt on videotape. Of course Alex, Obasanjo's chief fire
fighter was immediately dispatched hose in hand to douse the
flames.It appears that Alex has accepted this as the only thing he is
good for in Nigeria. What a shame!
Oh, there is so much to celebrate. We must celebrate justice in
Nigeria who can ever forget the letter bomb from the presidency that
killed renowned journalist, Dele Giwa? We must celebrate the
execution of Ken Saro Wiwa and other Ogoni environmental activists.
We must celebrate the destruction of the town of Umuechem. We must
celebrate with Obasanjo his great achievement, the destruction and
sacking of defenseless, inhabitants of Odi as well as the extra
judicial killing of six members of MASSOB at non-violent civil rights
rallies in Aba and Port Harcourt. We must celebrate the continued
repression of the oil- producing communities and the stealing of the
oil wealth in their land by greedy army officers and politicians most
of who have never set foot in the communities they are exploiting.
There are so many achievements to celebrate that it is very difficult
to attempt to enumerate all of them. In the early 1960's the
different ethnic groups lived fairly happily together. In fact a
Hausa man, an Alhaji, was the Mayor of Enugu, the political capital
of Eastern Nigeria. Today if you are Igbo for example living in Lagos
or Kaduna, you have to wear a steel helmet and a coat of armor all
the time because you can never tell when and where the mayhem will
start. Isn't that cause for celebration. This is part of the product
of thirty years of hard work by these patriots.
But if you are perplexed that Nigeria is celebrating corruption,
nepotism, laziness, brutality, injustice, graft, repression,
oppression, rape, murder, robbery and other heinous crimes, do not be
so surprised. You do not have to look far to find the cause. First
listen to the war cry of the Nigerian soldiers during the
Biafra-Nigeria war. Because it is so disgusting we will not even as
much as mention it here. Find it yourself and read it. Second, find
out how those Nigerian soldiers actually behaved during and after the
war. They killed innocent unarmed cilivians including very young
children. They starved millions of children, women and old men to
death. They stole their property and carted them home by trailers,
trucks and trains. They raped women and children. They destroyed
whatever they could not steal and declared that they won the war. No
wonder Biafrans simply called them Vandals. Third, who are the people
who have been ruling Nigeria these past thirty years? They are these
same soldiers and their civilian collaborators who committed these
horrific crimes against. It is these same people who murdered, raped,
loot, destroyed, stole and robbed that are ruling Nigeria. So why are
Nigerians expecting these people to behave differently from what they
did to Biafrans during the war? Naturally after they ran out of what
they stole from Biafrans, they extended their evil behaviours to
other Nigerians these people are your fathers,brothers, husbands and
friends. Did you ever ask them the abominable crimes they committed
against Biafrans. Ask Gowon, Obasanjo, Danjuma, Babangida and
Enahoro. In the meantime let's sing hurry! hurry!! Roll out the drums
and let us celebrate for Nigeria has become great, thanks to our
patriots.

Original_Search_Title: Who's Crying - The North Or The Rest Of Us?
Headline: Nigeria: Who's Crying - The North Or The Rest Of Us?
Body_Text:
Lagos — "The North shall not cry. Those who believe there can be
Nigeria without the North are merely idling away. The North is the
principal stakeholder in Nigeria. No North, no Nigeria""
That is a part of a "powerful" speech made on behalf of the North by
its appointed spokesman, Abdullahi Adamu, lawyer, governor of
Nassarawa State, at a recent reception in Kaduna for Vice-President
Atiku Abubakar.
Adamu was the Minster of State for Works under General Abdulkarim
Adisa in the Sani Abacha dispensation. He has always been a
politician. I make bold to say that if you take politics out
Abdullahi, there will be nothing left. Not even his Law. What he has
done has been to subjugate his law studies to his politics. If you
ask me, it is serving him right.
I may not be strictly correct to surmise that he must have been a
substantial partaker in, if not the sole writer of, the drafting of
the speech. Because the speech was not, to my mind, directed at
reason; nor the reality of the Nigerian situation. It was calculated
to disorientate, in the manner of the devil's advocate, the man in
the dock - regardless of the merit or the demerit of the case of the
litigant the cross-examining advocate represents. Counsel who adopt
that kind of tactics usually intend to gain technical points in the
hope that the presiding judge may look at their clients favourably.
But when the chips are down, the facts of the case usually carry with
the bright and the upright judge.
The rhetoric was grandiloquent but bared of its bone, what did
Abdullahi say on behalf of the North he represented. To me, it means
the North is crying already because the milk is spilled. The Yoruba
knew that through centuries of life experience. They say: Ko dun mi,
ko dun mi, agbalagba ng bu opa l'e mefa nitori iyan ana (It doesn't
matter, it doesn't matter, yet the elderly man curses six times on
account of missing the pounded yam last night). The North is
definitely crying because of lost opportunity - opportunity to rid
its people of ignorance; opportunity to take its people out of
poverty. Who must they blame but themselves?
"Those who believe there can be Nigeria without the North are merely
idling away", Abdullahi intoned. He knew the converse of his
rhetoric: who says there will be a Nigeria without the Southwest;
without the Southeast; without the South-South; without the
Middle-Belt, if it comes to that?
"The North is the principal stakeholder in Nigeria", thundered
Abdullahi. What is the investment? How is the North investment higher
than that of the others? Nigeria surely did not come about at the
North's instance - either by its political efforts or by conquest.
What has been the North's contribution to the commonwealth that makes
her the principal investor in our fortune since the union began in
1914?
"The incontestable fact remains that in the political equation of
this country, the North holds the ace", asserted Abdullahi. Yes, he
made a statement that should make some people think. That assertion
is correct and has history behind it. I cannot say how the North has
been able to hold some people in vice grip but it is a fact that
Adamu's North has gone that far because it has used the East and the
South- South to mount and to retain power. Even in the face of
decimating their people, time and time again. If this is not the
whole truth, let people of the Southeast and the South-South show me
what they have gained from their retaining the chains on their ankles
and on their hands. Of course, the people of the Middle-Belt have
always been the cat's paw. Recently, they have been making some
ineffectual noises about breaking their yoke, however. But can they
or would they?
Adamu's speech at Atiku's reception was a blackmail that was clear
for what it is. I grant that the North has a right to live the way it
wants to live but the South also has that self-same right.
