trading and so he took to begging. Today, he is a very bitter man. "I
want to tell the government and those ruling our land that they
should know that they are like potatoes. They are the roots and we
are the leaves. Once the leaves are not looking good, then the root
is definitely not going to look good," Zakariya said.
He said if the government was sincere with its Universal Basic
Education, UBE, programme, it would go a long way in improving
people's lives. "If they give free education and I know that I will
get food I will definitely go to school, but even as it is, big men's
children who have good western education have no jobs. They become
liabilities on their parents, so what is the hope of the children of
the poor?" Zakariya asked.
The impact of UBE is yet to be felt in most public schools. For
instance, at a primary school in Ajeromi, Lagos, pupils sat on bare
floors and were exposed to direct rays of the sun coming through worn
out and perforated ceiling. With tears in her eyes, a teacher in the
school who spoke under anonymity told Newswatch: "I believe that the
government is not blind. This is one of the government schools in
Lagos. The situation in the school can speak for itself".
She said the school had long awaited promises from both the local
government and state primary education board, SPEB, which promised to
remedy the situation. As of last week nothing had been done to redeem
the promise.
Unemployment is yet to abate. Fidelis Osamor, public relations
officer of the federal civil service commission told Newswatch
recently that about 90,000 forms had been given to first-degree
holders at the Abuja headquarters of the commission and the states.
He said the commission had to adopt stringent criteria for collection
of forms to peg the number of applicants at 90,000.
But there seems to be no respite for the applicants as yet. Newswatch
learnt that the 52 ministries and extra-ministerial departments have
failed to declare their vacancies or those of their agencies as
directed by the commission. The ministry officials are also said to
be sidelining the civil service commission by recruiting staff
directly on ad-hoc basis initially and later regularising them. This
situation is believed to have made it difficult for the commission to
carry out its function of giving jobs to the applicants.
Nigeria has never fallen short of attempts at alleviating poverty.
Previous governments devised several schemes to alleviate poverty in
the land but virtually in all cases, the attempts fell short of
expectations. For instance, the establishment of the National
Directorate of Employment, NDE, by General Ibrahim Babangida, former
president, in 1986 was to promote skill acquisition and self-
employment. But the project failed to achieve the set objectives due
to inadequate funding and corruption on the part of its officials.
Other poverty alleviation programmes like the Peoples' Bank of
Nigeria, PBN, Nigeria Agricultural and Co-operative Bank, NACB,
Family Economic Advancement Programme, FEAP, and National
Agricultural Land Development Authority, NALDA, have similarly failed
to alleviate poverty in Nigeria. The organisations were all
rationalised by the present regime to form a National Poverty
Eradication Commission, NPEC.
Since 1980, poverty level has been on the rise in Nigeria. The
federal office of statistics, FOS, poverty profile of Nigeria
published last year, showed that the level of poverty rose from 28.1
percent in 1980 to 46.3 percent in 1985 but dropped slightly to 42.7
percent in 1992. It rose sharply to 65.6 percent in 1996.
According to the report, the estimated number of the poor rose from
18 million in 1980 to 35 million in 1985. It further rose to 39
million in 1992 and to 67 million in 1996. The report showed that
poverty was at a higher level in the rural areas than in the urban
areas because 70 percent of Nigerians live in the villages.
The introduction of the Structural Adjustment Programme, SAP, in 1986
is said to have complicated the poverty situation in the country. The
growth of the gross national product, continues to dip. From 3.0
percent in 1992, it dropped to 1.3 percent in 1994 and then rose to
2.2, 3.4, 3.8, and 2.4 percent in 1995,1996, 1997 and 1998
respectively. Going by the UNDP's human development index,, HDI,
Nigeria ranks 142nd with HDI of 0.400 among the 174 countries listed
in 1997.
By 1998, Nigeria dropped to 146th position, becoming one of the
world's 40 poorest nations. The clear import from the UNDP report is
that poverty has been on the increase in direct production to the
extent of the deterioration in the economy.
A report of the independent commission on population and quality of
life published in 1966, noted that half a century after the UN
pledged to abolish hunger, millions of people still live a life of
destitution. Poverty, says the report, is "a blatant attack on
(human) dignity."
Reported by Tunde Asaju, Obong Akpaekong, Nick Odeh, Geoffrey Ekenna
and Rosemary Isado.

Original_Search_Title: Farming Of Fish Spreading
Headline: Nigeria: Making Millions From Fish Hatchery
Body_Text:
Lagos — Aquaculture or fish farming is fast spreading all over
Nigeria. Experts believe that it will be one of the highest growth
industries in the country by the year 2010.
We all now the importance of fish as a food item and also as a cheap
source of animal protein. Nigeria with a population of over 100
million people is a huge market for food fish. The demand is high but
the supply is quite low.
Indeed, there are many fishermen in Nigeria - both in the artisanal
(canoe) industrial (trawl) sectors, but their wild catches and
supplies are not enough to satisfy the requirements of the teeming
population. Even when these productions from Nigeria's inland and
coastal waters are heavily supplemented with imported fish yet the
demand continues to out-strip the overall supply. And yet, the fish
prices remain prohibitive to many.
In Nigeria, I have met many people who don't eat meat but I'm yet to
meet a fellow who doesn't eat fish in any form - except, of cause,
the Hare Krishna devotees, and they are few in number. Thus, there is
real money in food fish production which is more affordable to the
people than say, chicken. So in order to make fish readily available
at affordable costs, farming it is the way forward.
But since a lot of people would like to venture into fish production
business without being fishermen, the only road to their ambitions
will be to build fish farms in their premises, farm estates or on
marginal lands not suitable for agricultural crops. Yet in their
localities streams and rivers and impoundments with good quality
water waste idly, untapped. Others have borehole water facilities in
their premises and ignorantly leave it only to serve domestic
purpose. But a wiser use would be to attach a couple of 8m2 concrete
tanks and make tens of thousands of Naira weekly on live fish sales
to neighbourhood customers and market women re-sellers.
It is a fact that the major problem of most people interested in
commercial fish farming is how to obtain the fingerlings for stocking
when required. Once there is a ready and constant availability of
fish fingerlings in a particular community or locale, many people
there are usually attracted to fish farming in a short time.
Indeed, there is real money to be made in commercial hatchery
business which has no season. It is ideal for anyone wishing to be
self-employed and making hundreds of thousands of naira monthly in
fingerling sales - depending on the scale of your operation in your
premises or farm. It is also ideal for young school leavers and
university graduates wishing to make big money early in life.
But the scarcity of commercial hatcheries in Nigeria is a big problem
both for fish farmers and intending ones. For example, in Lagos
State, fingerlings are purchased from not more than three hatcheries.
But the, one must have to place orders weeks or even months in
advance, and at the end you may not get up to the number you require.
In most cases, Lagos fish farmers purchase their fingerlings from as
far as Ibadan and Ogun State. A fish farmer from Edo State may have
to buy his fingerlings in Lagos, Warri or Ibadan. Farmers in the
eastern states may go to Port Harcourt or Warri for supplies. Those
in the northern states purchase theirs from New Bussa in Niger State.
Imagine the distances, and by the time you transport the fragile
fingerlings to your farm, most of them would have been dead. These
problems could be a business opportunity to any body or a company
wishing to diversify into a lucrative ready cash venture.
In view of this, many farms nor are trying to build their own
hatcheries both to escape the high cost of fingerlings and also to be
to have a ready stock of fingerlings when they wanted in order to
expand their operations. But unfortunately, they lack the technology
for mass fish seed production. Without a ready availability of
fingerlings at the right time and quantity, fish farms cannot expand
their operations or increase their productions.
Some multi-national oil companies in Nigeria have set up rural
community development projects in the oil producing areas. These
laudable programmes are aimed at assisting rural farmers to boost
food production. They, among other activities, engage in improved
seed multiplication programmes for fish, cassava and yams. That is
improved fingerlings and crop seedlings which they distribute to
farmers. AGIP has the Green River Project at Obrikom in Rivers State;
Shell runs the community development programme in all oil producing
areas and; ELF has its Elf in rural life project at Oboburu also in
Rivers state.
But go to any of them and request for say 30,000 fingerlings and if
you are lucky and patient enough the highest you can get is 10,000.
In short, they normally offer 10,000 because they have many other
farmers to supply.
In hatchery business venture, all you need to start is a borehole
facility with good quality water supply and a space of about one plot
of land or less. With some breeder fish (catfish, for example) in
stock in your farm or purpose-built hatchery, using our modern
technology the operator can mass-produce millions of fingerlings a
month - either for his farm needs or for sale. One fingerling of
about 3 weeks old sale for between N5-10. Those of 4-5 weeks old sale
for between N10-15.
Assuming you have a small hatchery and produced 300,000 early
fingerlings of catfish this week, with our technology 70 per cent or
more will survive to reach advanced fingerlings stage in three weeks.
Assuming also that only 100,000 survived and you sold each at a
minimum price of only N5 instead of N10. Your revenue for that month
should be N500,000 (five hundred thousand naira). Yet the cost of
micro feed (Sanders Artemia) for feeding them to achieve faster
growth will not be less than N10,000. But you will be hard pressed
supplying buyers from within and outside the state where your
hatchery is located. But then, your immediate community needs, too.
And for those already in the business, this is the lure of
aquaculture. Thus, for your fish farms and hatcheries, it pays to
appoint consultants who are leading the way. Those who look ahead and
involve themselves now, will be the leading players in this
development, and obviously also the first to benefit.
Mr. Sharon Anochie is a Lagos-based aquaculture technologist and
consultant.

Original_Search_Title: Assessing Benin's Economic Performance 40 Years After Independence
Headline: Benin: Assessing Benin's Economic Performance 40 Years After Independence
Body_Text:
COTONOU — On the whole, Benin has achieved little
economically 40 years after independence even though its budget
substantially increased from 5.402 billion CFA francs (recurrent and
capital expenditure) in 1960, to 231.338 billion (recurrent) in 2000
(700 CFA francs = 1 US dollar).
With the devaluation of the CFA franc in January 1994, the 2000
budget shows state expenditure rising to the tune of 375.800 billion
CFA francs, which includes a deficit of 144.462 billion CFA francs to
be filled by foreign partners.
During the 1980 and 1990 decades, Benin's budget has been
characterised by a substantial deficit more or less, according to
officials in Cotonou.
The population has increased from 2.106 million in 1961 to some 6.5
million this year. This shows a 2.6 percent growth rate in 1979-1980
as against 3.3 percent currently.
On the other hand, economic growth rate which increased from 3
percent in 1981-1985, to 5.7 percent currently, is not strong to
create enough wealth to improve the well-being of the people.
Thus, the people are increasingly impoverished. The situation was
particularly acute with the drastic conditions of the three
structural adjustment programmes (SAP) negotiated with the World Bank
and the International Monetary Fund (IMF), after the collapse of the
banking system in 1989. The economy recorded a negative growth rate
of 2.9 percent during the revolutionary and Marxist- Leninist era.
The State could not pay its 40,000 employees and even had to slash
this to 30,000 in order to create room for growth, based on the
recommendations of a 1998 national study on long-term development of
the country.
In the view of World Bank and IMF representatives in Cotonou,
improved economic performance arising from the implementation SAP has
enabled the two institutions to grant Benin debt cancellation of 460
million dollars, or some 322 billion CFA francs.
This "external debt cancellation decision comes with a national
programme aimed at reducing poverty in Benin", finance and economy
minister, Abdoulaye Bio Tchane, pointed out.
The country's outstanding debt is close to 800 billion CFA francs.
Inflation, which the government estimates at 2.8 percent, is eating
deep into household incomes, particularly after the recent increase
in the price of petroleum products by between 32 and 77 percent.
Furthermore, galloping inflation has weakened the purchasing power of
the average person in Benin, thus increasing poverty in most
households. The per capita income dropped from 203 dollars in 1985 to
192 dollars in 1995.
According to the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in
1995-1996, 67 percent of the rural population and 55 percent of the
urban population of Benin lived below the poverty line.
The agency says "900,000 people of Benin, some of whom face
intermittent food shortages, are affected by extreme poverty".
However, Benin is generally self-sufficient in food with cereals
production (maize, millet, sorghum, and rice) that increased from
335,790 tons in 1978 to 700,000 tons presently.
Production of tubers (cassava, yams) has also risen constantly.
The government has even set aside one billion CFA francs in the 2000
budget to boost cassava production, part of which will be exported to
the European Union (EU).
Lack of access roads makes it difficult for food products and the
main cash crop, cotton (production of which increased from 335,000
tons in 1999 to 360,000 tons this year) to be transported from remote
areas.
Despite government's occasional maintenance efforts, many rural
access roads are not passable during the rainy season and for most of
the harvest period.
At the social level, Benin has one doctor for every 10,000
inhabitants. Mortality rate averaged 14.3 per 1,000 people between
1992 and 1997. The illiteracy rate fell from 68.8 percent in 1996 to
59.9 percent in 1992.
The national university has a student population of 12,000 while the
number of jobless people are close to 8,000 this year against 6,640
in 1988 and 7,180 in 1993.
At independence on 1 August 1960, former Dahomey, also referred to as
'Africa's Latin district' because of the number and quality of its
educated elite, was renamed Benin in 1975 under the Marxist-Leninist
revolution.
This small West African country covering 112,622 square kilometres
could have achieved more despite its limited natural resources, had
it not been completely destabilised by the spiral of military coups
that ensued just three years after independence.
Indeed, six military coups took place between 1963 and 1972, often
with the complicity of politicians engaged in bitter rivalry.
The last putsch, staged in 1972, brought the then Maj. Mathieu
Kerekou to power, for the first time, and ushered in a revolutionary
military rule.
The regime, which embraced the Marxist-Leninist ideology in 1974,
with the participation of civilians in the government, ruled the
country with an iron fist for 17 years.
It was supported by the highly politicised Benin army, which brutally
repressed the slightest inclination to challenge or contradict
official revolutionary ideas.
Many student protesters were imprisoned and sometimes tortured to
death. Former heads of state (Hubert Maga, Sourou Migan Apithy,
Justin Ahomadegbe and Emile- Derlin Zinsou) went into exile after
they were put under house arrest for over ten years.
Many other politicians and intellectuals followed suit.
Most of them returned home only to attend the historical February
1990 national conference, which granted amnesty to all the political
exiles.
Meanwhile, the national economy had collapsed since 1988. Western
donors, who could not stand both the nature of the regime and the
catastrophic manner in which the country's meagre resources were
being managed, abandoned the State.
In 1989, on the eve of the national conference that was to dissolve
all the revolutionary institutions and set up a democratic process,
the government abandoned the Marxist-Leninist option.
The conference also restored public freedom, particularly the freedom
of the press and a full multiparty system.
A one-year (1990-1991) transitional government was set up. A
transitional parliament granted President Kerekou personal amnesty
for all wrongdoings committed during the 17 years of his rule.
In March 1991, at the end of the second round of the first free
presidential elections, interim Prime Minister Nicephore Soglo was
elected for a five-year term beating Kerekou, his challenger.
In March 1996, Kerekou defeated Soglo in the second round of the
second presidential polls to make a come-back to power.
The next presidential election will be in March 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Staple Foodstuffs Stabilise
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Staple Foodstuffs Stabilise
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of foodstuffs and other consumer items in the country
have experienced relative stability in recent months.
Analysts attribute this development to weak purchasing power
influenced by low disposable income of consumers and the inflation
rate which has continued to fall to a single digit since December
1999.
The prices of edible items in Lagos markets have remained stable for
sometime, while in other cities there are slight differences.
In most contemporary markets in Lagos, a bag of garri sells at an
average cost of N2,100 while a tin sells at N280.
A bag of beans costs an average of N5,200 while a tin of beans sells
at an average of N650 and a derica cup stands at N40 or N45 depending
on the specie.
A bag of rice sells at an average of N2,500, a tin costs N750, an
olodo/mudu sells at N180, while a derica cup costs between N40 and
N45 depending on the specie.
A tuber of yam falls within N120, N90 and N60 depending on the size.
A mudu or olodo of crayfish sells at an average of N250, while a
basket of onions costs N 120.
A basket of fresh tomatoes sells at an average of N900, a tin of
vegetable oil sells at N650 while a bottle of palm oil costs N70. A
bag of egusi costs N6,500, a derica cup sells at N80 while a bag of
"ogbono" sells at an average of N28,000.
Information from the Federal Office of Statistics (FOS), Lagos,
indicates that the composite Consumer Price Index (CPI), stood at
34140.0 in April, 2000, indicating that the cost of retail prices of
consumer goods and services has risen by thirty-four fold between
September 1985, the base period, and April, 2000. The index
represents an increase of 2.0 per cent above 3347.2 recorded in the
corresponding month of March, 2000.
The price of edible oil dropped in many cities and between April,
1999 and April, 2000 the composite food index fell by 1.0 per cent
showing that the average level of prices of most food items in April,
2000 was lower than the corresponding average level for the same
period a year ago. The percentage change in the average of the index
for twelve month period ending April, 2000 was a decline of 0.9 per
cent.
All items excluding farm produce index worked up by 1.9 per cent in
April, 2000. Within the twelve months, the index rose by 2.0 per cent
and the percentage change in the average of the index for the
twelve-month period ending April, 2000 were 6.4.
The composite consumer food index rose by 1.8 per cent in April, 2000
and the rise in the index was largely caused by increases in the
prices of staples.
Analysts attribute this development to the general aggregate rate
from all the states of the federation, thus price differences exist
in some states, while it is stable in others. Some of the items that
recorded price increase and caused the rise in the consumer index
include food items, kerosene, and clothing materials.
The urban and rural indexes registered increase of 2.1 and 2.0 per
cent respectively. However, the average consumer price level for
April, 2000 showed slight changes compared with the corresponding
level in April, 1999, and within this period, the urban index merely
rose by 0.6 per cent while the rural index fell slightly by 0.2 per
cent.
The average annual rate of rise of consumer retail prices or the
percentage change in the average CPI for the twelve month period
ending April, 2000 stood at 1.8 per cent, indicating that a fixed
market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100
during the twelve month period ending April, 1999 sold for N101.8 on
the average during a corresponding period of April, 2000.
S/No Food items Quantity Price
1 Garri A bag N2,100
2. Beans A bag N5,200
3. Rice A bag N2,500
4. Yam A tuber N60-N120
5. Crayfish A mudu N250
6. Onions A basket N120
7. Fresh Tomatoes A basket N900
8. Vegetable Oil A tin N650
9. Egwusi A bag N6,500
10 Ogbono A bag N28,000

Original_Search_Title: Blinded By Free Trade And Comparative Advantage Dogma
Headline: Liberia: Blinded By Free Trade And Comparative Advantage Dogma
Body_Text:
Atlanta — In his reaction to my recent article, "The Rice Import
Liberalization Agenda in Liberia: Some Critical Policy Issues", Harry
Greaves, Jr. has clearly evaded the crucial arguments of the article
and focused instead on preaching rhetoric about comparative
advantage, free trade and globalization.
In asking the question, "What's the Case for Self-Sufficiency in Rice
Production?", Greaves asserted that the article was based on "the
false premise" that Liberia should be self-sufficient in rice
production. He then tried to argue that such a policy option is
unnecessary in the context of a new "global economy in which the easy
accessibility and relatively low cost of information has made trade
amongst peoples over vast expanses of geography a snap".
He continues, "As a consumer, I can log on to the Internet from my
perch in Monrovia, and in an instant get quotations from rice
producers as far afield as Thailand, Vietnam and Louisiana to enable
me to make an informed decision as to whether I would be better off
to buy my rice from a farmer in Tappita or from one of foreign
competitors". Greaves also described my article (which is also
available online for readers to judge) as advocating "policy
prescriptions purely on the basis of emotion", and he cautioned us
that the answer to Liberia's economic questions "lies in research and
analysis, not in ideology".
It is obvious, ladies and gentlemen, that this commentator has either
misunderstood or deliberately chosen to distort my arguments. To
reiterate, the main thrust of the article was about explaining some
of the possible policy implications of rice import liberalization for
macroeconomic stability and Liberia's longstanding preoccupation with
rice self-sufficiency. It was not about judging or advocating any
strategy as an appropriate policy option for Liberia. Had this been
the objective of the article, every effort would have been made to
present the case successfully. It is therefore offensive and
intellectually bankrupt for someone else to ascribe a false objective
to my thoughts. Most students of Liberia's modern economic history,
perhaps with the exception of our critic, are aware that successive
Liberian governments, since at least the early 1970s, have to
different degrees embarked upon a strategy of rice self-sufficiency.
Under its 1976-1980 National Socio-Economic Plan, the Tolbert
government, for example, introduced and pursued this policy option.
The Doe government also made similar efforts in the 1980s. About a
year ago, Taylor gave his most vocal support yet to this strategy
when he accused Liberians of being "too lazy" to produce their own
food, and threatened that "anyone who wanted to eat imported rice
would pay more for it" (Star Radio Liberia Daily News Bulletin,
October 24, 1999). Few months later, however, this same Taylor
government was embarking upon rice import liberalization. My article
was about explaining the domestic and international imperatives
driving this new policy attitude of the government, and how rice
import liberalization might possibly affect Liberia's longstanding
pursuit of (or flirtation with) rice self- sufficiency as a policy
option. Nowhere in the discussion that a judgement was passed on the
appropriateness or inappropriateness of this policy. I hereby
challenge Greaves to identify where in that article I have expressed
"emotion- laden cant, hype and theology about the need for Liberia to
be self-sufficient in rice", as he falsely claimed. Where in the
article was any support or condemnation espoused for rice
self-sufficiency as a policy option? Where in the article did I
advocate the adoption of "policy prescriptions purely on the basis of
emotion", as insinuated by him? Greaves failed to identify where
these things were expressed in the article.
One would think that a man, who claims to be interested in "research
and analysis, not in ideology", would have avoided the temptation of
being blinded by orthodox economic ideology and concentrated instead
on the practical policy questions raised in the article. However,
like a 6-year old boy anxious to show off his new toy, Greaves chose
to impress upon us his knowledge of the old theoretical dogma of
Ricardian comparative advantage and free trade (all of which are
being somewhat discredited by global economic and human realities).
Instead of debating on how rice import liberalization might affect
Liberia's internal and external balances and the policy option of
rice self-sufficiency (which was the objective of the article), he
decided to preach about how international trade is free, and
therefore Liberia does not need rice self- sufficiency because
consumers like him can "log on to the Internet" from Monrovia and
locate cheaper rice in Thailand, Vietnam and Louisiana instead of
buying it from Tappita. It is obvious that this man's whole reaction
to the article was prompted by mere ideology and not based on any
genuine interest in debating the serious policy issues raised in the
article.
Could it be that our critic is so irretrievably committed to
neo-liberal economic ideology that he considers any expression of
differences with this order as a personal challenge? Perhaps, he felt
that my brief discussion of the possible negative implications of
rice import liberalization was a challenge to his ideological
convictions, and therefore he had to come up to defend and protect
his creed. But in doing so he has committed a serious analytical
blunder. As someone who claims to be particular about "research and
analysis", he should have been careful about the points at which to
critique the article. He should have, for example, tried to refute my
argument that the spread of import liberalization to many more
sectors of the Liberian economy may result in government revenue loss
and have a destabilizing effect on the government's self-established
objective of fiscal policy restraint. Instead of distorting and
evading the basic arguments of the article, he should have attempted
to refute the claim made that the extension of import liberalization
to more economic sectors, including rice, may lead to export dumping
and a possible discouraging impact on the domestic production of
rice. Oh, less we forget, these policy implications of import
liberalization are not relevant because, for him, the Liberian
consumer can log on to the Internet from Monrovia and get rice
cheaply from Louisiana.
It is important for Greaves to realize that we do not need to be
taught the virtues of comparative advantage and free trade, for it is
common knowledge that nations must engage in some form of
international exchange of goods, services, and capital investments.
Therefore, what is being questioned is not the principle of
international trade and investment as such, but the systemic
inequalities underlying the global economic system as well as those
ideological tools that are used to perpetuate them. Unfortunately,
certain individuals have succumbed to these ideological stratagems
and therefore find nothing wrong with the current system of
international trade and investment. For them, the global economy is
operating perfectly, as predicted by the neo-classical theories of
comparative advantage and free trade. Such a conclusion is false,
however. And while it is unworthy to be dragged into the ceaseless
debates about the virtues of economic liberalism, there are few
points that have to be made here to let our critic know that,
contrary to his deep-seated convictions, the world trading system is
not as smooth as predicted by the nineteenth-century theories of
comparative advantage and free trade. It is important to make these
points since his argument is that these theories are the true
determinants of the pattern of international trade and therefore
Liberia must conform to them. And afterwards, I will adapt the
discussion to the specific issue of the appropriateness of rice
self-sufficiency, since this is what Greaves is anxious to talk
about.
To begin with, it is worth remembering that Adam Smith, David Ricardo
and their neo-classical successors invented their theories of free
trade long after the British had established their industrial
predominance. As noted by Theresa Hayter (1985), in the early days of
British industrialization, industrialists sought and obtained ample
protection for their 'infant' industries against foreign competition.
The British not only protected their own industries from foreign
competition but also destroyed those of other countries. Thus, the
fact that Portugal focused on wine production had nothing to do with
the natural workings of comparative advantage. Such was imposed by
the British government through the Methuen Treaty of 1703 (Hayter,
1985). Our critic seems not to also understand that the current
pattern of international trade, in which most African countries are
producers and traders of primary commodities, has less to do with the
natural workings of the market. Part of this international division
of labor and pattern of trade was established through the imposition
of slavery, colonialism, and neo-colonial economic relations. Is he
not aware of the colonial destruction of Africa's textile industries
(Rodney, 1974), or is he blind to the rubber plantation history of
our own country? These are relevant points to remember because
individuals, like Greaves, are insisting on converting us to the
false belief that nations have 'natural' destinies to produce and
trade in different goods, and thus, for example, a country like
Britain was naturally destined to produce industrial products, while
Liberia was destined to trade in non-industrial products. They want
us to believe, for example, that it is the natural destiny of
"Thailand, Vietnam and Louisiana" to produce rice, while it is the
destiny of Liberia to produce rubber, "cocoa or coffee or some other
crop".
The theory of comparative advantage, romanticized so much by our
critic, and which is supposed to be the theoretical foundation of
international trade, has long been contradicted by real-life global
economic situations. For instance, the Heckscher-Ohlin Model, which
is the expanded version of the original Ricardian theory of
comparative advantage, was found to be incapable of explaining United
States' pattern of trade. In his famous Leontief paradox, the 1973
Nobel Prize winner Wassily Leontief provided strong evidence that
although the US was the more capital-abundant country (with higher
capital-labor ratios), long before Western Europe and Japan started
catching up, it was not exporting capital-intensive goods and
importing labor-intensive goods. Such a finding was in sharp contrast
to the comparative advantage theory that a country should produce and
export those goods that are intensive in the factors with which the
country is relatively well endowed. This empirical evidence is
mentioned here to underline the widely known fact that most of the
theories of comparative advantage and free trade, being so much
romanticized, are not as absolutely realistic as some believe. If
these theories were the true determinants of global economic
realities, then there would be greater international economic welfare
and harmony among nations. But this is not the case, except in Mr.
Greaves' wonderland.
The Ricardian theory of comparative costs, or relative advantage,
asserts that free trade makes countries to specialize in those
economic sectors where they have a relative advantage. This
specialization, in turn, leads to expansion of international trade,
resulting in increased global economic welfare. But, as Yoichi (1992)
correctly argued, what the enforcement of this theory has caused, at
least in the last 30 years, cannot be regarded as greater
international economic welfare. According to this theory, for
example, Japan was supposed to specialize in those industries in
which it enjoyed a relative advantage. The pursuit of this
specialization, however, resulted in a chronic trade surplus between
her and the United States, which has been the source of persisting
economic and international friction between the two countries. The US
transformed agriculture into an export industry, resulting in a
worldwide surplus of farm products, which precipitated a "grain war"
with the European Community. Moreover, this ceaseless pursuance of
comparative advantage has led to the excessive availability to
international trade of primary commodities, contributing to a
worldwide destruction of the natural environment.
Another point that our critic should note about comparative advantage
is that the pursuance and application of this theory is not always
politically and strategically neutral. So for example, in a genuinely
free market environment, Europe and Japan should have abandoned food
production and allowed the South, which has comparative advantage in
food production, to produce food for them (Tandon, 1999). But this is
not the case. These countries are not only involved in agriculture
and food production but are heavily subsidizing and protecting their
agricultural industries, mainly for security and political reasons.
And yet they are the originators and masters of the Internet, who
could have easily logged on to it from their perches to locate
cheaper food around the world, in line with Greaves' thinking. Had
there been a true devotion to comparative advantage based on a
genuine free market, the South should have also been the natural
clothier of the rest of the world. But this is not so. For 30 years
the Multi-Fibre Agreement (MFA) gave protection to Northern producers
of textiles and clothing, and while the Uruguay Round of trade
agreements calls for the dismantling of the MFA, it has left in place
'safeguards' for Northern producers.
This is why our critic must understand that his renditions about
comparative advantage and free trade are a bunch of empty rhetoric
and ideological posturing. All comparative advantages, except perhaps
those based on natural resource endowments, are deliberately
constructed by societies through conscious economic, technical, and
political efforts. They are not handed down on a silver platter. The
sooner we understand this the earlier we will move to build such
advantages for ourselves and stop demanding others to show us "data
which demonstrates that Liberia enjoys such a comparative advantage
in rice production". On the issue of the appropriateness of rice
self-sufficiency for Liberia, which the critic wants us to talk
about, I am aware, in agreement with others who had commented on this
issue, that there are several structural and institutional barriers
that have to be overcome to make rice self-sufficiency a realizable
and feasible policy option for Liberia. Thus, there is a need for a
careful study of this policy issue, as called for by other Liberians,
such as Sirleaf (November, 1999). I also agree that the country would
be better off focusing on food security involving raising overall
self-sufficiency rates in the production of not only rice but also
other major food commodities, such as cassava, sweet potatoes, and
yams (Nathan Associates, 1989 in Toe, 1994). My disagreement is with
only those who are insinuating that because of the existence of these
obstacles there is no reason for self-sufficiency, and therefore no
one should discuss the implications of government policy for such an
option. My disagreement is with only those who harbour a fanatical
faith in the imaginary perfection and ability of free trade to
deliver food to the doorsteps of Liberia's poor consumers. It is
these individuals who are also making misguided and unfounded
statements about the situation of food self- sufficiency in the
world.
Greaves states in his opening paragraph, "But there is no economic
law which stipulates that a country has to be self-sufficient in its
staple. Indeed, if we applied this logic to every nation on God's
green earth, then we would all have to return to subsistence farming,
. . . Thus, the Japanese (great consumers of beef) would have to herd
their own cattle and eschew Texas beef, . . .". This statement is
erroneous, deceptive and self-contradictory. While the Japanese may
be great importers and consumers of Texas beef, this is not their
shushoku (staple food). Rice is their staple food, and it has been
that way for centuries. It is not beef that most Japanese would kill
you for, but it is rice. And because it is their staple food, they
have been self-sufficient in it since the 1960s, and have been
adamant about not allowing its importation. It is only a year ago
that Japan finally agreed to allow some importation of rice, even
then behind very high tariffs. Therefore, while there may be no
economic law stipulating self-sufficiency in a country's staple food,
many countries including Japan are completely self-sufficient in
their staples.
Another sweeping and unguided statement that our critic made, in
defense of his free trade and comparative advantage credos, is the
assertion that the information cost of trade is low for Liberian
consumers because they can now "log on to the Internet" from their
perches in Monrovia. At this stage in the development of Liberia, it
is clear that this is yet another Disney-Land thinking on the part of
Greaves. While the progress of globalization is bringing economies
closer through advances in information technologies, these
technologies are at present only accessible to very few privileged
business, intellectual, and political elites in Africa. Where, at
this time in Liberia, can the common consumers log on to the Internet
to "get quotations from rice producers as far afield as Thailand,
Vietnam and Louisiana" to enable them make informed decision about
where to buy rice? Such will not be possible for a very long time.
This means that it is only the few business importers of rice in
Monrovia who will have access to such an information advantage and
use it perhaps to profiteer. How will they profiteer? Well, they
(perhaps including Greaves) will log on to the Internet from their
perches in Monrovia and locate cheaper rice in Thailand. But when
they import the cheaper rice to Liberia, they will sell it to the
poor people in West Point and Tappita at higher profit margins. This
is why at the present moment the informational advantage of the
Internet cannot be construed as necessarily conferring greater power
on the common consumer in Liberia.
As mentioned earlier, there are several institutional and structural
obstacles that have to be overcome to make rice self-sufficiency a
viable policy option for Liberia. But this does not imply that there
are no compelling reasons for self-sufficiency in rice. One of such
reasons, to me, is the question of community and, by extension,
national food security. As noted above, food security has to be based
on achieving overall self-sufficiency in other major food crops
including but not limited to rice. Such a broadening of the strategy
for food self-sufficiency will reduce the over-concentration and
investment on a single food crop, thereby improving Liberia's chances
for achieving food self- sufficiency and security. Self-sufficiency
is important for achieving community and national food security in
view of the periodic volatility of world and domestic economic
conditions. Subsistence farming and the domestic production of
Liberia's major food crops would serve as a safeguard against such
unfavorable economic fluctuations. It is important for rural
Liberians and communities, who constitute the majority of the
national population, to always be secure of their food supplies even
during times of world economic disruptions.
One particular example of these adverse international economic
conditions is the unreliability and fluctuation of Liberia's foreign
exchange flow. Greaves may be giving us the false impression that the
importation of rice from Thailand is free. But this is not so,
because even if all the consumers in Liberia could log on to their
Internets and determine that it is better for them to import their
rice from Thailand and not Tappita, they would need to have the
required foreign exchange to pay for it. In this connection a
critical issue, which our critic seems to be less concerned about, is
the fact that Liberia's main sources of this foreign exchange (export
earnings and foreign capital flows) have experienced periodic crises
at least in the last 30 years. A particular mention is made of the
period since 1968-1969 when various crises became pronounced in the
external sector of the Liberian economy. Thus, the country's
financial crisis, which had been occasioned by debt payments in the
late 1960s (Pereira- Lunghu, 1995), was further exacerbated in the
last half of the 1970s when revenues from exports of iron ore and
rubber began to decline due to the eruption of the global economic
crisis. Indeed, it was in the context of this financial crisis that
the Tolbert government introduced the strategy of rice
self-sufficiency aimed at saving foreign exchange on the importation
of rice. Heavy external borrowing in the 1980s, despite weakened
external demand for Liberia's export commodities, and climaxed by
seven years of civil war in the 1990s, has now left the country with
very deplorable Debt Service-to-Exports and Debt Service-to-GDP
ratios of 70.8 percent and 11.6 percent, respectively (Preliminary
figures from IMF Staff Country Report, April 2000). The negative
consequences of such high debt burdens for foreign capital flow and
foreign exchange are easy to understand.
This is why we cannot solely rely on world trade and financial
markets to deliver our staple food. In much of Liberia's economic
history, we have relied upon importation of our staple food, paid for
through international trade exchange and sometimes through food aid
and the good will of friendly nations. This has not, however,
prevented food shortages. A main reason for this is that the sources
of finance that we have depended upon to pay for our food imports
have not been reliable. This is why I believe that we cannot solely
rely upon the importation of our major foods, because there will
always be times that we may not have the required export earnings and
foreign assistance needed to pay for our food imports. Therefore,
efforts have to be made not only to broaden our export base but also
to increase food self-sufficiency and security.
The views just expressed in the preceding paragraphs, on the reasons
why Liberia needs to achieve food security through self-sufficiency,
are my first public declarations on this issue. They are
preliminaries, but I shall be writing a full article dedicated
exclusively to this issue of whether or not the country needs
self-sufficiency for ensuring food security. These issues are here
for any interested persons to debate them, but in the process of
doing so we must avoid misrepresentations and distortions.
Geepu Nah Tiepoh is a development economist and consultant with ACLAD
Development, Canada.
For subscription information, go to: www.theperspective.org or send
e-mail to: editor@theperspective.org

Original_Search_Title: War Against Militant Groups
Headline: Nigeria: War Against Militant Groups
Body_Text:
Lagos — More than the allegations of arbitrariness and miscarriage of
justice, there are other factors that many have informed the
directive from Federal Government to state Police commands to clamp
down on militant groups in their areas of operations.
The regular press briefing of the Lagos State Commissioner of Police,
last Tuesday, witnessed a radical departure from its usual course.
That day, Mr. Mike Okiro, the commissioner, was not in the mood for
the exchange of banters that usually preceded the business of the
day. Rather, the police boss was serious and furious. The opening
paragraph of his prepared statement said it all.
"The Lagos State Police Command has observed with dismay, the
unfortunate events and incidents of man's inhumanity to man, wanton
destruction of lives and property of innocent citizens within Lagos
metropolis, including the unprovoked killing of law enforcement
agents, police officers and well meaning people of Lagos State by a
group of hoodlums who have christened themselves Oodua People's
Congress (OPC)," Okiro remarked. Having set the stage for the day's
agenda, Okiro recounted series of recent embarrassing activities of
the OPC that are currently pitching it against the state. Among
others, Okiro accused the largely South-west Yoruba based
organisation of engaging in senseless murder of innocent citizens in
the guise of fighting robbery. He cited instances of victims of the
group's latest acts in Ajangbadi, a suburb in Ojo Local Government
Area of the state. The commissioner also alleged that some members of
the organisation are often used to settle scores by parties involved
in disputes.
On this instance, he recalled the case of one Mrs. Sibat Eyitayo, a
resident of Aguda, Surulere, who, earlier in the month, was attacked
by persons suspected to be OPC members. Her assailants were hired by
one Mr. Maurice Amonye of number 8 Nureni Street Ipaja, to unleash
violence on her because she was indebted to him. The police chief
equally cited cases in which the OPC had in the last couple of days,
made life meaningless to the residents of the state by holding
certain areas and individuals to ransom.
These, Okiro frowned at and remarked that the state police command
would not shirk in its statutory responsibility of protecting the
lives and property of residents as well as maintaining law and order.
The commissioner capped his address with a warning "that the
existence of democracy does not and will not imput a state of
lawlessness or disregard for the laws of the land or the fundamental
rights of law abiding citizens."
Not many, who had keenly followed the unfolding events in the state,
in the last couple of days, were surprised by Okiro's riot act.
In fact, if anything in their estimation, the reaction from the
police boss was rather belated. Two embarrassing activities
attributed to the OPC prepared the ground for the police sudden
response. There was for instance, a bloody clash between Okiro's men
and members of the ethnic organisation last Sunday. The clash, which
had the trappings of a battle, cut across Itire, Mushin, Mile 12,
Oshodi, Surulere and Agege areas of the state. Death toll in the
fracas was put at over 20, with the police loosing two of its men. 18
people were reportedly arrested in connection with the mayhem. The
actual cause of the clash remain sketchy. But a source told The Post
Express on Saturday that trouble started when police authorities
refused members of OPC in Bariga to hold an unauthorised meeting in
the area. This did not go down well with the organisers of the
meeting who, in trying to have their way, fell out with the police.
Before the Sunday episode, a clash, the previous Thursday between the
largely Igbo traders at Alaba International Market, Ojo, and members
of the OPC, had left about 15 persons dead. The crisis was triggered
off by the killing of an Igbo boy, Ikechukwu Obi, by members of OPC.
The late Ikechukwu was said to be having an outstanding rent dispute
with his Yoruba landlord.
The latter took the case to court where it was adjourned till July
27.. Before the date of the scheduled hearing however, the landlord
was said to have lodged complaints to the OPC, labelling the deceased
a robber. Without proper investigation, the members of the militant
organisation allegedly pounced on the trader, killing him and setting
his corpse ablaze. The news instantly filtered into the electronics
market. Promptly, his colleagues asked for justice and in the
ensuring skirmishes, a full blown riot erupted, which with the
passage of days almost pitched the Igbo traders and their Yoruba
hosts at different camps. The state police command laboured to
restore order. The state Governor, Bola Tinubu, condemned the wanton
destruction of lives and property, threatening to impose curfew on
any trouble zone.
On their own, the leaders of the OPC have always claimed that the
organisation is only being blackmailed by mischief makers. On several
occasions, Kayode Ogundamisi, the secretary-general of the outfit has
maintained that members of his group do not harm innocent people.
"What the OPC has done is to make sure that the neighbourhood is kept
safe," he insisted.
Giving the impression that his organisation is a benevolent type,
Ogundamisi held that they were forced into dealing with robbers
because of police copious inadequacies. He said specifically that the
Nigeria police "are underfunded and do not have enough fire power to
face these robbers."
Those that have favourable sentiments for the organisation easily
throw up this explanation as a strong reason for its activities.
Before it began being enmeshed in personal and group agenda, the
argument had worked for OPC. Today however, the genuiness of the
explanation appears to be a matter of conjecture and generally
depends on who is involved.
All the same, similar organisations that profess elimination of
crimes as their main objective, are springing up in different parts
of the country. Apart from crime detection and prevention, they are
also used in protecting group interests. Prominent in this class are
the South-east based Bakassi Boys, the Ebgesu boys of the South-south
and Arewa Peoples Congress (APC) of the North.
A common denomination, on the surface, appears to be running through
the organisations. They are essentially informed, on the one hand, by
the glaring inability of the police to provide the needed protection
and the desire to protect group interest. The Post Express on
Saturday, gathered that these dual functions, informed the invitation
of the Aba based Bakassi boys to Onitsha, Anambra State, by Governor
Chinwoke Mbadinuju, recently. Before the arrival of the dreaded gang,
the commercial city was virtually a no-go area. Robbers struck both
at day and night making insecurity the order of the day. On two
occasions within an interval of three days, Lagos-bound commercial
luxury buses were attacked, over 40 passengers killed and millions of
naira taken away. Within the streets, similar situations prevailed.
The citizens became helpless and began to flee the town. And the
police had no response. Some of their men were even accused of
conniving with the men of the underworld. Forced to the wall
therefore, Mbadinuju had no alternative than to reach out to the
Bakassi squad whose earlier exploits in Nnewi spoke volumes. The Boys
promptly responded to the invitation and in a matter of days, their
operation began to yield results.
Even the hardened robbers fled the town. Those that were not lucky
were apprehended, thoroughly screened, made to confess their sins and
matcheted to death. All over the city and beyond, people heaved a
sigh of relief.
But the Federal Government is no longer amused with the messianic
roles ascribed to the vigilante organisations. Accordingly, it has
mapped out strategies to clamp down on all of them, irrespective of
what the people think about their individual activities and
objectives. Acting through the Inspector- General of Police (IGP),
Musliu Smith, the authorities are said to have directed state police
commands to descend on all militant groups operating under the guise
of vigilante groups. Apart from the allegation of the groups being
used to settle personal scores and in the process, killing innocent
citizens, the IGP was said to have reminded his colleagues that it
amounted to a vote of no confidence on the police for the militant
groups to be seen as the real outfit maintaining law and order. Smith
specifically directed that militants who lynch suspected criminals
should be arrested and charged for murder.
Signs of the latest directive began to emerge last week, when Smith
was invited alongside the Minister for Police Affairs, General David
Jemibewon (rtd), to a Federal Executive Council (NEC) meeting. A
source at the meeting disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo
after assessing the security situation in the country, gave the
police a low performance rating. He, particularly was said to be
miffed that both Jemibewon and Smith had allowed their men to
abdicate their statutory responsibilities to the unconventionally
trained militant groups. Pointedly, he was said to have told the two
henchmen of the police to either sit up or be prepared to get the
boots.
The duo, The Post Express on Saturday learnt were not only ears as
Obasanjo read the riot act. They told him the police perceived
incompetence in executing its duties had nothing to do with inability
on the part of their men but had to do with a combination of
personnel and infrastructural inadequacies currently hitting the
force. They reminded the president of the problem of obsolete
equipment and shortage of personnel with which the police had had to
contend with over the years. The IGP specifically told the president
that his men had not abandoned their responsibilities to militant
groups but had all along seen these organisations as complementing
their efforts. However, he was said to have assured the president
that with the rate at which some of them were going, measures would
be taken to curtail their excesses. It was in carrying out this
pledge, our source learnt, that the directive from police high
command was issued to the state.
However, beyond the factor of drawing the attention of the police big
wigs to the uncomfortable exploits of the militant groups in crime
eradication, Obasanjo is said to be concerned that the rate at which
events are unfolding in the country, some of these militant groups
could be turned into regional armies and in the process threaten the
corporate existence of the nation. Certain  experiences in the
recent past were said to have combined to inform his fears. In the
East, West and North, there had been incidents that had made him lose
days of sleep.
There had been, for instance, the recurrent activities of the OPC in
his native South-west. Apart from the two most recent cases of
violence involving the group, it had in the past acted or been
portrayed in lights that made the government jittery. There was for
instance, the late November 1999 showdown between the group and a
segment of Hausa traders at the popular Mile 12 market in Kosofe
Local Government Area in Lagos.
A leadership tussle among some members of Sukura Yam Sellers
Association in the market had degenerated into an ethnic warfare
between the Yorubas and Hausas.
Before calm was restored after two days of fierce fighting, the death
toll fluctuated between 40 and 60. Visibly angered by the rate of
destruction, Obasanjo ordered that from then "anybody who calls
himself OPC should either be arrested or shot on sight if he resists
arrest."
Obasanjo's, warning to his kinsmen was as well intended to inform
other ethnic organisations that they were not above the laws of the
federation.
Barely two months later, militant Muslim youths in Kaduna State
engaged in acts that suggested that the warning was not heard or not
meant for them. Following the controversy generated by the state's
attempt to introduce the Islamic legal code, local youths unleashed a
regime of terror to the Christian adherents in the state.
Incidentally or by design, the Igbos were the hard hit. As the death
toll rose, the South easterners realised that as in the past, they
had fallen victims to an issue that was not of their making lorry
loads of decomposing corpses brought into Aba, the commercial nerve
centre of the east and incidentally, the base of the Bakassi boys,
brought out the ire in the group. Instantly, they went for revenge
killings. But peace was soon restored.
Interestingly, while the groups battled against themselves or went
for reprisals, the law enforcement operatives were caught napping.
For one thing, they appeared better organised and possessed superior
fire powers to the police. Thus, analysts with critical bents have
been quick to observe that whatever peace that may be prevailing in
the country, with the militant groups fully intact, may not last the
test of time.
"What we have, right now, could be likened to a balance of terror as
we had in the days of the cold war" observed Mr. Dick Uduma, a
lecturer in Political Science, Abia State University, Uturu.
According to the don, "with the massive arms build up by these
associations, the country could as well be said to be sitting on a
keg of gun power. In that case, any thing could happen at anytime."
His fears are not in vein. Rather recurrent incidents in different
parts of the country give bite to them. In the north, for instance,
the governors of the Sharia states are not shifting grounds from
their rigid position. As a counter force, the southerners are
throwing away their petty jealousies and suspicions of one another
and coming together to form a formidable force against the Sharia.
There is therefore the fear that when conditions entirely slip out of
control, individual blocs may fall back on the militant groups in
their fold to pursue their agenda. Analysts with a strong sense of
history and international relations easily agree that that was how
the crises that are presently consuming Somalia, Yugoslavia, Congo
Republic and Sierra Leone, began. Obasanjo, a major actor in the
search to bring out peace in these trouble spots, does not lose sight
of this. On Tuesday, he raised an alarm that the ultimate objects
behind the formation of these groups as well as the Sharia haze were
an attempt to embark on secession by the blocs involved. It was to
nip these suspicious moves in the bud that the president called on
the police to sit up to their constitutional roles.
Obasanjo's marching orders could sound well in ordinary times. "But
these are not ordinary times," retorted Haruna Ismail, a lawyer, at
his "Gaskiya" Chambers, Ikeja, Lagos. He told The Post Express on
Saturday, that "in situation as we now find ourselves, security
becomes a matter of personal responsibility. Whatever a particular
group puts up to guard itself may not be genuinely faulted."
His questions were, "if the OPC, The Ebesu, The Bakkassi, the APC, as
you call them are liquidated, who do the people turn to in periods of
crises? ? Is It the army who have been forced to the barracks or the
police who are busy collecting bribes on the roads?" These questions
are yet to be answered.

Original_Search_Title: Drought and Famine in Africa, The Root Causes
Headline: Africa: Drought and Famine in Africa, The Root Causes
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Feudalism hampered the development of agriculture
before the Derg came to power in Ethiopia.
When the Derg seized power, "military communism", which nationalized
land, made agriculture worse. People hoped that the government which
assumed power after the Derg would improve the situation of rural and
urban land ownership. The present government has not done any better.
It still continues the policy of the Derg. It is a communist at heart
regretting the fact that communism collapsed when it just came to
power after championing communism for more than a decade when it was
waging guerrilla warfare against the Derg.
It is the sole owner of land. Playing the role of the landlords,
which the people of Ethiopia thought they have gotten rid off, it
leases lands at astronomical prices, limits land-holdings to the
extent that the lands which farmers till are too insignificant to
contribute anything to the food surplus of the nation. It kills the
incentive of people who want to engage in essential commercial
farming. And it impoverishes more the already impoverished peasants.
Most of all, it becomes an indirect cause of hunger.
The land lease policy of the current government of Ethiopia, is
indeed, a major factor which contributes to the worsening of famine
in Ethiopia. Unless this policy is abolished altogether, agriculture
and urban life will not get any better. The rationale of the
government for not privatizing land is that it fears the peasants
would sell whatever land they are tilling, to rich landowners and
either become rural proletariat or migrate to the cities to become
jobless beggars. This sort of attitude undermines the intelligence of
the peasants. The government's argument is tantamount to saying that
it knows better than the peasants what is good for them.
The peasants are not that foolish to sell their land (their
livelihood) and abandon the place of their birth, if they could do
well on that land. Since Ethiopian peasants were never given either
in the past or in the present, the fertile lands they needed badly to
improve their livelihood, to think that they would sell those lands
and head for the cities to be more impoverished is a sheer
speculation on the part of the government.
On the contrary, it is when they are denied the land they need
terribly that they are impoverished and migrate to the cities losing
any hope. This has been evidenced by the thousands of peasants who
ran away from the rural areas because of bad land lease and
distribution policy and over-taxation to turn into beggars in cities
like Addis Ababa and Nazareth. On the other hand, if land was
privatized, at least some individuals would have the incentive and
the energy to work hard and create jobs for those poor farmers and
contribute to the surplus of the food of the nation preventing hunger
and stopping the migration of peasants to the cities.
Speaking about Ethiopia, the government is duty-bound to enrich the
nutritional habits of its citizens as well. The absence of proper
nutritional education and diversification shorten the life-span of
Ethiopians and add fuel to the flame of hunger. Ironically enough,
when some Ethiopians say the price of food is soaring or there is
shortage of food, mostly they have in mind teff and meat. They don't
mean cerials, vegetables and fruits which grow wild or which they can
grow in their backyard to ward off hunger. Of course, by lack of
meat, they are not thinking about sea-food or wild animals. I
wouldn't be surprised if some Ethiopians would rather die than eat
prawns, shrimps or crabs, which in the West only the wealthy could
afford.
According to Reuters, a miracle took place in Southern Ethiopia in a
region affected by drought and famine when out of the blue rained
live and some dead fish from the sky, which could be likened to the
dropping of manna and bird meat from the sky to feed the people of
Israel in the wilderness of Sinai. Obviously, the initial reaction of
those religious hungry Ethiopians was not a pleasant one. They just
ran away from it in shock. I wouldn't be surprised if they let all
those fish rot out in the open air instead of storing and eating them
later. Unbalanced diet and non-diversified eating habits should not
be overlooked while considering malnutrition and famine.
I pointed out the problem of drought and famine briefly, but I didn't
discuss the solutions. The solutions are simple. It should be clear
from the outset that relief aid won't solve the problem. From the
donor governments point of view, relief food, instead of relieving
the famished people of Africa or Asia, actually relieves the farmers
of the donor countries from the burden of storing unwanted surplus
food for which they have no market. Instead of burning the excess of
food or dumping it under the sea as it has often occurred in the past
to control price, they would rather buy it from their own farmers and
dump it on the victims of famine. On top of that, they are creating
business for their shipping companies which will have an ideal
opportunity to transport tons of food, thus boosting their profit. If
that was not the case, the donors would have gone straight to the
famine-stricken countries with the money handy and bought food on the
spot saving the time that is wasted on sea and in the air for
transportation. This way, they could have reached the victims quicker
while they are still alive, and they could also have enriched the
local merchants who always have some food in store for sale
regardless of drought and famine.
Furthermore, they would have nourished the people with the local food
they are familiar with, instead of stuffing them with food they are
not used to such as rice and other nutritionally unfriendly grains
which cause pain instead of relief. Though the intention of relief
aid is good when one considers the common folks of the West who give
generously their hard-earned money to help victims of famine, relief
is nothing but relief. It is indeed good as far as alleviating
temporary hunger, though most of the time it doesn't reach the
victims at all, or if does reach them, it is when it is already too
late; and even though it could be used as a political instrument by
inhuman politicians.
In any case, it is not a long lasting solution. It might even be a
problem than a solution since it makes the victims dependent than
self-reliant. It breaks the pride and self-esteem of the victims. It
leaves them at the mercy of others and reduces them into shameless
beggars. So, a long-lasting solution should be found. Instead of
giving a man a fish everyday, as the old saying goes, teach him how
to fish, so that he could be self-reliant.
Frankly, no foreign expert can teach much the indigenous peoples of
Africa when it come to agriculture. On the contrary, the expert can
learn a lot from them, since they have accumulated knowledge,
seasoned and refined over the last few millions of years, until it
was suppressed by colonialism. The solution is in reverting to the
old ways of agriculture, to the time Africans farmed using drought
resistant seeds, applying mixed and alley farming and practicing
rotation system with organic fertilizers in exactly the same manner
as they did before the arrival of the colonialists on the African
continent.
To this end, the old seeds which used to germinate and grow with
little or no rain even in the most arid parts of the world, those
neglected, abandoned and forgotten seeds also known as "life-savers"
should be found and identified. A case in point is Ye-eb, a plant
which yields nuts growing in the arid desert of Ogaden, Ethiopia,
where drought and famine have affected the people at present. This
plant can grow with less than six inches of rain. People in the area
survived for hundreds of years eating the nuts of Ye-eb when food was
scarce. Unfortunately due to civil-wars and social problems,
dislocations, and focus on other crops which are absolute
rain-dependent, this plant has been forgotten and is disappearing.
Had this plant been taken care of and allowed to spread all over
Ogaden instead of drought-sensitive crops such as corn and sorghum,
these people whom foreign relief has not yet reached due to
inaccessibility of the region, would definitely feed on Ye-eb and
avert death. Ye-eb is one of the many African plants which should be
retrieved and planted abundantly in the future to fight famine.
Amaranth is most amazing African plant which has all the vital
vitamins, protein and minerals one needs for one's life. It grows
without any rain; just with early morning moistures. Fortunately,
people in some remote African areas still eat it during famine and
drought and survive with it, whereas those who depended on corn and
other plants which grow with the help of fertilizers and rain, die by
thousands. By the way, Amaranth was the most common food in the
Americas and it is still being enjoyed in Mexico as an indigenous
food. One can find it in Illinois as well. Unfortunately, people in
the US and Europe think it is only a garden flower which is just
beautiful to look at.
A number of good individuals whose hearts burn with the passion to
help the starving people of Africa have researched on indigenous
African plants which grow in arid regions with little or no rain at
all, and come up with a list of interesting crops. The person who
dedicated his life most to find these crops and experiment how to
improve and multiply them was a gentleman called Robert Rodale. He
spent millions of dollars and many years to this end. In his book,
SAVE THREE LIVES, he names these special crops and tells their
incredible story. A few more of these life-saving crops which
Africans should restore and grow more instead of coffee, cotton, nuts
and corns are the African Yam Bean, the Bambara Groundnut, the Desert
Date, the Hausa Potato, the Hungry Rice, the Marama Bean, the
Tamarind, and of course, Ethiopia's staple food, teff.
If I was not bound by time, I would have liked to elaborate on the
botanical property and nutritional value of all these incredible
plants. For now, however, I guess this suffices. Nevertheless, I
would like to state that instead of coffee, cocoa and cotton, if
Africans were allowed and encouraged to grow these indigenous plants,
feed on them and export the surplus, they can generate a huge amount
of hard currency in a mutual and healthy manner thus converting these
plants into cash crops.
Experts tell us that there are at least 2,000 indigenous food plants
lying unappreciated in scattered parts of Africa while millions of
Africans are on the brink of famine and death. Legumes, those ancient
African plants could be revived to play a vital role in fertilizing
the crops naturally by releasing a tremendous amount of free nitrogen
which will save the financial and ecological damage which chemical
fertilizers cause. According to Robert Rodale, if rows of
fast-growing leguminous trees are planted in line with alley-cropping
system leaving some rooms for other crops to grow in between, the
leguminous trees not only will generate free fertilizers, but will
also prevent soil erosion and provide shade for those plants that are
sensitive to excessive heat. Parallel to this, the leguminous trees
could be cut five times a year and grow back right away to solve the
problem of firewood and at the same time, resolving deforestation,
which by itself causes drought and famine. Besides, the leaves of the
leguminous trees will be an ideal source of food for animals. Growing
leguminous trees in the form of alleys indeed serve diverse purposes.
How about tractors? Don't we need them at all? Well, if tractors
contribute to famine and hunger as they have done in the past, we
don't need them. It is better to farm with some animals or even with
hand tools and avert famine and hunger, isn't it? Nontheless, if
tractors or any farming machinery could be built right in Africa
consistently, and African engineers and technicians will be available
to guarantee the durability of these machines, we should go for them.
After I have said all this you might be wondering what the role of
The World Bank is in all of this, and whether it can play any role at
all in the future. It depends on your goal. If your goal is to
exercise your will and power and control governments to achieve your
own agenda whatever it may be, then you may continue to lend money
directly to the governments of the developing nations, knowing full
well that they won't pay off their debts totally, and understanding
that they won't spend the money on projects which will benefit the
masses of people directly.
If your goal is indeed genuine and you really want to help those who
suffer from poverty, hunger and malnutrition, then you should deal
with alternative private organizations which work on grass-root
levels hand-in-hand with the people who need every assistance badly,
advocating the return to the good old ways of African agriculture
which have proven to be successful for millions of years before they
were interrupted by outside forces. If such organizations don't
exist, encourage and promote their creation since you have the voice
and the resources to do so. African farmers are honest and
hard-working. If you help them to travel back on the old path of
agriculture and ecological preservation, they will succeed immensely.
Not only will they be able to feed themselves and solve the
ecological equation, but they will also be able to market the
drought- proof, indigenous African crops and their by-products, and
pay you back your loans. I will be their guarantor!

Original_Search_Title: Ndigbo As Nigeria's Village Fools?
Headline: Nigeria: Ndigbo As Nigeria's Village Fools?
Body_Text:
Lagos — A lot of people were stunned at how quickly the Ohaneze and
the governors of the states in Igboland took to the rostrum to
dissociate themselves from Chief Ralph Uwazuruike and the Movement
for the Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) when
they took their non-violent campaign to the flag-hoisting level.
Some, if not all of us can vouch that all that they said so far is
not beyond the ordinary: That Ndigbo do not have a sense of belonging
in Nigeria. The Ohaneze itself, even some of the specific individuals
who constitute it have said the same thing themselves by calling it
the marginalisation of Ndigbo in Nigeria. In fact, across the Niger,
our neighbours have not hidden what can best be termed their own
resolve to opt out of Nigeria altogether if the existing political
arrangement is left un-addressed. Which explains why they founded the
O'dua People's Congress (OPC). And ever since the OPC stepped into
the mix of things, no right-thinking Yoruba has stepped out to
dissociate him or herself from it in any form or shape. Which is in
fact, the way it ought to be. As a bosom Yoruba friend of mine told
me here recently, the least that a group like the Ohaneze that calls
itself an apex body or any reasonable Igbo could have done about
MASSOB is to maintain an unusual silence while we watch to see how
the kite will swoop down to drag off the dwarf.
Not only that the Ohaneze's quick negative reaction gave me a lot of
cause for wonder, it equally worried me as well. And since the toad
is never known to embark on a race at noon time without a cause, I
was compelled to embark on my own investigation to discern why. Prior
to all that, I was privy to information from some privileged sources
that former dictator, General Abdulsalami Abubakar had made a
clandestine trip to Igboland. That was a little before the flag
hoisting by MASSOB. In fact, my findings indicate that in the course
of that trip, Igboland was not the only southern area that he
visited. The other leg of that same trip took him to northern
Yorubaland where he was sighted at the house of General Olanrewaju. I
found out that while he preferred to visit Igboland under the cover
of darkness, he was not concerned at all being seen in northern
Yorubaland in broad day light. But I'll return to that shortly. Also,
I found out that the former dictator's trips were conceived by
leaders of the Caliphate society. I must quickly mention that
Olanrewaju was one of those who was almost slaughtered by the Abacha
regime in which Abubakar himself held some crucial positions. In view
of that I may not have been the only one who wondered why the former
dictator visited a man who they almost killed in a phantom coup plot.
Well, some of the other things I found out about Abubakar's trip to
Igboland especially about the fact that some of those who he met with
in Igboland are prominent elements of Ohaneze are enough to convince
me that the witch that shrieked in the dead of the night has
something to do with that neighbourhood child who was found dead the
next morning. The other thing is that the present occupant of Aso
Rock is terribly distressed about what the former dictator said in
Igboland. What I tried but could not ascertain yet are these: Whether
the occupant of Aso Rock is intent on discerning who actually sent
the former dictator on those trips. The other thing is what he
intends to do about it. But if you ask me to speculate, I'll quickly
tell you that he probably may be aware of the faceless clique behind
the trip. And that he'll simply be unable to do a thing about it all.
He can only be distressed. My reason? He has refused to appreciate
the underlying dynamics of the goings-on around him. In fact, he has
always been. But back to my main story.
Please, don't fall off your seat because of what you'll hear next.
Abubakar went to Igboland specifically to canvass some of the
so-called Igbo leaders on what he termed the urgent need for Ndigbo
to close ranks with the Hausa-Fulani and gang up against the Yoruba
to save Nigeria from disintegration. That wasn't all. He tried to
take those he canvassed back to history about how Ndigbo had always
ganged up in the past with the Caliphate rulers against the Yoruba.
He referred specifically to the times in the late 1950s, in 1964 and
in 1979. He termed what took place at those times as indications of
age-old friendship between Ndigbo and the Hausa-Fulani. Indeed!
While my guess is that the same mission may have taken him to
northern Yorubaland as well, I can swear in the name of my ancestors
that he was not too concerned about being seen in broad day light in
the home of Olanrewaju. Which tallies with what I found out. Which
says a lot about the mindset of those who sent him out on that
mission. I can tell you that as far as northern Yorubaland is
concerned, they still regard the place as part of their legitimate
domain.
I haven't found out what he received as a response from those he
canvassed in Igboland. But some of those people who he visited with
are in the leadership of the Ohaneze which quickly went to the podium
