against corruption. Two anti-corruption bills are currently with the
state House of Assembly. The bills spell out in details acts considered
as corrupt practices. Without mincing words, Kalu is keeping faith with
his electoral promises. At his inauguration, he promised to make a
difference within his first year in office. No less a person than
Obasanjo who cannot be said to be flippant has commended the Governor.
Same with Obasanjo's deputy, Atiku Abubakar who commissioned Obohia,
Ohunke, Omuma, Osusu, Umule and Ogbor hill roads within the governor's
first 100 days in office.
But what is defining Kalu's tenure more than anything else is his
penchant for stoking controversy. However he is not just controversial
for controversy sake. Rather, it is a product of his conviction and
worldview.
For instance, against the backdrop of the recent Kaduna mayhem where
many Igbos were massacred, Kalu was in the forefront of agitations for
a confederal arrangement. This call came in the wake of his stance that
he could no longer keep quiet if Igbos were being attacked. To many,
the controversial statements were unfortunate but that is vintage Kalu,
who as it seems has become the embodiment and rallying point of the
Igbo spirit. It is to his credit that Kaduna State Governor wooed him
to Kaduna to persuade Igbos to return to the troubled city after the
Sharia riots.
Another controversial act of Kalu was the way and manner he
dissolved his cabinet, shoved aside personal aides, in a move
unprecedented in the history of governance in the country. Although
many railed at him for this action, the truth is that as one with both
the yam and the knife, he was in order, since he has the power to hire
and fire. All in all, Orji Kalu has a focus and is poised to take Abia
to greater heights. However, to do this, it will be in his own interest
and enternal glory if he reconciles all sections of Abia, particularly
that section that feels it is being alienated in the scheme of things
in the state.
This is one governor whose focus has been centred mainly on the
disadvantaged rural dwellers. This pre-occupation informed the emphasis
he has placed right from the outset on agriculture
With a predominantly agrarian populace the state is exploring a
rural transformation through agriculture where it has comparative
advantage. The level of poverty in the state and the desire to
transform it rapidly according to Governor Turaki is being hampered by
the problem of lack of studies on the problems of the state for a
focused programme of action which would be easily sustainable.
One of such schemes which will yield its first fruit by the end of
the year is what could simply be called the sugarcane miracle of
Jigawa. It is a scheme whereby rural farmers are provided with high
yielding disease resistant industrial sugar cane to be planted in farms
clustered together so as to achieve assembly effect. The second reason
is to be able to establish small scale processing industries so that
when the sugarcane is harvested, they will be processed in the factory
built within the location of the farm.
One attraction of the scheme seem to be the satis approach whereby
the biomass of the sugar cane would be used to generate electricity for
the rural community where the processing factory is located.
The calculation is that a family which cultivates a hectare of
sugarcane which research has shown has one of the highest yields per
hectare in the world, will net N250,000.00. A pilot scheme of the
experiment from where it got materials for cultivating the farms proved
98 per cent successful hence the contagious enthusiasm with which it
has been embraced.
On its own, the government launched what it called the Millennium
Village where the sugarcane was planted in large scale with irrigation
facilities provided. This is in addition to mango, date palm of which a
hybrid with high yield that matures in two years has been developed.
Other crops being cultivated already are guava, tamarind, lemon and
another economic tree, the gum Arabic. Most of the crops are to be
targeted at foreign markets to yield foreign exchange for the state.
Governor Turaki has also introduced a novel administrative style in
the state. This is calculated to bring government closer to the people.
While the in-thing is the sitting of ministries and parastatals in
state capitals in other states, Jigawa spreads its own ministries
across the emirates while other ministries were dissolved into
departments and run by professionals. For example, the state ministry
of agriculture in overseen by the deputy governor and it is located in
his own native emirate.
Another innovation introduced by Governor Turaki in Jigawa State is
the Peoples' Forum called the Jigawa Peoples Congress. It serves as a
forum where all grievances, suggestions, and other knotty issues are
resolved or reported. It is also a link between the ruler and the ruled
where all immunity is removed and wrong done either by the governor
against the people or the people against the governor are tabled. It
serves a second function of acting as a liaison between non
governmental organisations and community development based
Organisations which helps to propagate government programmes as well as
protect the interest of the people.
The governor is also vigorously tackling the decay in educational
facilities and standard and to his administration has mapped out
special days, Fridays for members of the executive council to go to the
classrooms to teach the pupils. A more permanent arrangement is the
plan to hire teachers from some countries like India and China at a
cheaper rate to teach the children in both primary and secondary
schools. Again, all teachers in the state are to be retrained to bring
them up to modern standards. Another approach is the area of
remuneration where the state is trying to improve condition of service.
Other schemes in the pipeline include the provision of computer
education with plans to establish an Internet University with
professors from abroad to handle it so that the people of the state
will not lag behind in the world of information systems and technology.
Turaki's dream is to build a beautiful city like Port Harcourt,
small but well planned so that people would want to visit and stay.
Already, a team from Singapore has visited the state to conduct an on
site survey and would be coming back to commence the landscaping of the
state. This is to complement the tourist attraction development of the
state which already has a lot of salespoints in the Baturiya Wetland
birds sanctuary where birds from all over the world gather every
September to November yearly in what foreign tourists have branded 'the
international Birds conference'. There is also the Birninkudu ancient
rock painting said to have been ingrained in the rocks by early cave
men and the proposed Wawan Rafin Resort while Maigatari village, said
to be of importance to location development in tourism will be looked
into.
Just like some states are planning to generate their own power,
Jigawa state apart from calling for them to be linked to the national
grid plans to generate its own power at a cost lower than the current
8-10 cents per kilowatt as some companies have agreed to give them at
five cents although there is a proposal already in which indicates a
supply price of 2 cents if the state can supply its own gas to the
foreign firm that will supply the power.
Major achievements:
Transformation of rural areas; making the people get a feel of
government's activites in their lives.
Drawback:
Political disharmony in the state. Problem of lack of electricity in
several parts of the state.
Rating: Above average
The first six months of the Governor Joshua Chibi Dariye's
administration in Plateau State were basically low keyed especially in
project execution as the regime spent more time streamlining invincible
issues to reposition the work force in line with it's vision of re-
engineering.
Re-engineering, as the secretary to the state government, Ezekiel
will explain, is about refining and adding value to government and this
perhaps explains the retreat, the first of its kind in any state which
governor Dariye and his Executive Council attended for nearly seven
days at the Kent Academy, Miango- Jos.
The first six months were utilised basically used to effect payment
of the N3,000 minimum wage and arrears to workers, payment of
accumulated leave grants of N79 million, re-engaging with UNDP after
the suspicions placed on the state for not honouring its counterpart
funding of programmes of the world body; payment of scholarship
allowances to students as well as the release of N86 million as grants
to the voluntary agency schools. The government also established the
Dangin Contractors Commission along with similar bodies to restore
sanity in the civil service, assess all on- going projects in order to
streamline them into Dariye's re-emerging mission. Shortly after the
retreat at Kent Academy, the governor launched his administration's
projects implementation phase by flagging off the contracts for roads
in each of the three senatorial zones and at present, work has
progressed over fifty percent on the Bokkos/ Barkin Ladi road; Riyom-
Tahoss and Zarmko Ð Madubi roads.
In spite of these strides, the administration has come under
criticism for moving "slowly" and allegedly spending too much on
frequent foreign trips. The governor is also contending with the
problem posed by some power blocks in the state some power bed rocks
particularly old politicians including those close to former chairman
of the PDP, Chief Solomon Lar. Many in this camp believe Dariye "is a
small boy who did not pay much political dues before winning the
governorship". They also complain that the governor is too independent
for their liking especially as he has taken far-reaching decisions
without consulting with them. The recent face-off between Dariye and
Sports Minister Sango many say is an example of such ill-feeling.
An APP stalwart James Shapruk however told Thisday that the
governors trips had begun to yield fruits especially with the recent
decision by a U.S firm to take over and complete the abandoned Jos Rock
International Hotel and also build over 1000 houses for sale to people
of the state. The administration has constructed the Zia bridge in
Dengi, and is building three hospitals in each senatorial zone and
converting them to specialist hospitals. It has renovated 51 secondary
schools with at least three in each LGA; and is building five wells in
each district, a total of 208 wards and has re-awarded the contract for
the construction of the Jos- Zaria main stadium for N1.2 billion out of
which N400 million has been paid as take off sum.
The SSG revealed that to celebrate his one year in office, Governor
Dariye will commission twelve projects among which are the Jos Township
stadium, the multi-million Naira indoor theater which the government
completed and the Dadrin Kowa Youth center.
Even where it appears the regime has fared well in the past twelve
months, the issue of water supply and petroleum seem to be hard nuts to
crack, the multi-billion naira greater Bukuru-Jos ADB assisted water
scheme remains a major headache just as government appears to have run
out of ideas on how to solve the problem of acute fuel shortage which
is affecting socio-economic life as well as agricultural development.
The story of the Rivers state government in the last one year, has
uniquely posted some positive entries on the balance sheet at the end
of the first "trading season".
Governor Peter Odili of Rivers state has carved a niche for himself
in the way he has carried on with the business of governance.
Specifically, he has touched the lives of his people in several areas
like:
EDUCATION
Dr Odili has embarked on the renovation of several dilapidated
school buildings. He has also ensured the prompt payment of teachers
within the state. But his critics point out that he has concentrated
his efforts on schools within Port-Harcourt city alone.
HEALTH
Odili has approved free medical health services for school children
of age six and below as well as adults above 65 years of age. But not
much haas been said about stocking the government hospitals with drugs
and relevant equipment.
TRANSPORT
Perhaps it is in the area of transport that Odili has made the most
remarkable impact. He has affected all the means of transport in the
state. He provided 25 school buses for school children to carry them to
and from their schools free of charge. This is novel and unchallenged
in the nation.
More than that, the Odili Government has also provided train
services for the state's civil servants. This has been hailed as it has
reduced the gross cost of transport expenses of civil servants. It is
said that about five thousand passengers patronise the train on a daily
basis. The people in the riverine area of the state are not left out.
About ten out-board engines(ferries) have been provided to also service
their interest.
POWER GENERATION
In this area, Odili has also shown some good initiative, albeit
quietly. Recently, the state government also acquired some high-powered
generators to generate electricity in some communities. This provides
the basis for future privatisation scheme.
MICRO-CREDIT SCHEME
This is another area the state has provided the lead for others to
follow. The state already has some co-operative schemes which are aimed
at empowering the people economically. It is not certain how many
persons are already benefiting from this scheme, but that there is such
a scheme is on its own salutary.
HOUSING
Governor Odili at his inauguration had promised that within his
first 100 days in office, he would build one thousand housing units.
One year after, he has not delivered on this promise. He has however
initiated some housing programmes but the affordability to the masses
is another matter.
PEACE MANAGEMENT
Rivers state is a volatile state with several rounds of violence. It
is a veritable source of social unrest. Odili's capacity to rule has
been tried severally on account of the social upheavals. At one time or
the other, several communities have gone up in arms against one another
or even amongst themselves. Some of them include the Choba rage with
Wilbros resulting in the controversial rape story.
Similarly, the Okirika and Eleme war indeed upset the peace of the
state s several lives and property were lost.
And then again, there is the recurrent Ogoni uprising which has
resulted in several deaths too. But in all of these, Odili has managed
to contain the riotous tendency of the Rivers state youths.
Odili, a medical doctor has shown good sociological grasp in the
understanding of the Rivers people.
As he once noted, "peace is the fundamental platform on which any
development can occur. We appreciate this from the start and then we
first of all had to understand the sociological factors that can
militate against peace. And then ensure you keep your eye on them."
He has also demonstrated being a good politician by working to get
the headquarters of the proposed NDDC located in Port-Harcourt, against
the better favoured Yenagoa in Bayelsa state.
Major Achievement:
Provision of some basic infrastructure
Drawback:
Inability to deliver on all promises made.
Rating:
Above average.
As a state, the greatest handicap of Ekiti is funds. The state does
not seem to have enough to meet its obligations to its people and that
is the problem the governor of the state. Otunba Adeniyi Adebayo, has
had to face in the last twelve months. Apart from allocation from the
federation account, the state cannot boast of any meaningful source of
income. Nevertheless, the governor has been trying to justify his
election as the state's chief executive.
In a state where education is viewed as a veritable industry, the
governor seems to have scored a bull's eye when he decided to pay fifty
percent of the examination fees of all the students of the state's
origin sitting for the on-going West Africa Examinations Council, WAEC,
examinations. Many saw this as very good step on the part of the state
governor, especially in a state where the poverty level is very high.
This enabled many students to be able to sit for the exams. In the same
vein, the governor also supplied computers to some secondary schools in
the state. According to him, this measure was meant to aid computer
literacy in secondary schools in the state. For Adebayo, his priority
seems to be education. The state university, University of Ado-Ekiti
too has benefited from this direction. He is set complete another set
of hostels in addition to the one he had built as accommodation is the
greatest problem facing the students of the university. Above all,
right now, secondary students in the state do not pay school fees
again.
Another area where Adebayo did well is in the area of the state-
owned television station. The state's last military administrator,
Atanda Yusuf, a retired Navy Captain, had commissioned the station even
when there were not enough equipment to transmit signal from the
station. Adebayo had to close it down, re-award some contracts and now,
the station is functioning.
Despite paucity of funds, the governor has been trying to do
something in the area of health. Many health centres have been upgraded
to general hospital in an apparent move to make health care delivery
available to the people. These hospitals have in turn been supplied
with drugs.
It may not exactly be a plus for the Iyin-Ekiti-born lawyer, but
taking into consideration the situation in other states, the governor
deserves kudos for being one of the few governors who have maintained a
good relationship with their deputies since they assumed office almost
a year ago. His deputy, Mr. Paul Alabi, has been having it smooth with
his boss. Though some have attributed this to the difference in the
ages (the deputy is much more older), many will however give it to the
governor for his maturity and his ability to carry his number two man
along. In the same vein, the state has been relatively peaceful in the
last one year in a country where many states have had more than their
own fair share of crisis. In fact, it is a credit to Adebayo that there
has never been a single incident of communal clash since he assumed
office.
It has, however, not been song of victory all the way for the son of
retired General Adeyinka Adebayo. He has not been able to effectively
tacke the menace which bad raods constitute in the state. Coming from
Osun State, after Ijebu-Jesa, the smooth road terminates at Itawure and
the dancing begins. The road is still as it was long ago and there is
no sign it will change. And it is like that in most parts of the state.
Apart from this, siting of the Federal Government Girls College in the
state has almost become a battle of supremacy between him and education
Minister, Tunde Adeniran. Adebayo prefers Aramoko while Adeniran
prefers Efon Alaaye. Now the problem has not been resolved with the two
communities laying claim to the school.
Also the governor has not been able to shake off the toga of being a
socialite who will not bat an eyelid in leaving state matters and
coming to Lagos to attend parties.
Adebayo also kept tongues wagging because of his loyalty to the
former deputy governor of the old Ondo State, Nathaniel Aina, and also
an Alliance for Democracy, AD, chieftain in the state. Apart from being
the party boss, his son was given a plum government job and above all,
Aina was named the chairman of the Governing Council of the state
university. He has since declined because of the criticism that
followed the apparent lopsided appointments.
Major achievement:
In the area of education.
Drawback:
His apparent failure to grasp that apart from education, he has to
graple with other area as well
Rating:
Average

Original_Search_Title: HIV And The Abala Cure
Headline: Nigeria: HIV And The Abala Cure
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joseph Abalaka, M.D. has been in the news in recent times.
is much-trailed therapy for the cure of persons ailing from the Human
Immuno-deficiency Virus (HIV)/ Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome
(AIDS) has split public opinion down the middle.
In the event, very little about the debate is clarified by the
advocacy of the contending parties.
The Health minister's eagerness to resign if anything of substance
comes off Abalaka's claim is as unhelpful as the latter's professed
disposition to inject himself with the dreaded virus in a bid to prove
the value of his cure. In as far as Dr.
Abalaka claims for his therapy, the ability to reverse an HIV
sufferer's sero-prevalence, it ranks alongside the combination drug
therapies, based on protease inhibitors, that are currently popular in
the west. Thus far, it scarcely extends the frontiers of scientific
knowledge.
But it promises a great deal at another level: it challenges all
known therapies on price. Whereas current anti-HIV remedies cost
upwards of $12000 (about N1.2million) per annum, Dr. Abalaka claims for
his therapy the humble cost of N25,000 These obviously are the details
that matter.
For if this claim were true, it bespeaks some advance in the
compounding of drugs. Lamentably, the contrasting declarations made for
and against the likelihood of Dr. Abalaka having chanced on a nostrum
of such powerful import overshadow the central issues.
More than any other, both the ministry of Health and the claimant
should know that procedures exist for substantiating the veracity of
such claims. Indeed, a couple of years back, the countries of the OECD
agreed on common standards for conducting the clinical trials on which
these procedures are based.
Doubtless, such procedures have been expedited in the past
­especially for some of the present anti-HIV treatments. But it is
essential that Dr.
Abalaka's claim demonstrates fidelity to this process in order that
it be taken seriously. For starters, he ought to file a patent
application (describing both the idea behind his treatment and the
method for working it). It is then expected that pre-clinicals -
tests on laboratory animals­to ascertain the drug's toxicity (how much
collateral harm it does to the recipient) and bioavailability (how well
it is absorbed and delivered to the point of need) are conducted and
recorded. Phase two and phase three trials (testing on humans)
follow from this.
The basic requirements for these ­large numbers of HIV-infected
people, and a fair network of relatively sophisticated teaching
hospitals and related medical facilities­are already in place.
Fundamentally, the task is to determine the category of patients to
administer the drug to, when and how, while all the time ensuring that
a statistically reliable result will be obtained therefrom.
Conducting these tests and arranging the result in a way that could
convince regulatory authorities to give the necessary approval for the
commercial development of the drug demands that it be given to a great
number of people who suffer from the disease the medication is designed
to cure. Similarly, some or any of the existing HIV intervention
therapies must be administered on an equal number of persons.
This latter set must not be aware of the fact that they are not
benefiting from the new drugs. It is for this reason that the search
for a commercial development of a new drug is a very costly process.
And about a third of this cost, which is put at around $500 million by
the leading pharmaceutical companies, is accounted for by clinical
trials.
Which is where the government comes in. It is improbable that Dr.
Abalaka can muster anything like the sums needed to go through this
process unaided. Thus once he demonstrates himself amenable to the
idea, it behoves the federal authorities to help speed things up, to
lend financial assistance and to guard the patent on this jealously.
Sadly, this prospect is soured by two traits local to the Nigerian.
The most off-putting is the penchant for not taking ourselves
seriously. On the other hand, those in charge of our healthcare system
have not handled the matter with the urgency it deserves. No one is
saying that Abalaka should be given a carte blanche.
But for crying out loud, a desperate disease calls for a desperate
cure. The danger ultimately is not that Abalaka might turn out to be a
quack.
Actually, this would give a rather desirable outcome. It does not
only settle the matter one way, it would be of immense relief to those
who argue that the euphoria over Dr. Abalaka's claim will deflect from
the larger message of the anti-AIDS campaign.
The possibility instead, if at all there is anything to Dr.
Abalaka's claim is that as it happened with the yam-pounder concept,
this idea might yet see better days outside this shores.
Publication Date: May 11, 2000

Headline: Africa: HIV And The Abalaka Cure

Original_Search_Title: End The HIV/AIDS Cure Controversy
Headline: Africa: End The HIV/AIDS Cure Controversy
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is about time that the claims by Dr. Abalaka that he has
developed an effective vaccine for reversing HIV infection be scientifically
ascertained and the raging controversy laid to rest in the overall interest of
the nation.
Claims on the discovery of cures for HIV and full blown AIDS are not strange
in Nigeria. Unconstrained by the rigid rules of scientific proof required of
orthodox western trained doctors and unaffected by their code of ethics that
punishes advertising with deregistration and expulsion by the Nigerian Medical
authorities, every roadside herbalist claims a cure for not only HIV but full
blown AIDS. Unfortunately the media, driven by the need to generate revenue,
have been less circumspect in airing such unverifiable and unverified claims.
Thus when Dr. Abalaka came on the scene his claims could not but evoke doubts.
But Dr. Abalaka is not another roadside herbalist opportunistically
exploiting the AIDS scourge to fleece off the public. He is a trained medical
doctor registered to practice in Nigeria. Thus, his claims to have developed an
effective vaccine against HIV infection does not fall into the hocus pocus
category of the herbalists. Besides, the corroboration of the effectiveness of
the ABALAKA VACCINE by very senior military officers who had sent soldiers
certified to have tested positive to HIV during their peace-keeping operations
for treatment which they claim had been successful, raises the level of
seriousness of the claims. In contrast, however, the Federal Minister of
Health, another trained medical doctor has emotionally refuted Abalaka's claims
and the mandarins in the Health Ministry have been generally sticking to the
official script that Dr. Abalaka's claims are frivolous.
It should be recalled that this is not the first time a Nigerian makes a
break-through in science or technology on the home turf only to be denied and
defamed by his colleagues and ignored by the government. It is on record that
in the mid-70s one student Fagoyinbo of The University of Ife (now Obafemi
Awolowo University) invented a yam pounding machine which was used to produce
good quality pounded yam for visiting General Obasanjo the then military Head
of State. There were encomiums and songs of praise. But there it all ended
until the Japanese smuggled out the technology and produced an improved version
of the machine which were massively imported into Nigeria and paid for in
foreign exchange. The same ineptitude and prodigal neglect were exhibited over
the first successful Nigerian test tube births and the invention of an
effective cholera vaccine by Nigerians in the private sector. This raises the
suspicion that professional rivalry borne out of the fact that those who
receive official funding only hold seminars and talk shows while those without
such motivations produce results. It could be surmised therefore that the
policy of one's rival never killing a full-grown game might be at work in these
matters.
A continuation of this trend is undesirable for the nation. It weakens the
intellectual development of the country, deepens the nations embarrassing
dependence on foreign scientific products and technological breakthroughs,
devalues the African image in the world and contributes largely to our poverty
in the midst of plenty. An effective made-in-Nigeria HIV vaccine, for instance,
could off set our national debt in a few years, beyond making Dr. Abalaka rich.
It is said that so far, no arm of government has shown special interest in this
matter. We therefore, call on Mr. President to first facilitate the patenting
of Abalaka's vaccine and second set up a high powered panel of neutral experts
to evaluate the claims on HIV vaccine. The on-going controversy must end
soonest and it must not become another yam-pounding machine experience.

Original_Search_Title: Workers Nationwide Mark May Day
Headline: Africa: Workers Nationwide Mark May Day
Body_Text:
Lagos — Workers nationwide will mark today's May Day amid expectation that
the Federal Government will announce a new minimum wage.
President Olusegun Obasanjo is expected to address workers at a rally by the
Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC) at the Tafawa Balewa Square in Lagos. Also
expected at the event are the Senate President, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, the Speaker
of the House of Representatives, Alhaji Ghali Na'Abba and Lagos State Governor,
Senator Ahmed Bola Tinubu.
It is expected that Obasanjo may use the occasion to announce a new enhanced
salary package for civil servants nationwide.
Speaking on a new salary package for workers yesterday, an NLC official
called for a review of the 1981 legislation pegging worker's minimum wage at
N250.
Mr. Chidi Nnorom, who is the Chairman of the National Union of Air Transport
Employees (NUATE) said in Lagos, shortly before a meeting of the NLC's Central
Working Committee (CWC) that the new minimum wage would serve as a benchmark
for future hikes.
Amid speculations of a raise of the minimum wage to N7,500 and allegations
of a sell-out by labour, Nnorom said whatever was agreed upon now would not
debar further negotiations. He said that the new rates, which would be backed
by laws, would serve only as benchmarks for unionized workers.
"When you talk of minimum wage, it is meant to protect only unionized people
and serve as a guide to individual unions in their negotiations with their
employers," he added.
On the N7,500 minimum wage being touted, Nnorom said he was not aware that
any amount had been fixed, saying that the NLC's CWC was meeting to get briefs
from its negotiating committee on the matter ahead of today's May Day
celebrations.
He urged workers to exercise restraint on their clamour for jumbo wages and
enjoined them not to allow their ranks to be broken.
The labour leader also urged extreme tact "in dealing with the person who
has the knife and the yam", noting that apart from increased wages, workers
needed an improved transportation system, housing and social services. On his
expectations today, Nnorom said" "it would be a day for sober reflections and a
day to remember our colleagues in the struggle."
In his message, former presidential aspirant, Alhaji M. D. Yusuf warned the
political class that unless the pervading poverty among the citizenry is
urgently stemmed, more Nigerians will be disillusioned with civilian rule - a
situation which, he warned, may trigger violent ethnic and religious conflicts.
Yusuf also berated the civilian administration for the inability of the
legislative arm of government in some states and at the Federal level to work
for the overall good of the populace one year after being elected into office.
He said though today is "historically a workers' day, May Day is also for
national reflection.
"There is no doubt that the last twelve months have been a trying period for
our political leaders. In this context, people can understand that many
mistakes will be made both from the side of the executive and the legislature
in the first few months of civilian rule.
"But a situation where for one year, the executive and legislative arms of
government, in some states at the Federal level have not developed the capacity
and the political will to work together for the well-being of the people of
Nigeria, is to say the least, disappointing. The situation becomes even
shameful when we consider the fact that both the executive and the legislative
arms of government are largely under the control of a single party, the Peoples
Democratic Party, PDP", he stated.
Absolving the PDP of the blame, he noted that the party has entered into an
alliance with the two "opposition" parties - the All Peoples' Party, APP and
the Alliance for Democracy, AD to facilitate the smooth working relationship
between the executive and legislative arms of government.
Yusufu however regretted that the two arms have failed to agree to work
together. He said the government has, for instance launched a Poverty
Alleviation Programme but that there was nothing on the ground to indicate that
it is taking off several months after.
"As the poverty of the majority of the people get worse, there is an attempt
to divert attention from this shameful paradox of a very rich country with very
poor citizens, whose rulers are receiving larger and larger amounts of revenue
which can be used to improve the living conditions.
"The three registered political parties in control of the executive and
legislature are jointly responsible for the situation we are in. The party
leaders and their elected and appointed public officials do not show any
serious concern about these terrible conditions of the citizens of this
country", Yusufu observed.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Waiting For Bill Clinton
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Waiting For Bill Clinton
Body_Text:
Lagos — I understand that while Bill Clinton was asked on CNN who were his
ten best friends among world leaders or those he most admired amongst the
various Heads of State he had met in almost eight years as the President of the
most powerful nation in the world, many were taken by surprise when he named
Nigeria's President Olusegun Obasanjo - as number eight. He quickly added:
"Perhaps I should include my buddy Jesse Jackson since he clearly believes
that he is the real president of the United States of America or at least a
section of it - Black America."
Surprise, surprise Tony Blair, Prime Minister of Britain did not make it
largely because he is becoming more and more "presidential" than "prime
ministrial". By way of digression, the rumour mill in the White House had it
that the reason most Americans - both white and black were horrified at the
prospects of Jesse Jackson's presidential bid eight years ago was that the CIA
put word around that Jesse was planning to introduce Sharia law into America !!
Anyway what is far more relevant is that this is not the first time that
General Obasanjo has been able to forge a close personal relationship with the
President of the U.S. Back in 1978 when the General was the military Head of
State of Nigeria, Jimmy Carter who was then the President of the U.S. came on a
State Visit to Nigeria. The two Heads of State quickly became friends and it
was a friendship that would stand General Obasanjo in good stead during his
darkest hours as an inmate in General Sani Abacha's dungeon. While he was
incarcerated for three years Jimmy Carter was a tireless campaigner for his
release. Even before them, while General Obasanjo was being tried (and
condemned for coup plotting) it was the exertions and intervention of Bill
Clinton, at the prodding of Jimmy Carter that saved Obasanjo from the gallows.
Abacha wanted him dead.
Anyway, back to Carter's visit the serious aspect of it was his offer to
have American companies [led by Mobil] build Nigeria's LNG plant, run it for
twenty-five years and then hand it back to Nigeria. For Carter, the political
pay off was that it would get the American companies, which he had prohibited
(by Presidential fiat) from building the trans-Siberia gas pipeline in the then
Soviet Union, off his back. Most, if not all of them, would get a piece of the
action in Nigeria. The package would cost Nigeria nothing. Indeed, the deal
looked too good to be true; and it was precisely for that reason that Obasanjo
turned it down !!
On the lighter side, one of the spin-offs from that visit was the swiftness
with which Carter and Obasanjo established a rapport based on warm personal
relationship, good humour, simplicity and a world view anchored on the prism of
high moral principles. This was at a time when Obasanjo preached the gospel of
"low profile" (it was home made Peugeot cars for all government officials; no
"First Lady" and no alcohol at government functions) while Carter insisted that
governance in America and moral uprightness were not incompatible. At any rate
while Nigerian officials were agonising over whether the food to be served
should be "Nigerian" or continental", General Obasanjo jokingly suggested that
it should be pounded yam and egusi for everybody. Eventually, a compromise was
worked out - those on the high table would have a choice of pounded yam or
imported Texas steak. Somehow, the ding-dong leaked to the CIA which in turn
whispered to Carter to beware of Nigeria and strange food. However, as the
banquet progressed Carter did not merely taste the pounded yam and egusi as a
demonstration of friendship and goodwill, he sportingly ate it with great
relish. His reward was a spontaneous standing ovation from the other guests.
Their verdict was: what a great man; yet what a man of great simplicity. He was
the first American President to publicly admit to committing adultery "in my
heart by looking." (Bill would go one better; or a hell of a lot worse!!).
The story did not end there. When a few months later it was General
Obasanjo's turn to return the visit - with a state visit to the U.S. a
sumptuous dinner was laid on by the White House. When the main course was
served, (guess what) it turned out to be pounded yam with egusi!! Obasanjo was
overwhelmed. He departed entirely from his script and commenced his speech with
that famous line:
"Carter, omo kata la rhyme with Carter"
which in Yoruba language roughly translates as.
"Carter, you lovable and charming rascal - you are so thoughtful and
endearing"
For Bill Clinton's visit which was originally scheduled for March but is now
postponed to June, Obasanjo does not want any surprises. Indeed, even within
the White House there is considerable consternation that Bill Clinton is
determined to make the trip regardless of the negative security reports by the
CIA about the situation in Nigeria. Most of it was triggered off by the Sharia
riots in Kaduna and other parts of Nigeria. Besides, some are cautioning that
it is much too early for Obasanjo to claim the triumph of democracy. Indeed,
the more cynical take the sanguine view:
"Nigeria - nothing has changed. They simply changed the actors. Sadly, some
of them have not even bothered to read the democracy script."
The old guard within the White House are very wary. They suspect that
Obasanjo may seek to embarrass the American President by raising what they
consider to be old chestnuts (repatriation by a different name) - the debt
issue and the peace dividend.
America's dilemma is self evident. It wants to take Africa seriously but the
image of Africa, particularly Nigeria, in the United States is "black" - as in
thoroughly negative - instability, crime, violence, rapacious leaders, greedy
politicians, crooks in power, looting of the treasury, "419" scams (advance fee
fraud), arbitrary arrests, chaos; corruption, cannibalism; mismanagement of the
economy, entirely wrong priorities and anarchy. All these may well be the
consequences of long standing prejudices and self-fulfiling prophecy. However,
we cannot afford to ignore the sequence of events as relayed by CNN. In early
January, President Clinton was on CNN declaring that Africa was right at the
top of his agenda for year 2000 and that he was committing February to Africa,
as "Africa month". However, on the same CNN only yesterday the American
Secretary of State Madeline Albright (who could conceivably become the next
president of the Republic of Czechoslovakia where she was born) was on CNN but
she was more guarded. In response to enquiries about the Africa initiative and
programme, she was somewhat coy. All she would volunteer was: "When we say
Africa, we are actually talking about four countries - South Africa, Nigeria,
Egypt and Morocco " CNN's Jim Clancey tried to pin her down regarding the
robustness of democracy in Africa, particularly Nigeria. She paused, chose her
word carefully and with considerable restraint, almost reluctance, volunteered
the verdict: Fragile." The interviewer wanted a more explicit response but
being a seasoned diplomat she skillfully steered the discussion away to some
other subject.
Regardless, Bill is determined to come.
Bill Clinton's enthusiasm for Africa is a wonderful tonic and compensation
for - (or anti-thesis of) the thesis of British historian Professor Trevor
Roper (Oxford and Cambridge University):
"Nothing good can come out of Africa".
Originally, Clinton's itinerary would have taken him to Sokoto (where he was
very much looking forward to watching the exotic durbar); Port Harcourt; Lagos
and Abuja. By the look of things, Bill's visit will be confined to Abuja and he
may not even spend the night - for security reasons. It is unthinkable that the
President of the United States of America would come to Nigeria and not visit
Lagos. Be warned. This is one more reason why Lagos has had enough of Nigeria.
Nigeria is selfish !!
Nigeria has conveniently chosen to forget that many of the black guys who
used to hang out with Bill about three decades ago in the U.S. are now back
home in Lagos. They are planning a huge party for him where he will play the
saxophone - just like the good old days. Back then, Bill's thing or party trick
was to play the saxophone blindfolded. He even became adept at dancing the
watusi - and the ultimate accolade - he could outdance any black guy in limbo
dancing.
The consensus in Lagos is that we have accommodated Nigeria for thirty
years, let us try giving each other some space for a few years. After that, we
may well decide to get together again. One thing is clear, if Nigeria is not
willing to share Clinton with Lagos, Lagos will issue its own separate
invitation to Bill, Hillary, Chelsea and Monica - yes Monica. We have always
been liberal in Lagos. It is part of our tradition to mind our own business and
that does not include preaching to anybody about their domestic or travelling
arrangements.
Let us be clear about one thing. Not all Americans are enchanted about what
they have cynically tagged "Clinton's Africa Safari" - an adventure in wild
terrain. Some are even more sarcastic by demanding:
"Bill, who pays the bill for these trips?"
Indeed, there was an uproar in the Senate over the hefty bill ($41 million)
for his four nation trip to Africa - Senegal, Ghana, South Africa and Botswana
six months ago.
All these have put Bill Clinton on the defensive and he has had to issue a
public statement to the effect that Africa is (and Africans are) very close to
his heart. He has made it abundantly clear that he is not coming to Nigeria
just for friendship sake on account of his warm personal relationship with
President Olusegun Obasanjo. Certainly, not. It is a business trip. No; that is
not the right language. He means that he means business. He has identified
Nigeria as a country that America and Americans can do business with. That is
why hundreds of American businessmen - major players - will accompany him. They
are looking principally at the oil and gas sector; power and steel; and
telecommunications.
At the behest of President Obasanjo, agriculture just scraped on to the
list. Not only is Nigeria Africa's most populous country, every fifth person in
the black diasporas is a Nigerian. For good measure, Nigeria has enormous
resources which if properly managed could wipe out poverty in one generation.
Even more fundamental is the angle which the White House spin doctors are
pursuing with regard to the forthcoming visit of the American President to
Nigeria. It is just possible (for security reasons the precise date is still
under wraps) that he will be here on 12th June - the anniversary of the 1993
aborted Nigerian presidential election. How time flies !! The mind boggles at
the thought that had Bashorun M.K.O. Abiola, the winner of the election been
allowed to rule he would not only have completed his first term in office, he
would have only one year left of his second term.
At any rate, the story doing the rounds amongst the White House press corps
is that Bill Clinton is coming on an "audit mission" to Nigeria - to check out,
at first hand how democracy is faring in Nigeria. The idea is to checkmate
President Olusegun Obasanjo's incessant demand for a "democracy dividend". Bill
Clinton's line is that before you can declare a dividend you must get a
reputable firm of auditors (such as KPMG, Chartered Accountants) to look at the
books and if there are more credits than debits in layman's language we can
declare a "profit" which would provide the wherewithal to declare a dividend.
Bill Clinton is well qualified to be the auditor-in-chief of democracy. The
only other serious contender is the Prime Minister of India whose country is
the largest democracy in the world. The only trouble is that nobody can
remember his name. Indeed, the general assumption is that the Prime
Ministership is still firmly within the Ghandi family. Hence, Mrs. Sonia Ghandi
though Italian by birth still gets huge sacks of mail addressed to "Mrs.
Ghandi, Prime Minister of India".
Just to be sure that he is not caught napping, General Obasanjo has planned
for the Clinton visit with military precision. Old habits die hard. He has even
borrowed from the philosophy of General Sun Tzu, the Chinese war strategist who
several centuries ago observed:
"(1) Know thyself and know thy enemy and you will win 100% of all wars.
(2) Know thyself and not the enemy and you will win 50% of all wars.
(3) Know not thyself nor the enemy and you will lose all wars."
This is not to say that Clinton is perceived as an enemy. It just means he
is not to be taken for granted.
Two other factors have crept into the equation. President Obasanjo's
favourite quotation from William Shakespeare is:
"June cannot know the glory that went with May."
It is both symbolic and profound - as a reminder of Nigeria's past glory
that had been utterly destroyed long before Obasanjo was sworn in as President
of Nigeria on 29th May 1999. Even more poignantly, the President has chosen to
ignore Senator (Chief) Arthur Nzeribe's dire warning that he may not still be
the President of Nigeria by the time Bill Clinton comes calling in June.
Headline: "OBASANJO: BEWARE The IDES OF MAY" (Nzeribe)
"For the purpose of this thesis, March extends to May 29, 2000. I foresee
many stormy seas ahead for President Olusegun Obasanjo. The problems will
become so complicated that they will result in his Presidency terminating,
democratically, before its first anniversary. Obasanjo's problems are multi-
faceted but four major issues will sound the final death drum for his
administration.
(a) 2000 Appropriation Bill
(b) The Anti-Corruption Bill
(c) The Niger-Delta Development Commission Bill and the mother of them all
(d) The Sharia matter.
Obasanjo's resignation is the only alternative to chaos, catastrophe and
disintegration. Does he really wish Nigeria these calamities ? It is wrong to
argue that it is too early. Nine months is too long a time to appreciate that
the man is not capable of changing. The job is just too big for him to handle
and we are drifting, perhaps merely hoping for a divine intervention."
It is against the background of such formidable enemies, both overt and
covert, that General Obasanjo chose the theme for his defence strategy:
"A Race Against Time - No Time To Lose" (But Everything to Lose) Obasanjo
has taken stock and realised that the expectation that he would enjoy at least
one year's honeymoon was totally misplaced. Almost from day one, it has been
turbulence all the way - without respite. It is now self-evident that it is
because the scale of the damage that had been inflicted on the economy or the
assault on the body politic had been grossly underestimated. The problem is
that with almost a year gone, matters could drag on for the next three years
without the kind of dramatic change on which President Obasanjo has nailed his
mast. Suddenly, it will be time for the next election. By then, the chicken
would have come home to roost. The likely outcome offers a vast variety of
permutations and some of them could be extremely unpleasant - even ominous.
*To be continued
At the personal level, Obasanjo has every reason to be wary of 29th May. On
that date in 1966 he came pretty close to being a victim of the outbreak of
mutiny in the army which eventually metamorphosed into a counter coup that
installed General Yakubu Gowon as Nigeria's Head of State on 29th July 1966 at
the age of only thirty-two years. The then Major Obasanjo was only twenty-nine
years old. Ever since, May has consistently been a troublesome month for
Obasanjo. However, 1999 offered a remarkable departure. He was sworn in as
Nigeria's Head of State (for the second time) on 29th May. Ironically, there
were awesome predictions that he would drop dead even before the swearing-in
ceremony. Thereafter, the sorcerers predicted he would not last a month; then
it was six months and ever since it has become a rolling plan ! Hence, for him
to have survived this long is no small achievement regardless of the landmines
just waiting to explode right under his feet.
Therefore, in preparing for his first anniversary, President Obasanjo has
dusted up his military manuals and adopted the tactics of "defending with
surprise attack". Additionally, rather than celebrate his first year in office
on 29th May 200O,
He shifted the date to 29th March. Amazingly, the Nigerian press who pride
themselves on being vibrant and eagle-eyed missed the event entirely. It was a
well crafted decoy and to further daze those who were planning to ambush him,
he devoted the celebration to his most virulent critics and unrelenting
detractors. He invited them all to the celebration party at Campos Square,
Lagos which is within shouting distance of our family house in the centre of
Lagos. For his real friends and admirers, the President has reserved Saturday
29th April when his son Gbenga will get married at the Obasanjo Farm, Otta. As
for the 29th of May, the President has reserved that date for the unrepentant
sycophants who will book lavish newspaper adverts and television spots hailing
the President as the new Messiah (regardless of his protestations that he is
not).
As part of the celebration of his first year in office, the President
devoted Sunday 23rd April to the opening of the new chapel in the Presidential
Villa, Aso Rock. The construction of the chapel started in earnest in January.
Although it is meant to serve as a place of worship for the President, members
of his family and his staff, it can comfortably accommodate three hundred
people.
In his opening address, Reverend [Professor] Yusufu Obaje, President of the
Nigerian Baptist Theological Seminary, Ogbomosho and Chaplain of Aso Rock
disclosed that the decision to build a chapel within the villa was informed by
the President's desire to give priority and glory to God and to have a place to
worship.
"The need for a suitable place of worship was obvious, considering the fact
that the former Heads of State who had resided in the villa were Moslems.
Therefore, there was no need for a church even though there are three mosques
within the Aso Rock complex."
It was a long service but the President was in a relaxed and joyous mood.
His mien spoke volumes. It was a sharp contrast to his swearing ceremony during
which he appeared agitated and irritable.
In his closing sermon, the chaplain seemed inspired when he declared:
"God needs a man who has been tried and tested, a man who has been in
prison, and in bondage to carry on his work in Nigeria. President Mathew Aremu
Okikiola Olusegun Obasanjo is the man whom God has called to lay his foundation
in Nigeria."
The chaplain chose the word "foundation" carefully. He made no reference to
the pillars nor the roof. Perhaps that is all President Obasanjo can hope to
achieve - the foundation for a prosperous and united Nigeria. Everything else
is a bonus.
The President was beaming as he emerged from the Church which was
overflowing. Far too many people had turned up but General Obasanjo seemed to
be happy to see them all. He was fulsome in his apologies as he went out of his
way to shake hands and embrace his guests. Someone who looked very much like
Professor Adebayo Adedeji, the former Minister of Economic Development and
former Secretary- General of the Economic Commission for Africa was directly
behind General Obasanjo as he emerged from the Church. Someone standing behind
me whispered that unknown to most Nigerians, Professor Adedeji was General
Babangida's first choice (with the support of General Obasanjo) to head the
Interim National Government. It was when he insisted on having a free hand as
regards the choice of members of his cabinet that the job went to Chief Ernest
Shonekan. All that is now history anyway. Incidentally, the same Professor
Adedeji was a hot candidate for the Secretary-General of the United Nations -
well ahead in the scoring, before the job eventually went to the much less
fancied Kofi
Annan of Ghana. This was the same job that General
Obasanjo wanted and his candidature was vigorously supported by General
Ibrahim Babangida. Now history has gone full circle and right in the centre of
it all is none other than General Olusegun Obasanjo.
The cheers for the President as he emerged from the Church were deafening.
He was overwhelmed and for the benefit of those who could not get inside the
Church, he cheerfully gave a repeat performance of the dancing he had done so
majestically inside the Church, accompanied by his wife, family and friends and
the rousing song:
"Mio se ni jo ?
Oluwa da erin si ayo mi Mio se ni jo"
This translates as: "Why will I not dance when it has pleased the Almighty
to mingle laughter and joy with his blessings for me ?"
It was moving; it was infectious; it was electric and awesome. Soon, the
entire crowd had joined in the dancing.
Aso Rock had never seen anything like it. Christians and Moslems were
holding hands and singing the same tune.
"Mio se ni jo ?"
Trust the press to ruin matters by asking the President awkward questions
such as:
"Mr. President are you going to make any changes in your cabinet and put
round pegs in round holes."
General Olusegun Obasanjo was as nimble as ever. His cool reply was:
"Of course, I am going to make changes. Chief Yomi EDU the Minister for
Special Duties is moving to Education."
He would not stop dancing
"Mio se ni jo ?"
The press were quick to corner President Bill Clinton's trouble-shooter in
Africa - Ambassador Thomas Picketing the Under Secretary of State for Political
Affairs. Apart from serving as U.S. Ambassador to Nigeria, he went on to one of
the toughest assignments for any career diplomat
Ambassador to the then Soviet Union. He was in Abuja to repeat the same
warning he repeatedly conveyed to Mikhael Gorbachev of Soviet Union.
"Democracy does not come easy. Just make sure you don't press the self-
destruct button."
In the case of Nigeria, he had an even more poignant message, after
intervening in the ceaseless rancour between the executive and the legislators:
"President Clinton's visit is only a few weeks away. You better not press
the self-destruct button."
It was the press which mischievously quoted him as saying:
"You guys had better make sure you do not press the self-destruct button -
at least not until after President Clinton's visit."
President Olusegun Obasanjo got the message. His first year anniversary will
be void of pomp and pageantry. There will be no dancing or merry-making. The
President will be hosting a retreat at his farm in Otta, Ogun State to think
and reflect on the enormous task of steering our nation away from
the precipice. At the retreat, Mr. President will call the tune and change
the song to
"Mio se ni ronu ?"
Why will I not seek redemption - through repentance and the acknowledgment
of the power of the Almighty whose mercies towards Christians and Moslems alike
are as awesome as they are infinite ?
We are all God's children. Some just prefer to carry guns.

Original_Search_Title: Ado, Ekiti State Capital, Exhibits Traits Of A Growing Metropolis
Headline: Nigeria: Ado, Ekiti State Capital, Exhibits Traits Of A Growing Metropolis
Body_Text:
Lagos — Like termites on their way to war with soldier ants, the
vehicles inched their way forward in a snarling traffic. At the old
motor park, Ado, the Ekiti state capital, a Toyota bus materialised
from the adjacent Mobil petrol station, honked in a deafening manner
and joined the line of automobiles in a careless tributary.
That Friday, 18 February, it narrowly missed the rear light of a
Mercedes Benz car. "Kee se o? Urugbon re tasere (what is wrong with
you, wearer of a goatee?)." The occupant of the second vehicle blurted
in Ekiti dialect, as he craned his head out of the window, his neck
veins as taut as piano chords. Passers-by and bus conductors hailed the
encounter with glee.
Heavy traffic and the carelessness that goes with it, is one of the
new faces of Ado-Ekiti. General expansion, new roads, modern buildings,
a boisterous night life, hotels, the golf course, two higher
institutions, a broadcasting service, government ministries,
parastatals, public and private companies are the other major features
of this new state capital. And of course, social unrest, the flip side
of urban sprawl.
Nestled among rocks and hills, the bases of which provide rich
alluvial fans for agriculture, Ado Ekiti is more than a melting pot for
indigenes of the many towns that constitute Ekiti State. It is a
pressure cooker of nature, history and modernity. Overlooking the city
on the eastern side is the Olota, an imposing igneous rock . Opposite
it is the Ayoba, a highland with wooded wind ward and Savannah- like
lee ward sides. While Oke Ajo, another rock of basalt, stands guard at
Oke Ila, Utere, a conical structure, overlooks both the Ado-Ekiti
metropolis and Igirigiri, an agrarian village. Agidimo, a sloppy
highland is home to the famous Christ School and the state's specialist
hospital.
Before it became the capital of Ekiti state when it was created on 1
October 1996, by Gen. Sani Abacha, the late head of state, Ado-Ekiti's
perimeters did not go beyond Ado-Grammar School, Omolayo Standard
Press, Falegan Estate and Opopogboro. Now, the town has extended beyond
these points with eye-catching private buildings dotting the landscape.
Government is not left out either in building construction. Standing
out are the Government House, the Conference Centre (built through
direct labour under the regime of Lt. Col. Mohammed Bawa, a former
military administrator), the deputy governor's office, begun by the
Navy Captain Atanda Yusuf regime and the ministry of finance created on
the slope of Ayoba. The High Court of Justice was given a new design by
the Aramoko-Ekiti-born architect, Fola Alade, the brain behind, all
federal secretariats in the country.
On the other side of the hill from the governor's office, a de-luxe
white mansion with a black gate and four stars in bass relief, sits
like a brooding hen. It is owned by Alhaji Jobi Fele, former chairman
of the Oodua Group of Companies and the man behind Jobitex Nigeria
Limited. At the back of Ola Oluwa Grammar School, on an extended ridge,
is the sprawling mansion of Otunba Reuben Famuyibo, one time
gubernatorial candidate of the state. Popularly called the "White
House," the premises which is visible from almost one third of the town
is like a medieval castle sans the draw bridge. On one of the five
structures, "St Peters" is painted in black while a brown statue of a
farmer stands sentry on the premises.
"I know this first set belongs to Reuben Famuyibo, and the other one
is for his brother," a pupil of the adjacent Ola Oluwa Grammar School
said, pointing at the top of the hill. "But I don't know who owns the
third one," he confessed, clearing his nose with the back of his hand.
Other structures like these abound on Ikere Road, Ureje, Adebayo
Estate, Government Reservation Area (GRA) and Iyin Road.
Any visitor approaching the town from Akure would dance the palongo
on the bad road to Ikere. But from the latter town, it is a smooth ride
to Ado-Ekiti as Picollo Bruneli Engineering is busy putting finishing
touches to the major artery.
Speaking on the development of his hometown, Chief S. B. Falegan,
former managing director of the Federal Mortgage Bank, said that the
city is vertically and diagonally divided by federal roads. He lamented
that the World Bank had to cancel the $250 million Omuaran (Kwara
State)- Ado-Ekiti Road, because of a greedy businessman. "The Federal
Government has just now awarded the contract in the year 2000
supplementary budget."
Associated with road construction and rehabilitation are
beautification parks in the state capital. These are in Ijigbo
roundabout (where the statue of Aduloju, the ancient warrior was
erected), Odo Ado, Okeyinmi, post office and the Lt. Col. Adekunle
Fajuyi Park which, apart from commemorating the former military
governor of the old Western State, killed in the counter coup of 1975,
now holds the status of the unknown soldier. Along these roads, and
parks, traffic hold-ups are experienced daily, especially during
official resumption and closing hours. "This is as a result of influx
of people to this town after the state creation," Tayo Oba, a
commercial bus driver told this magazine at the AP petrol station,
Okeyinmi road. Other areas are Ajilosun, Okesha, (where, as an indigene
said, it was possible to serve the siesta) Fajuyi and Similoluwa.
Night life, an alien culture is another new feature of Ado-Ekiti,
for which different clubs abound. King's place is opposite the
University of Ado Ekiti annexe at Adebayo Estate, Carnival Relaxation
is not far from the Union Bank, Mammas Night Club, owned by Are Femi
Adebayo, is situated at Okesha, opposite the police station and Tikus
Nightclub is at Dalimore. Others are First Creation on Stadium Road and
Igbinedion not far from Okesha market. "It is called Igbinedion,
because it is a popular centre for igbin (snail)," Abiodun Ojo, a night
crawler, explained, as he waved flies off his bottle of Guinness.
Also, the Godfather makes use of the Fajuyi Memorial Park. Owned by
Remi Shittu, the night club began operation in October 1998 and it has
branches in Adekunle, Ajilosun, (family support programme playing
ground), Atanda Yusuf Plaza, a spitting distance from Fajuyi park and
Ondo town. "Our success lies with my oga who used to work with the
Ministry of Commerce," Adegbite Adedeji, the manager, explained.
On a higher level, there are now many hotels in the town. AETNA,
Olujoda, both on Ikere Road; Sigma International, Ikare Road; Bijou
Relaxation Centre, Oke Ese; First Creation, Kajola; Michofab,
Opopogboro; Major, Hotel Comfort, Kay Galaxy and Kure on Ilawe Road.
Others are Sadiat, Cottage, Moronfolu, West End, Micho, Bolingo, Agbaye
and Fem Guest House.
Unfortunately, Owena jointly owned by Ondo and Ekiti states, is in a
moribund state. Gov. Adebayo revealed to The News, however, that his
administration had incorporated a new company, Fountain Hotels. "We are
going to build a five star hotel on the premises of the Owena Hotels.
The architect is working on the drawings." Still on recreation
facilities, a modern golf course, built by the regime of Bawa, is
situated on an abandoned cattle ranch and dairy farm at Erinfun.
Architect Alade, wearing a pair of white shorts, a vest and Coca
Cola cap and Oba Oluwole Amele, the Alara of Aramoko Ekiti ( former
Councillor Balogun in the Village Headmaster soap opera), were busy
playing when The News called on 18 February. Oba Amele said that he
started playing golf at the club in Ikeja, Lagos. "When I became Oba in
1998", the traditional ruler revealed, "Chief Alade advised me to keep
playing the game in order not to grow too fat." The Kabiyesi added that
he likes playing golf because "it is a game you play against yourself,
a game of personal conviction." It is, however, not all play in Ado-
Ekiti. The pounded yam-loving people have two higher institutions: the
University of Ado-Ekiti and the Federal Polytechnic. Chief Omolayo, an
industrialist is building five blocks of hostels, while the state
government is also constructing a lecture theatre and the Oodua block
on the University campus.
At any rate, many secondary schools are in dilapidated state. While
Ola Oluwa College has no new buildings since the days of Chief Fasuba,
the principal, Ado-Grammar School has no chapel (the existing one has
been converted to classrooms). Christ's School, Mary Immaculate and
others are yearning for development. Adebayo, however, told The News
that his administration is mapping out plans to go into the schools.
What is causing the delay is the Federal Government's Universal Basic
Education (UBE) programme. "We are waiting for them to concretise so
that we know exactly what it entails and where to come in."
Ekiti indigenes who used to hook up to the Ondo State radio and
television stations now have their own broadcasting service, under Mr.
Tunde Erinsukunola, general manager.
However, Ado-Ekiti is not without the problems associated with urban
expansion. In the words of Falegan, "since the creation of the state,
the population of the town has tripled. The demand on social facilities
is higher.
Publication date: May 8, 2000

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Is Unfair To The Oil Producing Areas - Guy Ikokwn
Headline: Nigeria Is Unfair To The Oil Producing Areas - Guy Ikokwn
Body_Text:
Lagos — Second Republic Chairman of the defunct Nigeria People's
Party (NPP) in the old Anambra and the immediate past chairman of the
PDP in Anambra State, Chief Guy Ikokwu declares in this interview with
Igbonekwu Ogazamorah and Obiorah Ifoh that Nigeria has been too unfair
with the more the N500b oil fortune it made in the past particularly
with the way it was applied in the oil producing communities.
The Nigeria of today is one where there are reports of group
stockpiling arms. Ten there is the claim that certain fundamentalists
are arming some northerners in the East and all those things. With
these, do you believe that Nigeria is going to survive with the kind of
values we are operating at this moment?
This is a ponderous question. But I'm optimistic. Nigeria will
survive because over the past sixty years this country has been welded
by certain factors and interests across the nation. But more people now
have an interest in the indissolubility of the union than in its
dismemberment. So, we will have problems, we will have fracases, but at
the end of the day not the sort that will lead to the nation being
dismembered. I believe that more people have an abiding faith in the
greatness of the country or the vision of Nigeria's leadership position
both in West Africa and in Africa, than to opt for its fragmentation or
dismemberment or Balkanisation. Problems, yes we will have problems, we
will continue to have problems even when you have stability, even when
you have a stable government, stability doesn't mean you won't have
problems because politics is about resolution of problems and
conflicts.
With regards to arms I do not have the information. I have heard the
allegation made, the security services, the police and the SSS should
be better informed and should be in a better position to enlighten the
presidency, the National Assembly and the nation. The National Assembly
if they are certified from the security reports which they have, the
constitution allows them to investigate, they can raise a panel or they
can do certain investigation and where the representatives from the
area where you have this so called "arms catch' are enlightened about
what is going on and them they have a responsibility in the National
Assembly to make sure that situation does not materialise or to abate
it.
Because that the function of the National Assembly, that is why its
in continuous session to deal with the daily problems affecting the
constituency not just the members themselves, but their constituency
and then the nation as one corporate whole. But as far as Nigeria is
concerned I believe that the country is destined to be great. If
Nigeria did not disintegrate in the days of Abacha, anybody talking
about disintegration now, really, does not love this nation and
probably does not love his own catchment area because we cannot be
worst off now them we were under the Abacha regime. So we just have to
take measures to enumerate whatever problems, whether they are
political, economic, social, cultural or security wise, there must be
dialogue on these issues on these problems.
There are what people claim to be the three main indices of a stable
society-Justice, equity and fairplay Do these things now go hand-in-
hand with your optimism. The believe in the indissolubility of the
country and the stand of those who you think cannot go for
dismemberment of this country at this moment.
These are credible indices for a stable, united country - justice,
equity, fairness, these incorporated in our coat of arms, incorporated
in our National Anthem, in our national ethos and cradle into the
constitution, the 1999 constitution even with allots defects. But it is
one thing to have these things in print it is another thing to practice
them. I believe, sincerely, that if Nigeria practices true federalism
and not false federalism, then this country will be able to forge
ahead. By false federalism I mean during the military regime we were
calling Nigeria a 'Federal Military Government' it is an anathema it is
a contradiction in terms it is a misnormal to say 'Federal Military'
what is federal in the military? The military is a unitary set up, it
has a command structures that is absolutely unitary from top to bottom
and just dishing out commands. It does not give any room for dialogue
or for querrying that command, even the administrators for instance
were on military postings, are military governors or military
administrators.
So, there is nothing federal about the military interregnum that we
have had for over thirty something years and that is what has distorted
Nigeria federalism. Over the years when the military were in power from
the end of the civil war. What you had infact was a dismemberment,
gradual distortion and dismemberment or dismantling of the federal
structure. At a time for instance we used to have two separate
constitutions, one constitution for the country another constitution
for the regions, to take care of their regional secularities and
problems. But the military discarded the regional or state constitution
and put in place just one nation constitution.
They did that to the local government, they appointed Dasuki to head
the local government reform or harmonisation, so that we have one local
government system from Bomadi to Sokoto, that it is the very antitheses
of federalism. The civilian government have tried to shake this off,
the more they try to do that, the more you see them as confrontational.
Whereas in the true sense of it they are trying to practice true
federalism to take us back to a situation where the federating units
are autonomous as far as the legislative and executive lists under
their own portfolio or reasons are concerned. So you have two lists,
one is the federal and one is the state or regional and then concurrent
where either of them can.
A situation for instance where you had supervisory councilor for
education and agriculture being appointed from Abuja, for Cross River
State, for Oron is anathema, that is the very opposite of federalism.
What has the man in Abuja got to do with a local government councilor
for Agriculture in Oron? Does he know the area, does he know the
people? With the resolve that people at local government now alter to
keep towards Abuja as federal for their appointments, for their money,
then it got to the stage where federal government now took over
financing of local government and then put in the constitution. In the
present 1999 constitution you find that the federal government also
funds local government directly and the state government are also to
fund the local government and under the constitution, the state
governments are allowed to make laws through the state assembly for the
structuring or restructuring or otherwise of the local government in
their area.
And some state governments immediately have started to do so. By
setting up committee or commissions for the creation of more local
government areas within their state without reference to Abuja. The
next thing is to know the fiscal power or authority of the federal
government vis-a-vis the local government. The local government matter
is purely a state matter. I'm just giving you an example where true
federalism can work. There is nothing, in the nature of local
government structure in my own area, let's say Idemili, that will make
Idemili look like Kebbi or Sokoto area. In Idemili we have 19 towns
under the old Idemili local government.
With the creation of new local government areas under the Arthur
Mbanefo committee during the Abacha days, we now have two local
governments. Idemili south, now still has 12 towns, 12 communities -
autonomous communities in one local government. As of today, most of
the towns in Idemili south do not feel the impact of the local
government because it is still far remote from their daily life. Before
the civil war and before the advent of the Dasuki harmonisation of
local government, in the south, in the Yoruba land, in Edo, in Delta,
in Igboland, in Akwa Ibom and in Cross River and in Rivers because of
the intensity of the population because of the preponderous of
autonomous communities, each autonomous community has its own local
council, we used to call them native council. Like you come and abolish
council which was in my own town Oba and you now remove it, instead of
us having 12 wards and 12 representative in the town going to council
meeting 20 kilometres away to the local government and they come back
reporting to nobody. So, what you have under the Abacha days was that
the councillors for the town goes to meeting, share the money for
fertiliser, take the money for education, primary education and so,
they share the money, embezzle and so on, because they report to
nobody, they do not report to the town union, they do not report to the
general assembly of their own towns. These are the councillors, because
nobody is holding them to accountability. So, they go there do what
they like and vanish, they will now tell the people in the local
communities to pay tax for this and that, they reneged because they
cannot feel the impact of the local government in their communities so,
you have a lot of money which is idle, which is not utilised for
development and so on, for road repairs, for markets, for education,
for hospital, for healthcare and so on. Which of these towns can very
well take care of by themselves, because some of them have a population
of about 50,000.
In Mbaise area, some of them may even be a 100,000. But you then go
to the north to Kebbi State to Zamfara - a local authority there has
probably one major town or may be 2 towns and the expanse is about 100
kilometres or 150 kilometres for 2 towns. Then you use that to come and
make a uniform system in the town and you then put 19 towns or more
under one local government, and you say you are harmonising and then
you give each town one councillor. So these are the areas we are
talking about true federalism. If we return this country and structure
it into truely federal state, where there are separation of power and
functions. the executive should know what is executive responsibility
and pursue it. The assembly should know what is Assembly authorities.
They are there to promulgate laws for the well being, governance and
development of the good governance not just any type of governance,
good governance and development of the state is their responsibility.
They have even federal judicial powers in the state Assembly and
vis-a-vis also in the National Assembly to virtually prosecute, to
investigate any matter, make findings and make recommendations. That's
why they have power of impeachment. If they investigate any matter and
find out the executive is guilty or any individual or public officer is
guilty of gross dereliction of duty, of gross misconduct then the
person can be impeached or be recommended either for a judicial enquiry
or for criminal prosecution or otherwise. Assemblies have really strong
powers. Then of course you have judiciary. So they are co-ordinate and
at the same, autonomous in their own area of responsibility that's why
you have the theory of separation of powers. But let me tell you that
many assemblies have not examined their powers properly because many of
them give in to the slightest carrot from the executive arm or from
some other external pressure. Probably one way out is for them to be
self accounting, for them to have their own budget for them to execute
their responsibilities. The samething also for the judiciary should be
self-accounting, if we want an honest, independent judiciary that does
not have to look over its shoulders to know what the executive wants or
to look the other way to know what the assembly wants to be done, their
conditions of service should be such as to insorlate them from external
pressure.
So, they can dispense justice without fear or favour. That's why the
first word in their indices was justice, then the other one is equity
in the distribution of resources across the board and the next one was
fairplay etc. In the case of fair play. Why should an area of the
country, a zone that produces 90 per cent of the wealth of the country
and yet they don't have drinking water. They don't have light; their
children cannot go to good school; they don't have post primary, post-
secondary, university placements.
A state like Bayelsa had only 28 kilometres of road. That is 100
arrant nonsense. When their children see such things today and they
hear that oil money will finish, in the next 40 years, look, prudence
dictates that they start asking question today because we've already
had 40 years of oil money. What have we done with it? Nigeria cannot
show to the world what we have done wit over 300 billion dollars of oil
money. If you go to Indonesia they will show what they did with their
own money at the same time that Nigeria found oil in commercial
quantity. Others are Indonesia, Malaysia and so on.
But they have done a lot with their own oil money so Nigeria now
becomes a net importer of finished goods from Indonesia. You see our
businessmen, our traders all flying out to the far east to Indonesia,
Malaysia to go and but palm oil from Malaysia and bring it back to
Nigeria to sell. Can you Imagine that! Is annoying, to go and buy soap-
tablet soap from Indonesia; you see their pharmaceutical - our
businessmen and traders take samples from Europe and flyout to
Indonesia and give it to their pharmaceutical factories there to
manufacture for Nigeria, then Nigeria becomes a dumping ground for all
