kinds of commodities - standard and sub-standard goods. Why won't our
foreign exchange or foreign reserve finish. How can you be buying from
the whole world all rubbish, we still import tooth pick to Nigeria,
still import comb, we are still importing hair dye chemicals and so on;
cosmetic items in Nigeria in the year 2000.
So, the people in the Rivers and the Delta States, the Niger Delta
basin and so on, are going to feel angry. The youths are going to feel
angry. They are bound to. Then you promise them 13 per cent derivation
from oil proceeds and it has not been paid till today, they are still
on their knees begging for the 13 per cent which was due to them.
Abacha did not pay, he was holding those 13 per cent the constitution
says that those oil producing areas be entitled to not less than 13 per
cent, they should be entitled to more, the National Assembly can make
laws or regulations for the Niger-Delta area-oil producing states to be
paid 25 per cent authoritatively in the revenue allocation committee,
they can do it and if the National Assembly passes it, then so be it.
It will go to them, but why do you have to manage for them?
Is their own allocation, why do you have to manage it for them?
Distribute it among the states that produce oil on a pro-rata basis you
know how many barrels they are producing. Why should federal government
create a dichotomy between on-shore and offshore? Is the military that
started this rubbish and the reason they started it is because the
military man at the top is dictator, they have always beem from Buhari
to Babangida to Abacha so a dictator is a one man band.
The bulk starts from his table and ends on his table, his word is
law. And what we have seen is that they have invariably embezzled money
which should have gone for the development of these areas. So, that
area is impoverished in 40 years the oil will finish, you have much
money, billions of NNPC money was spent to look for oil or prospect for
oil in Lake Chad basin. The same money was also used to prospect for
oil in Bauchi and Gombe States. Elf Nigeria has just left that area-
Gombe, and said they cannot see oil in commercial quantities, but the
last military administrators of those states have announced to the
country that there is oil in commercial quantities. Look! where we are
sitting here in Lagos, you start drilling for oil from here, if you
have enough money to drill, you can drill it until you get to Bayelsa
and you start sucking the oil which is in Bayelsa from Lagos, because
the earth is spherical if there is oil in the south pole, you can start
drilling from the north pole if you can drill all the way to the south
pole where the oil is you 'll pump oil.
You understand what I am trying to say, the samething, you can drill
diagonally, from Lagos here, you can put your drill and drill up to
Bayelsa. The point is it commercially justifiable? if it is not
commercially justifiable then it is a wasted investment because you are
putting in money then you'll be getting and nobody is going to throw
away that kind of money it is not father Christmas business. They
putting money, any money, good money in order to get some profit and
use. So, that dichotomy between offshore and onshore should be
abolished. Okigbo panel on fiscal matters, Dr. Pius Okigbo had said at
one time that the federal government under a true federalism does not
require more than 30 per cent to run effectively the federal
government, discharging the responsibilities of the federal government.
Why should federal government be interested in primary education and
UPE.
The concentration of powers in the area led to massive corruption
and embezzlements, because you 'have education tax which is like
petroleum fund terms and over 8 billion was realised, in the education
tax and was kept away, some of it were even put in private account
under Abacha. It took the eagle eyes of Obasanjo while looking at the
budgetary requirement of these parastatals, while he was looking at
that of education he found a sum of N1 billion which was not properly
accounted for that was about 3 months ago.
It had to go to the presidency, Obasanjo had to use his eagle eyes
to find out that N1 billion was missing, there was something wrong, and
he put a hold on the account, and yet our educational institutions are
rotting away - sub-standard, no facilities, delicacy infrastructures
and so on so, what is the business of federal government with primary
education. Give the states the money that they require to run the
activities and responsibilities which the constitution has given to
states and they will use their money and take care of their
responsibilities within their states at the states level; at the local
government level or otherwise.
Those who want to use their own prudently will use them prudently.
You know the story of the prince in the Bible that said he was going
away and he gave certain amount to his servants and his children and so
on. Very few utilised their own very well, others kept their own they
didn't do anything with it, some wasted it away. It a state does not
want to move ahead and it used its money to give interest-free loans to
all and sundry in the state I can tell you there won't be any
development in that state and in the next 4 years those people who
received interest-free loans will vote against the government because
that was not a judicious use of their money and their resources. The
people will have their say within that state. So, at the end of the
day, people will decide.
We do not have to have uniform roles in Nigeria because we don't
have uniform culture or otherwise. If I want to go and invest in state
A, if I go there I see the kind of climate of investment they have,
what kind of regulations they have, if I like it, I settle down and put
my investment there. But do you know that there are states you go to,
you put your investment, you are paying your tax, you are doing
everything, you build a house and so on, when your child wants to go to
school, they look at the name of your child-your brilliant child and
find out your child is not answering an indigenous name, they, will
discriminate, your child will not get a placement.
You applied for formal land, your won't get it unless you go and buy
the landfrom an indegene who has been given the land and yet he cannot
build and yet you are contributing to the governance and development of
that state. When you do this fair distribution of responsibilities and
so on, there is freedom of association, there is freedom of movement
and so on. The same way foreigner come into this place you don't have
to tell a foreigner he will put his money. Do you know that under the
military, under Gowon, there was a time when we had what you call - It
was a law passed by the legislative council, "Locational Approval."
If you take your own money 100 per cent you build a factory to
assemble radio set, you go to the federal ministry of trade first for
an approval, they will tell the state you will go and locate that
industry with your own money. They will tell you there are too many
industries already in Lagos or too many in Port Harcourt, please go to
state X and State Y choose between state X and Y with your own money,
is all locational approval.
How many people who said if you have to come to locational approval
then they will rather not invest there because they know that they will
not get their return on investment if they are compelled to do it, so
many did not invest and we were worst off for it. Before the war, I can
tell you petroleum was deregulated, this is a sensitive matter today
why should it be sensitive? Petroleum in a federal economy, Petroleum
resources should not have to be the same cost all over the country. It
is not so in America. In America in the same street filling station
sell petrol at different prices for the same grade of petrol, the same
grade of diesel, the same grade of kerosine, the same pack of Aviation
fuel in the same street, in the same town at different price, some
offer you incentives - You buy they give you one bottle of distilled
water free of charge, when you pay they give you coupon when your
booklet is full you take it, you go to the supper market you pick any
item worth the value of those coupon, these are incentives for
marketing.
They don't sell at the same price. But the federal military
government which is an antithesis, enacted a law which is called
petroleum equalisation fund compelling all petroleum marketers to pull
their resources together take part of the money which you pay for
petrol and put it not into a fund, so they use it in transporting
petrol to different parts of the country so you 'll pay the same price
of petrol in Port Harcourt as you pay in Maiduguri as you will pay in
Sokoto. That was subsidy for petroleum products which the IMF frowned
against, because when you subsidise something to the extent that the
man you are subsidising it for no longer values it.
The same thing was done for fertiliser, because the fertiliser
factory is at Onne down there in the south on the Atlantic shed. You
now subsidise it totally; for agriculture elsewhere and what happened,
for every hundred trucks of fertiliser that leaves onne, forty are
embezzled and diverted to neighbouring countries and to the
blackmarket, this is a result of subsidy and the nation became the
worst for it. That factory virtually closed down because it's owing a
lot of money, it cannot pay its creditors, and suppliers, because, its
no longer a commercial venture. That is not federalism, it isn't. Am I
paying the same price for onions in Lagos as onions sold in Kano? When
I travel to Kano I buy 3 or 4 big baskets of onions and put inside my
car, do I pay the same price for potato? I don't. Do I pay the same
price for tomato even yam? you use the federal government money to
build a dam, billions of naira, to build a dam after building it, then
you use it for agriculture there, very good. Do they pay back to the
federal government cost of that investment on the dam? Everybody just
goes there and either fishes or grows his crop and they sell where?
Sells it to the open market, then I pay more for it because it is far
removed from my own area. But Nigeria is an agricultural economy, so if
those who have agricultural potentials will utilise the immense
agricultural resources they will berich, just like America, America is
an agro-based economy today even though it is the most industrialised
economy in the world, it is still agro based. They don't joke with it.
Why are we joking with our own agriculture.
States that have agricultural potential no longer want to do
anything about their agriculture, they want oil. The day oil money
finishes that is the day they will go back to agriculture and those who
do not have land for agriculture will now practise agriculture on open
sea. So that's why you have this agitation that the system at the
moment is not fair, therefore we don't have true federalism. If we have
true federalism, people will be accommodated according to the gift that
they have, areas will develop at their own pace and at their own rate.
People will accentuate, states will accentuate those things they can do
more, there are area that are gifted for tourism and you know tourism
attracts a lot of foreign capital and investment.
Take country like Kenya, Kenya economy cannot do today without
tourism, Ivory coast today cannot do without tourism and you'll be
surprised, economies like Britain still thrives on tourism, they give
you visa, visa, visa. If Britain can give 200,000 visas a month to
Nigerians that have money to spend, they will do so, because they know
that those people coming into their country will bring their own money
to spend inside Britain, they are not coming to take a loan from
Britain, rather they are coming to spend their money, to purchase.
That's why if you have something to offer, if you have your own good
money to spend within the twinkling of an eye they will give you a visa
and they did that when Nigeria's economy was very strong in the 1970's.
We could travel to any part of the world without visa.
People will invite you and jump into a plane, land at their airport,
immediately you put Nigeria green passport on the table, the
immigration officer will ask you how long do you want to stay? "You 'll
tell them that you are here for jut five days" Oh no no! You can stay
more please," he gives out six months, For those six months you 'll be
spending your money in their own country so, Britain attracts well over
3 million visitors a year.
And each year they try to increase it, for it to move to 5 million
because those people are coming to spend money inside Britain and they
start producing things which foreigners will like to buy and put on all
kinds of exhibitions, all kinds of shows. We have areas in this country
that can be attractive for tourism. Take Kano State for instance, when
these inter-parliamentary union delegates came, they were taken to Kano
to go and see Durbar, it's a rich cultural heritage. The samething,
children in Kano who have not seen Mkpokiti Dance or this other Rivers
dance, the canoe dance, they will like to come here and see that
crocodile dance, in Rivers State.
So you have the Internal tourism and then you have the external
tourism and all these lead to exchange of money. Money is not meant for
you to hoard put it somewhere and lock it up, no, is a means of
exchange, the more you turn it over, the more it increase in its value
and makes through from hand to hand, from state to state etc. But when
you put up hurdles preventing people to go internally to go and invest
in state A or B. working all those states for under-development and
people are losing jobs they have a lot of people who are idle, this was
what led the federal government now to start talking of poverty
alleviation.

Original_Search_Title: Face of Africa Crown
Headline: Africa: Face of Africa Crown
Body_Text:
Lagos — All the necessary elements were in place. Music, designs by
African fashion gurus, fun, laughter and 16 contestants were the
dominant features of the show.
The venue was the Cape Town's Good Hope centre in South Africa and
the event was the final of the M-Net Face of Africa 2000. Guests came
from within and outside Africa to watch nubile young girls contest in a
race to pick a new Face of Africa model who will go on to sign a 3-year
modelling contract with Elite International Models Agency for $150,000.
After about one hour of high class fashion and music, Nkhesani
Manganyi, the contest official anchorperson was joined on the
flamboyant stage by a guest and it was time to announce the various
winners.
The race to find a successor to Nambia's Bevinda Mundenge, winner of
the 1999 edition, commenced last year when screenings were held in many
countries across the continent. The contest produced national
representatives in a regional final held in the four sub-regions of
Africa.
Four girls qualified from each country and they moved on to regional
finals.
The West Africa final was held on December 4, 1999 at the NICON
Hilton Hotel, Abuja from where Nigeria's Funmilayo Alaiyemola, Ojy
Egwuenu emerged alongside Cameroun's Marion Ngako and Aissata Diabate
of Mali. From East Africa came Lilliane Kanyamuneza, 19, (Burundi),
Patricia Namayiira, 20 (Uganda), Kabula Ngurusi, 21 (Tanzania) and Eva
Ndachi, 20 (Kenya). North Africa zonal contest produced Norchene
Cherif, 20 (Tunisia), Imen Merghni, 16 (Tunisia), Lara El Tanahy, 20
(Egypt) and Semhar Afeworki, (Eritrea).
The Southern region sent Suraya Dhalakan, 20 of Mozambique and three
others - Lerato Moloi, 17, Nombuleto Mazibuko, 16, and Swelihle
Mtshali, 18 - from South Africa.
Activities were ably directed by Mkhensani Manganyi who has had the
enviable record of anchoring all shows since the commencement of the M-
Net face of Africa. At the end, South Africa's Nombulelo Mazibuko
emerged the new face to pick the shoes successively dropped by
Nigeria's winner of the maiden edition, Oluchi Onweagba and her
successor Bevinda.
Four other girls emerged as runners-up and will contest in the Elite
Model Look contest in Paris, France later this year for a prize of
$50,000 modelling contract. The four girls are Marion, Norchene, Imen
and Lerato.
Although Nigeria did not win any slot this year, the country's
representatives shone as stars. Oluchi had earlier put Nigeria in the
limelight. She was introduced as one of the judges.
She also model an outfit by a fashion house alongside her successor
Bevinda.
Oluchi's grace and elegance on the runway that night was a testimony
to the exposure she had acquired in the fashion cities of Milan, Paris,
New York and other places.
This year's edition was indeed an honour at home for the winner.
Music was supplied by top-notch South African artistes and the local
crowd went into an orgy of happiness when their local girl won the
contest.
Lerato, one of the runners-up, is also a South African. For the
South African owned M-Net, the show was a celebration of African
values. And each of the contestants will leave the country with the
compere's words that blessed are the parents who gave Africans such an
array of beautiful faces.
For the Nigerian girls at the final, the competition remains an
opening to the world of international modelling.
Ronnie Dikko Though unmarried, actress Ronnie Dikko is regarded as
one of the best mothers on screen. She spoke to OYIN BADA-ONIME on her
life and desire for a good name
Family Circle was a huge success for Ronnie Dikko. Everyweek for
about two television quarters people saw a beautiful portrayal of a
loving mother by Ronnie. They saw her also as an understanding lady who
always stood by her husband even when things were tough. They were
mesmerised by her acting technique which captured the essence of a
woman's role in a family. Ronnie as Peju in family circle was the
dedicated mother and wife.
Ironically Ronnie who started acting by chance is not a mother in
real life.
She is not even married. Acting started for the tall, ebony black
graduate of the university of Ilorin by chance. A friend had informed
her about an open role in a television production for which many people
had already gone for auditioning. Waiting to give expression to the
talent she knew she had Ronnie attended an auditioning and after
mesmerising the production crew with her command of words and knowledge
of acting she was promptly offered a role. That programme she went for
was family circle which ran on the African Independent Television (AIT)
for months. For the excellent performance of the entire cast, the soap
opera got rave reviews from the moviedom and the Nigerian press. As
Peju in family circle Ronnie was mother to two daughters and a son. Her
husband, Ansah, was played by theatre and television actor Nobert
young.
Marriage, for the thirty something year old Ronnie is something she
desires.
Her good portrayal of the life of a doting mother in her numerous
productions may erroneously lead people into believing she is married.
No man has walked her down the aisle yet and as much as she desires
marriage, she does not want to rush into it just because her biological
time clock is already ticking. Married or not, Ronnie has sterling
qualities that stand her out among her peers. She has got attitude, but
the right one which explains her ability to relate to almost anybody. A
large dose of her down to earth nature was on display when Tempolife
visited her. Inviting our reporter to join her on her favourite mat,
she quickly let her hair down and gave a brief description of her
person. Picking her words, she described herself as a very nice person
who strive to be fair and reasonable with people. "I try to mix well
with people and I also love the good things of life".
Ronnie's good portrayal of the life of a wife and mother in Family
Circle has however brought with it an unwelcome stigmatization in the
Nigerian movie world.
Most of the producers who offers her roles always want to typecast
her as a mother.
This tendency to restrict actors to one role is a major minus in the
Nigerian acting environment. While Ronnie agree that she gave good
expression to the role of a mother she does not subscribe to the idea
of limiting her creativity and talent to just that single role. She
would love to see other actresses giving the opportunity to express
themselves as mothers but she also wants to demonstrate the wide
spectrum of parts she can act. She confessed to Tempolife that "I have
to be careful or else I will become permanently stereotype." In the
home movie Raging Storm. She acted the part of a woman who was married
to a cultist, a part which still restricted her to just somebody's
wife.
Type-casting or not, Ronnie finds each work she has done
challenging. She also admits that acting has been very good to her. It
has given her popularity beyond her widest dreams.
Everywhere she goes people call her Peju (her name in Family Circle)
and she responds with a smile and occasional handshakes.
Her typical day starts at 6:00am when her mental alarm clock goes
off.
Morning prayers are as important to her as waking up and she says
one to the Lord religiously every morning. The quiet time of the
morning is also spent on deep meditating reflecting on activities of
the previous day and working out plans for the new day. If she has no
appointment in the morning, she goes back to bed.
Although she does not have a husband yet to go everywhere with.
Ronnie has a companion which goes everywhere with her cellular phone.
The mobile phone helps her maintain an unbroken contact with her
numerous friends and business partners. Her vocation is not restricted
to acting as she has other businesses she does on the side.
Before she became an actress, Ronnie worked with a business
consultancy firm upon graduating in the 80s. She rose to become a
manager before fate brought her into the glamour world of acting.
The self confessed home girl possesses a love for local snacks like
Boli and Epa (roasted plantain and groundnut). The Edo State-born
actress is also very much at home with pounded yam and banga soup. Her
favourite clothes are those made from African fabrics which she insists
can be made into different styles. Her fashion sense deplores body
exposing clothes and she vows that she won't be caught dead in
revealing clothes.
Her puritan approach to fashion also affects the acting roles she
accepts.
She has rejected many movie because she was asked to act roles she
considered offensive or indecent. "I only act positive roles and this
has helped me to be controversy free. At the end of the day when acting
is over, I'm going to be left with my good and clean image" she
concluded.
Publication date: April 27, 2000

Original_Search_Title: KCC Wants City Of Floods, Cholera? (Opinion)
Headline: Uganda: KCC Wants City Of Floods, Cholera? (Opinion)
Body_Text:
Kampala — Although I am not in the hotel business, I agree with the two
central statements made by our Town Clerk Gordon Mwesigye in his article
"Kampala Not City Of Yams, Monkeys" The Monitor, April 10.
On the one hand, he argues, we need favourably priced hotels. On the other
hand, he asserts a little bit further, we need the environment to work for the
people.
I fully agree with the latter, and all our efforts in the National Wetlands
Programme are especially aimed at that. I am also prepared to agree with the
former because I do not doubt the ability of Golf Course Holdings to make some
sensible economic projections on their investments, in this case a hotel and
shopping centre on the edge of Kampala's most famous wetland.
Why, then, do I react and run the risk of being accused of issuing
"instinctive," "populist" and other unnameable utterances on this topic?
Because our Town Clerk fails to link the two and bring them to a logical
conclusion. During World Wetlands Day, the Town Clerk put up a passionate case
for Kampala's swamps. He explained to 200 participants and a discussion panel
that in the 1900 Agreement, the swamps were purposely left out of the mailo land
because everybody well understood that they should be left alone.
They were, and are today, draining the city's storm water and discharging it
steadily into Lake Victoria. In the process the silt and dirt is filtered, and
the water entering Murchison Bay is relatively clean. Is this important? Yes,
because it is from the same bay that we pump our drinking water.
The more dirt and filth we discharge into the bay, the more effort National
Water and Sewerage Corporation has to put into its waterworks to give us safe
and clean drinking water.
In addition, 90 percent of Kampalans are not connected to any sewage
treatment system, and if it were not for the few wetlands that sit in between us
and the lake, we would by now be drinking our own sewage. The Town Clerk knows
this very well, as he so aptly demonstrated during his lecture in the Sheraton
Victoria Room.
What then does he mean by "developing our swamps without destroying them"?
Does he have in mind the Bwaise and Kamwokya suburbs that are literally built
in the swamps, and which were hit by disastrous floods in August and September
last year?
Has he forgotten the 46,000 cholera patients at the height of the flooding?
Or does he have in mind the UMA showground, conveniently built in the middle
of a swamp and being flooded during every serious rainstorm?
Or does he picture the mindless in-filling currently going on at the railway
yard and the sports ground near the Clock Tower?
Mr. Town Clerk, where do think the water will go when you "develop" all the
low lying areas in the city?
It won't disappear. On the contrary, it will flood your roads, factories and
residential areas. It will float the contents of pit-latrines and sceptic tanks
into your gutters, streams and protected springs, in which our children play,
our women do their laundry, and from which our families fetch their drinking
water.
We know that in these modern days, arguments are won on financial grounds,
not on soft issues as sick children, and a dirty Murchison Bay. Well, according
to figures from the Ministry of Health, last year's cholera outbreak cost the
government over $ 2m (over Shs 3bn) in medicines alone.
The value of Nakivubo Wetland's tertiary water treatment is conservatively
estimated at Shs 2.3bn per year. We could also calculate the costs of road
repairs, damages to houses and industries and lost investment opportunities
because of flooding, and the Town Clerk's $ 15m suddenly become peanuts.
Oh, yes, what about the badly needed hotels?
We would strongly advise the Town Clerk to attract as many hoteliers to build
as many hotels as possible. But why the heck build one in an area that is one of
the last beautiful stretches of green in the city?
And, more importantly, why build it on a spot that absorbs large quantities
of storm water that, when it finds its original home filled-in, for sure will
find its way into Jinja Road and the Wampewo roundabout.
Mr. Town Clerk, the bitter truth is: water cannot be simply wished away.
* Reint J. Bakema is Chief Technical Advisor of the National Wetlands
Programme Support Project.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala Not City Of Yams, Monkeys
Headline: Uganda: Kampala Not City Of Yams, Monkeys
Body_Text:
Kampala — The hotel development by Golf Course Holdings on plot 64-86, Yusuf
Lule (Kitante) Road has generated a lot of controversy that has engulfed not
only Kampala City Council (KCC) but also NEMA, the Inspector General of
Government, and most recently, the Parliamentary Committee on natural resources.
A lot of utterances have been made, some instinctive, some populist and some
not worth the effort put into them.
Whatever they are, what is emerging is that they lack a little bit of
research.
KCC would be failing in its mandate if it did not develop the city for the
benefit of all its residents and the country at large. As of now, hotel
accommodation in the city is so incredibly low that Kampala normally would not
qualify to host even a modest sized conference that requires just average
facilities.
Our "top class" hotels cannot among themselves raise 1,000 rooms!
The Sheraton, which is the largest in terms of rooms, has 251. The second is
probably Grand Imperial with 103 rooms. All the rest have less than 100 rooms
each. The Nile, Equatoria, Fairway, International, Africana, Diplomate, Silver
Springs, Speke, Tourist, Sheraton and Grand Imperial all put together can only
manage a mere 903 rooms.
To put it another way: after a day's deliberations at the International
Conference Centre (capacity 1,200-1,500), the delegates would have to go back
and share rooms.
We are talking of a capital city of a country that is marketing itself as a
"quality" tourist destination.
Some people have argued that even the few beds we have are not fully
occupied, so there is no need for more. Such an argument is not tenable, because
the average person is priced out of these hotels.
Not many people, even delegates or tourists, can afford $150 (Shs 230,000) a
night.
So they resort to seedy places that cannot be called hotels.
This country's hospitality industry, let alone the city's, is to say the
least, still backward and needs heavy sustained investment to bring it to a
level that is within even smelling distance of our neighbours.
Golf Course Hotel, therefore, is a step in the right direction.
It will be an investment of some US $15m (Shs 23bn) and will add another 145
rooms to our meagre 900. Besides, it is not situated on the golf course land,
but is an independent entity in its own right.
During construction, it is expected that it will provide employment to about
150 people.
After completion, there will be between 100-150 jobs for Ugandans.
This is not to mention the revenue that will accrue to government and the
council.
Now, about the environment. It is now fashionable and politically correct to
talk about "preserving the environment". But most times, we mean different
things, depending on who is talking.
In KCC, we do not believe that the people should work for the environment. We
believe that the environment should work for the people. Thus the need not only
to preserve and protect it, but also to develop it.
Swamps, for example, can be developed without necessarily being destroyed.
The council would welcome investors to develop eco-tourism sites, by
utilising the abundant swamps all around us (half the city is a swamp).
The construction of the hotel does not therefore automatically mean that the
environment is being destroyed. The so-called Sheraton Gardens, for instance,
are a public park, the Jubilee Park, which belongs to KCC.
Can anyone really say that because the park has been developed, the
environment has been destroyed ?
As a matter of fact, there is another wasted "green" which could be developed
as a historical tourist monument, complete with a hotel and other friendly
amenities.
This is the Mutineers Park across All Saints Church, Nakasero. No one as of
now can imagine that that is a preserved green in the state it is. Therefore,
whereas the council is conscious of the environment, we at the same time would
not encourage bush, yams, potatoes or monkeys in the city in the name of
preserving the environment.
Which is why we find it interesting that some people have actually argued
that the Golf Course Apartments, one of the biggest private investment in
residential accommodation in the country, should not have been located where
they are.
But that is properly gazetted residential area. Should the periphery of the
Golf Course be left wild?
We are aware of course that a number of those talking loudest are members of
the Golf Club.
The club has just embarked on major extension (both vertical and horizontal)
to their existing club house.
What irony!
* The author is the Town Clerk of Kampala City Council.

Original_Search_Title: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Headline: Ghana: Bank of Ghana cheques bounce 10 times
Body_Text:
Accra — China State Construction Engineering Corporation Ghana Ltd has
presented a cheque valued at over ¢240 million ten times for payment at the
Bank of Ghana. In as many times the cheque bounced.
The Chinese Government owned company has undertaken major construction
projects in Accra and is presently renovating State House, popularly referred
to as Job 600. It has also been awarded a contract to construct the Alajo
drainage works around Accra's Kwame Nkrumah Circle area.
Further Chronicle investigations at CAL Merchant Bank revealed that
ENERGOPROJECT, another European construction company based in Accra is facing
financial embarrassment following a similar experience of dishonoured cheques
drawn on Bank of Ghana cheque. A cheque for ¢318 million it presented for
payment has had problems with Bank of Ghana tellers for more than a month
having been rejected on a number of occasions. The cheque, issued on the 24th
of February, 2000 and credited to the company's account at CAL Merchant Bank
has bounced and as at April 5, 2000 the cheque had been so defaced that there
was no space on it for the 'Return to drawer' stamps usually administered on
cheques by banks.
Incidentally, that particular Cheque is part of the payment of a multi-
billion cedi new office for the Ministry of Finance. Energoproject has also
undertaken major projects for government including the Accra International
Conference Centre, Cedi House and the ongoing high rise building opposite SSNIT
Accra headquarters offices.
Another company similarly hit by these recurrent incidents of bounced
cheques is Razel, a French Construction Company. Currently battling serious
cash flow problems which is threatening jobs of construction workers. There
were fears expressed by workers visited by Chronicle at some of their sites
that they will be laid off soon, and some of the expatriate staff confirmed
their precarious condition.
Perhaps the company that may be hardest hit is the one that has had a
chequered history of run-ins and dalliance with the Government, the originally
German owned Construction Pioneers, previously called Carl Ploetner.
Chronicle learn that they are owed in excess of ¢40 billion and were
recently forced to lay off workers to stave off collapse. A management staff
confirmed this information to The Chronicle at their Korle Lagoon project site
in Accra last Friday.
The Director of Budget at The Ministry of Finance, Major Tara said his
Ministry does not issue cheques but only approves of Government expenditure
after approval by the Controller and Accountant General.
His Public Relations Office however said that Government revenue for the
first quarter of this year was so low that it was exhausted before March and so
the Ministry if now having to wait for Parliament to approve their estimates
before their budget could be approved, adding that it was their hope that the
problem would be solved.
He explained that the Governor of The Bank of Ghana was so bent on asserting
his independence to curb excessive Government spending.
Dr. Kwabena Duffour has established a reputation of doggedness since he
assumed office, and has a very tight monetary control policy.
The Controller and Accountant General Mr. Tuffuor refused to answer the
question put to him directly in a note slipped to him by The Chronicle through
his secretary last Friday. He asked that this reporter should go and write a
proper letter.
Bank of Ghana official Secretary Mr. Odei said he is not authorised to speak
on issues even though traditionally BoG Secretaries had acted as Spokesmen. He
said he was not going to deny that their cheques have been bouncing. He also
asked for formal letter.
This is not the first time that cheques issued by the State have been
dishonoured by Bank of Ghana.
Last year, cheques hurriedly issued to a horde of angry contractors who had
besieged the Ministry of Finance because of liquidity problems.
Dr. Duffour at the time was quoted to have told the contractors that 'There
is no money here. Go and ask The Minister of Finance whether one send people to
go and collect yams from a yam barn into which no yams have been sent'.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortage Hit Kailahun
Headline: Sierra Leone: Food Shortage Hit Kailahun
Body_Text:
Freetown — The people in the Kailahun District are in desperate need
of food as hundreds are dying of starvation on daily basis. Concord
Times man who just returned from a trip to the district discovered that
most people are now merely walking skeletons.
The food shortage has affected both areas controlled by the
government and the rebels.
The people now forage for bush yams and bananas in a bid to survive.
Relief agencies have not made any appreciable impact in the district;
they have always complained of inaccessibility.

Original_Search_Title: Scorched To Death
Headline: Nigeria: Scorched To Death
Body_Text:
Lagos — Not a few people perish as a petrol tanker loses control and crashes
into the ever-busy Obodoukwu Road Market, Okpoko. Everybody has his palm
clasped to his nose as flies keep hovering around, attracted, of course, by the
foul smell emitting from the spot where the charred remains of the petrol
tanker still lies.
Scattered around the vicinity are what remain of yam tubers, tomatoes,
pepper, cassava and other assorted food items which belonged to the roadside
traders.
And close by, a white coffin lies in wait, with the attendants
intermittently wiping tears from their blood-shot eyes. Evidently, they have
wept to no end.
That was the poignant picture last Thursday, at the ever-busy Obodoukwu
Road, Okpoko, where traders have found a market. Indeed, the market is a
typical case of illegal street-trading. Nobody, however, seems to have ever
raised an eye-brow. So, the people must have taken the situation as normal,
hence nobody anticipated the event of Monday, 20 March. Eyewitness accounts
gave the time as "between 5.30 and 6pm." A petrol tanker whose driver obviously
lost control swept everything on its way as it descended the road. Exactly
opposite the NEPA service station, it rammed into the gutter and lay still.
In no time, sympathisers and rescuers alike were said to have gathered to
salvage those trapped therein. Then, the unexpected happened.
A deafening explosion occurred pushing a great quantity of petrol outwards
in different directions. The accompanying inferno "spread with rocket speed"
according to Rufus Maduekwe, a beans-shop keeper who told TheNEWS he saw it
all. No fewer than 50 persons were said to have been trapped.
Fire-fighters were said to have come from Asaba, the Delta State capital,
but before then, the damage had been done. The ailing landlord in front of
whose house the tanker crashed could not muster enough strength to vacate his
house which had gone up in flames. Consequently, he perished with it. The white
coffin was for the remains of a woman trader who could only be identified by
the colour of her wrapper which was not completely burnt.
Similarly, a six-month-old baby was discovered in the gutter the following
day having been washed there by the torrential rain that fell later that Monday
night. Its mother, as at last week, was still lying under critical condition at
Pieter Hospital, Owerri Road oblivious of what had become of her baby.
Some traders were lucky. Those whose "God did not sleep" on that day as
Onyeka Onyeanwuna put it were taken to Chibike Hospital and Maternity Home, on
Obodoukwu Road, Pieter Hospital, Njidechukwu Hospital and Graceland Hospital,
Okpoko. Onyeanwuna himself was one of those lucky few. The young, barrow-
pusher, told this magazine that he had gone to help rescue those initially
trapped under the tanker when the explosion occurred and entrapped him. But he
miraculously escaped death.
Nevertheless, he said he had already rescued one man, who though had one of
his legs broken, escaped the fatal explosion.
The same fate also befell Friday Ani and Joseph Eze, both of them barrow-
pushers too. Ani regretted that the man he was about rescuing went with the
fire. At Chibike Hospital, a nurse said a pregnant woman amongst others whose
condition were not very critical had been discharged.
As at last Thursday, nothing had been done to evacuate the trailer and those
trapped underneath it. Distended entrails were quite visible suggesting that a
whole lot of corpses were not washed away by the rain of that fateful Monday
night. Maduekwe said it was, in fact, the rain which finally put out the fire,
and "carried away many dead bodies."
Since the tragedy, Obodoukwu has become a Mecca of sorts, albeit an agony-
inducing one, for those whose relations and friends are no more. A NEPA staff
who pleaded anonymity told TheNEWS that a similar incident had occurred in the
same area in 1985, claiming over a hundred lives.
To avert more of such incidents in the future, he suggested that Ogbanu
Local Government Area where Okpoko belongs, should ban road-side trading. Until
the government hearkens to this advice, there may yet be more of such
unpleasant occurrences, since the traders are back to the roadside, displaying
their wares!
Publication date: April 3, 2000

Original_Search_Title: 58 Malian Child-Labourers Return Home
Headline: Mali: 58 Malian Child-Labourers Return Home
Body_Text:
Bamako, Mali (PANA) — Fifty-eight Malian youths illegally recruited from
various parts of the country to work in plantations in neighbouring Cote
d'Ivoire have returned home.
Officials said in Bamako Friday that the children aged between 12 and 17
years, arrived at the border town of Sikasso, some 380 kilometres from Bamako,
the Malian capital, on Thursday.
Nine of the children who returned were sick while one girl was pregnant, the
officials said, adding that they were retrieved from five camps in the district
of Mankono in Cote d'Ivoire, where they worked in cotton, corn and yam
plantations.
An official of the Malian consulate who accompanied the children said they
were removed from the plantations after Ivorian authorities decided to check the
trafficking of Malian children brought to work in the country.
Several sources stated that most of the children worked in plantations and
were forced to take drugs to improve their performance in farm work.
A recent publication of the United Nations Children's Fund whose regional
bureau for West Africa is headquartered in Abidjan, confirmed the existence of
networks for the trafficking of Malian children used as farm hands in Ivorian
plantations.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Battle Ready
Headline: Nigeria: We Are Battle Ready
Body_Text:
Lagos — Joe Igbokwe, an Igbo, a writer and a social critic, tackled
the Igbo question in the Nigerian polity. He spoke with ABIMBOLA
OGUNNAIKE.
Q: What is your view on the recent attack on Igbo in Kaduna and the
reprisal attacks in the East?

Original_Search_Title: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Headline: Ghana: Census Not For Tax Purposes - DCE
Body_Text:
Accra — The BirIm North District Chief Executive, Prince Yaw Danso,
has dispelled the misconception prevailing in the district that census
figures will be used as a basis for the imposition of more property
rates and other taxes.
He said this when he addressed the chiefs and people of Akokoaso at
their annual celebration of Yam Festival at Akokoaso, recently.

Original_Search_Title: The Changing Face Of Ado Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: The Changing Face Of Ado Ekiti
Body_Text:
Lagos — Before Ekiti State was created in October 1996 by the regime
of the late Gen. Sani Abacha, Ado Ekiti which became its capital, was a
rustic town. There was a plethora of old buildings which, given the
aesthetics of the time, were status symbols for cocoa and yam farmers.
These days, state-of-the-art structures, owned by government and high
networth private citizens, are springing up by the day.
The Government House, formerly owned by the local government, has
been given a face lift.

Original_Search_Title: Igboland On Fire
Headline: Nigeria: Igboland On Fire
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sharia killings in Kaduna excites reprisal attacks in the
Igbo towns of Aba and Owerri. Ebelo Goodluck & Ben Eguzozie report
Like a yam seedling divested of its first shoot, Sharia is ramifying
across the land as Nigerians wake up to its daily horrors. In Aba and
Owerri, bodies of slain Igbomen and women arriving from the Kaduna
mayhem stoke a flame of pent up anger. Thirty-two lives were reportedly
lost on the first day of rioting. The figure is bound to rise as the
death toll is ascertained in the coming days.

Original_Search_Title: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Headline: West Africa: IRIN-WA Update of events in West Africa
Body_Text:
Abidjan — CHAD: French grants 17.6 million euro for drainage systems -
Residents of two southern Chadian towns and the capital, Ndjamena, who often
must wade through their rain-flooded neighbourhoods may soon get some relief.
Chad and the French aid agency, Agence francaise de developpement (AFP),
signed a 17.6-million-euro (US $17.48 million) deal on Wednesday to build
drains in the southern towns of Moundou and Sarah, and in Ndjamena.

Original_Search_Title: The African film making experience
Headline: Africa: The African film making experience
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — Gaston Kabore is an award winning film maker from
Burkina Faso. He completed his studies at the Sorbonne University,
France, and has been working in the film industry ever since. He has
worked as technical cinema advisor for the Ministry of Information and
Culture, as well as Director of the National Centre of Cinema.
From 1985-1997 Gaston Kabore served as General Secretary of the Pan-
African Film Directors Federation. He has also been a member of the
judging panel at the Cannel Film Festival.

Original_Search_Title: The sights and sounds of Ho
Headline: Ghana: The sights and sounds of Ho
Body_Text:
Accra — Ho is the capital of the Volta Region. It is blessed with a
countless number of tourist attractions. They are both natural and cultural,
varied and spatially distributed.
MOUNTAIN RANGES/HILLS

Original_Search_Title: 'Because We No Cry...'
Headline: Ghana: 'Because We No Cry...'
Body_Text:
Accra — In these days when injunctions - whether interim or perpetual - fly
around our noses like mango season flies, I am surprised no one has gone for an
injunction order to restrain the callous harmattan weather that is making life
unbearable for us.
Or is it because there are some people who enjoy this harmattan weather? One
such person was Unoka, father of Okonkwo in Achebe's 'Things Fall Apart'. (My
former lecturer in English at Legon has already warned me I've done a critique
of all Achebe's books.)

Original_Search_Title: Gov. Nnamani Visits Umuode Refugees
Headline: Nigeria: Gov. Nnamani Visits Umuode Refugees
Body_Text:
Lagos — For the first time since February last year, the displaced people of
Umuode who have been living in refugee camps in Akpuoga Nike received some
succour from the Enugu State Government.
Governor Chimaroke Nnamani who visited the refugee camps declared
government's interest in resolving the age-long chieftaincy and land dispute
between the Umuode and Ozucku communities.

Original_Search_Title: ADF ambush UPDF convoy
Headline: Uganda: ADF ambush UPDF convoy
Body_Text:
Kampala — 3 rebels killed By Souza Justus In Bundibugyo Rebels of the Allied
Democratic Forces (ADF) Jan. 31 ambushed a contingent of UPDF soldiers at Ngite
village Harugale sub county in Bundibugyo district.
The incident occurred at 7:30 a.m when the UPDF was on a search-and-destroy-
rebels operation.

Original_Search_Title: Wipe Out Hunger in Kenya - Build Good Roads!
Headline: Kenya: Wipe Out Hunger in Kenya - Build Good Roads!
Body_Text:
Nairobi — We have enough food in the country; the only problem is our
inability to move food from surplus areas to areas where there are deficits..."
It was Kenya's Minister of Agriculture, Chris Obure, who said this recently.
So, does Kenya have a famine or is it merely a question of poor distribution of
food? To a hungry person, the difference is the same.
Famine does not occur overnight. The signs are as clear as daylight. The
dark of the night is the famine.

Original_Search_Title: Mind your language (Commentary)
Headline: Africa: Mind your language (Commentary)
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Only when Africans put their vernaculars first will they be able
to fully express their scientific genius, writes ALI MAZRUI in this article on
globalisation, language and gender.
What is development? How does it now relate to globalisation? Since among
fragile societies globalisation is a mixed blessing, we would have to redefine
development: Development equals globalisation minus dependency.

Original_Search_Title: Voting for NDC not panacea for development
Headline: Ghana: Voting for NDC not panacea for development
Body_Text:
Accra — Impression gathered from a tour of three districts of the
Eastern Region is that people think only those who voted for the
National Democratic Congress deserve development projects which they
consider as gifts and projects they are entitled to.
On December 14 last year, at an Abirem durbar of the chiefs,
assembly members and opinion leaders of the Birim North District, the
chief of Ayirebi, Obrempong Gyapere Agyekum II, questioned the
propriety of the government's repairing roads in the Oda Constituency
which voted for the NPP and not the NDC. "We voted for the NDC,  where
the government stands, we stand, so how come that you repair Oda roads
and leave ours out?" the chief who was spokesman for the Association of
Chiefs in the district asked the Minister of Roads and Transport,
Edward Salia, who had been invited to the durbar.
